The page also calls getCurrentOrderSummary on load to populate order-related state, including hasActiveOrder, orderId, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime. These values are used by the order summary widget and by the pickup-order component to detect when a pickup store change should be confirmed. Account state is maintained in memory based on successful calls to loginWithEmailPassword and signupAccount, as well as logoutCurrentAccount. After login or signup, the page uses the returned account or accountDashboard object to update AccountProfile-like state (including favoriteStore); favorite status for the current store is derived either from getStoreDetail.isFavorite or by comparing account.favoriteStore.id to store.id. When setFavoriteStore is called and succeeds, the returned favoriteStore is used to update this account context, and local isFavorite state is updated without requiring another getStoreDetail call. When logoutCurrentAccount succeeds, the account context is cleared; favorite-related UI treats the user as unauthenticated and no longer calls setFavoriteStore until the user logs in again. When setCurrentPickupStore is called and succeeds, the page may call getCurrentOrderSummary again to refresh order state; navigation to menu.html passes the currently selected store via the pickupStoreId URL parameter. Throughout, the page does not persist any state in localStorage; state is kept only in memory for the lifetime of the page and in the URL parameters used for navigation between pages.

components.functionality: On page load, parse id and fromZip URL parameters, validate that id is present, call getStoreDetail with storeId equal to id to load full store details and favorite status, call getCurrentOrderSummary to fetch current order context, store all responses in in-memory state, and propagate the data to child components. It also reacts to account context updates (from login or signup) by recomputing favorite status for the current store.
Render a conditional "Back to search results" link when a fromZip URL parameter is available, and navigate back to stores.html with the zip parameter set to that fromZip value when clicked.

Display key store information from getStoreDetail, including name, address, city, state, zip code, phoneNumber, distanceMiles, hoursSummary, and isOpenNow. It visually reflects whether the store is the current favorite using favorite state provided by the controller and hosts the favorite-store-toggle component.

Allow users to set this store as their favorite when authenticated. It checks whether an account context exists and whether the current store is already the favorite. When clicked by a logged-in user, it calls setFavoriteStore with the current storeId, updates isFavorite state and the account’s favoriteStore from the API response, and shows success or error messages. When clicked by a guest, it triggers an auth-required event to open or focus the authentication panel instead of calling the API.

Display a map preview image for the store using mapImageUrl from getStoreDetail and optionally provide a control for users to open a larger map view (handled via navigation or external link logic, without additional APIs).

Show the store’s hoursSummary, the isOpenNow indicator, and an amenities list based on the amenities array in the getStoreDetail response, presenting functional information like availability of Wi-Fi or drive-through service.

Provide the primary "Order Pickup from this Store" action. It checks store.canMobileOrder to determine whether ordering is available. It also uses current order summary data to detect whether there is an active order with a different pickup store and, in that case, can prompt the user to confirm switching the pickup store. Upon user confirmation (if needed), it calls setCurrentPickupStore with the current storeId. When the API call returns success, it can request a refreshed order summary via getCurrentOrderSummary and then navigates to menu.html with pickupStoreId equal to the store.id.

Display a compact view of the current order based on getCurrentOrderSummary, including hasActiveOrder, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore.name, and pickupDateTime. It updates its display whenever the controller refreshes order summary (for example after setCurrentPickupStore). Clicking the widget navigates to menu.html with pickupStoreId equal to pickupStore.id so that the user can modify the order.

Provide inline login and signup capabilities on the store detail page. For login, it collects email and password and calls loginWithEmailPassword; on success, it updates account context and notifies the controller so favorite status can be recomputed. For signup, it collects fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms and calls signupAccount; on success, it updates account context using accountDashboard and again notifies the controller. It surfaces errors using the notification-banner component and exposes an event that can be triggered by other components (such as favorite-store-toggle) when authentication is required.

Show basic account context such as the signed-in user’s name or email when available and provide a logout control. When the logout control is clicked, it calls logoutCurrentAccount; on success, it clears the in-memory account context, triggers account-context updates in the controller, and resets dependent components such as favorite-store-toggle and account-auth-panel.

Display transient success, warning, or error messages resulting from interface calls such as loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, setFavoriteStore, setCurrentPickupStore, getStoreDetail, and getCurrentOrderSummary. It receives message strings and severity from other components and shows them at the top or bottom of the page without storing any data outside in-memory state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps a strict top-to-bottom, single-column content flow within a centered, narrow main column. At the very top is a thin global header bar with three zones aligned to the page margins: the back-to-results link on the left, a subtle page identity/store context in the center (carried visually by the store detail header just below), and the account menu on the right. Directly beneath the header sits an optional full-width notification banner. The main store detail content is then presented in a vertically stacked sequence inside the central column: store title and address block, favorite toggle, map preview card, store hours and amenities block, the primary pickup order section, the current order summary widget, and finally any authentication panel needed for account actions. There are no sidebars; all interactive elements live in this single vertical stream.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps a strict top-to-bottom, single-column content flow within a centered, narrow main column. At the very top is a thin global header bar with three zones aligned to the page margins: the back-to-results link on the left, a subtle page identity/store context in the center (carried visually by the store detail header just below), and the account menu on the right. Directly beneath the header sits an optional full-width notification banner. The main store detail content is then presented in a vertically stacked sequence inside the central column: store title and address block, favorite toggle, map preview card, store hours and amenities block, the primary pickup order section, the current order summary widget, and finally any authentication panel needed for account actions. There are no sidebars; all interactive elements live in this single vertical stream.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar takes up a shallow band at the top of the viewport (around 10–15% of the height), with the central content band occupying the middle of the page and surrounded by generous top and bottom whitespace. The main content column is centered horizontally, constrained to roughly 420–480px max-width regardless of screen size, preserving the narrow, focused feel of the mockup. Each major section (header, map, info, order controls) occupies nearly the full width of this column with comfortable margins between them. The notification banner spans the full browser width but is visually light and shallow. Lower-priority text elements (helper text, secondary links) use tighter spacing and smaller visual footprint, staying close to their related primary elements without expanding the layout vertically.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and uncluttered. Each section is separated by generous vertical spacing, and within each section elements are grouped into clear, minimal clusters. There are no extra promotional blocks or competing panels; only essential store information, order controls, and account interactions are shown. Inputs, toggles, and buttons have clear padding and breathing room, mirroring the spacious form controls from the mockup. Secondary text (such as notes about orders or login prompts) is more compact but still well spaced, ensuring the layout feels calm and readable rather than crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is a clean vertical path. The user first registers the top header band (back navigation on the left and account menu on the right), then any notification message immediately below. The eye then drops to the bold store name and key details at the top of the central column, then to the favorite toggle positioned near that header. From there, attention moves down to the map preview (a visual anchor), then to practical details like hours and amenities. The main call to action in the pickup order section (typically a strong blue button) becomes the key focal point, with the current order summary following directly beneath as a contextual companion. If the user is logged out or needs authentication, the auth panel appears inline near these controls, discovered naturally as the user scans downward. Overall, the vertical scroll reveals content in a clear, predictable order without lateral distractions.

layout.overall_description: The Store Detail page presents itself as a clean, centered strip of content running down the middle of a white, full-width background. At the very top, a low-profile header bar stretches across the page, aligning a back-to-results link on the left and the account menu on the right, with both edges lining up with the same margins that define the narrow central column below. An optional, slim notification banner sits just beneath this bar, spanning the full width yet staying visually light. The main content appears as a single vertical column roughly 420–480px wide, centered on the page and framed by generous whitespace on all sides. At the top of this column, the store name and primary details are presented as the main heading, with the favorite toggle closely associated to it. Below that, a map preview card fills most of the column width, followed by a concise block describing store hours and amenities. The primary pickup order area comes next, with a large blue action button anchoring the page visually, and the current order summary widget tucked just beneath it to provide immediate context. If authentication is required, a streamlined auth panel appears in the same column near the order controls rather than overlaying the page, resembling the simplicity and spacing of the original login form. The entire composition feels linear, uncluttered, and easy to scan from top to bottom, with each section naturally leading into the next.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component functions as an invisible structural wrapper for the entire Store Detail page. It conceptually spans the full width and height of the viewport, sitting at the root of the layout. All other components (header, notification banner, and central content column) are positioned within its boundaries. It does not introduce any visual chrome or spacing by itself but defines the main centered content column and the vertical stacking order for the page sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-page container with no visible UI; it establishes the centered, fixed-max-width main content column and vertical stacking of child components, but does not scroll or fix independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-results link is placed in the top header bar, aligned to the left edge of the content grid. It sits horizontally in the same row as the account menu on the right, with the text baseline aligned vertically to the center of the header bar. The link occupies a small portion of the width (about 15–20% of the header), leaving ample whitespace between it and the center of the bar. It appears as a simple text link or subtle icon-plus-text, visually light and not competing with the main store header below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the header bar; it scrolls away with the page content if the user scrolls, and remains anchored to the left margin defined by the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The store detail header appears at the very top of the central content column, positioned a comfortable distance below the header bar or notification banner (roughly 48–64px of vertical space). It spans nearly the full width of the column (about 90–100% of the 420–480px max-width) and is left-aligned within that column. The main store name sits as a bold, prominent heading, with secondary information such as address or city stacked directly beneath it in lighter text. There is generous vertical padding above and below this block, establishing it as the primary entry point into the main content. The favorite store toggle is visually grouped with this header, typically aligned to the right side of this same row or in a tight sub-row immediately beneath the title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the central column; it participates in the vertical flow and does not have sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite store toggle is positioned in close proximity to the store detail header, sharing its horizontal band. On typical desktop widths, it sits on the far right edge of the central column, aligned horizontally with either the main store name or the top of the header block. It occupies minimal space (around 10–15% of the column width), appearing as a compact icon button or small control with subtle padding and a light outline or fill when active. Vertically, it aligns so that there is a clear grouping: the left side of the row is the store title and key text; the right side is the favorite toggle, with comfortable horizontal spacing between them. On smaller screens, it can wrap to a row directly beneath the store name but still stays visually attached to the header with only a small vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control anchored to the right side of the store header area; it moves with the header as the user scrolls and does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map preview sits directly beneath the store detail header block, separated by a moderate vertical gap (around 24–32px). It is presented as a rectangular card centered within the main column, spanning almost the full width (approximately 100% of the column width with slight internal padding or rounded corners). The aspect ratio is landscape, roughly 16:9 or slightly shorter, so it becomes a strong yet not overwhelming visual anchor. There is a clear white margin around the card to keep it light and aligned with the minimalist aesthetic, and any map controls or markers sit inside this card area without extending past the central column. Adequate spacing below the map (around 24–32px) prepares the eye to move down to the hours and amenities section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block; it scales in width with the central column but keeps a fixed aspect ratio, maintaining its rectangular shape as the page resizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is placed below the map preview with a clear vertical separation (about 24–32px), still entirely within the central column. It appears as a simple, text-first block or a light card: a small section title (e.g., Hours & Amenities) at the top left, with rows of information stacked vertically underneath (such as open/close times and a short amenities list). The content spans about 90–100% of the column width, keeping left-aligned text and generous line spacing for readability. The section is visually tidy and compact, avoiding dense tables in favor of a few clear rows. A slightly reduced vertical margin below this section compared to the map (e.g., 24px) reinforces that it is informational, not the primary call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section integrated into the vertical flow; it expands vertically as needed based on content but does not change position relative to other main blocks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup order section is placed immediately beneath the hours and amenities block, separated by a noticeable but not excessive gap (around 24–32px). It occupies the full width of the central column and is presented as a focused interaction area with the primary call-to-action button as its anchor. At the top of this section, a short, clear heading or label (e.g., Order Pickup from this Store) is left-aligned, with any brief explanatory text directly below it. Underneath, a large blue button spans most of the column width (around 80–100%), mirroring the primary CTA style from the login mockup. The button has generous vertical padding, rounded corners, and stands out strongly against the white background. Any smaller controls or sub-links (such as switching stores or viewing order options) sit just below the main button, left-aligned and in a lighter visual style. Vertical spacing above and below this section is slightly larger than around informational blocks, emphasizing its importance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky control area; it forms the main interactive focus within the scroll flow and expands as necessary to accommodate short helper text or states, but remains confined to the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current order summary widget appears directly below the pickup order section, separated by a small to medium vertical gap (around 16–24px) so that it reads as a related but secondary area. It is rendered as a compact card within the central column, spanning the same width as the other sections. Inside the card, there is a concise summary: order status, pickup time, and perhaps a few key items, all left-aligned with comfortable line spacing. The widget height is modest so that it does not visually overpower the main order button above; instead, it reads as supportive context. A subtle border or light background tint can delineate it from the rest of the page while keeping the overall design minimal. Extra spacing (around 32px) is placed below this widget before any auth panel or footer-like content, maintaining clear separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static contextual block; it stays in the vertical sequence under the pickup section and grows vertically only as needed for brief summary content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account auth panel is placed within the central column as a self-contained block, typically positioned just below the pickup order section and/or the current order summary widget, depending on the flow and logic (for example: above the order controls if login is required first, or below them as an inline prompt). Visually, it mirrors the login mockup: a simple card-like area with a concise heading (e.g., Sign in to save this store or Sign in to manage your orders), a short explanatory line, and stacked form controls if needed (email and password fields) with labels left-aligned and inputs spanning nearly the full width. A primary blue button in this panel stays narrower or less dominant than the main pickup-order button to avoid competing hierarchy; if present, it spans around 80–100% of the column width but uses slightly smaller size or subtler placement. Secondary links like Forgot password and a small Sign up link can appear beneath, in tighter vertical spacing. The panel uses generous internal padding but minimal external footprint, and extra whitespace above and below (24–32px) separates it from surrounding sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline panel that appears or disappears based on authentication state; it does not overlay the page, and instead participates in the vertical scroll with the rest of the central column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account menu sits in the right segment of the top header bar, horizontally aligned with the back-to-results link on the left. It is flush with the right margin defined by the same grid that constrains the central column below. The control itself is compact – typically an icon and/or the user avatar/name – and occupies about 15–20% of the header width on the right side. There is ample horizontal whitespace between it and the central area of the header bar, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. The menu does not visually dominate the page; its interaction (dropdown, etc.) remains within the header area when opened, not extending into the main content column by default.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored in the top header bar; it scrolls away with the page and does not remain fixed. Any dropdowns are positioned relative to this element within the header layer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner appears as a slim, full-width strip immediately below the top header bar and above the main central column content. It stretches from the left to right edge of the viewport, but its text and any icon are centered within the same horizontal margins as the main content column for consistency. The banner height is modest (roughly equal to or slightly taller than the header bar), with comfortable vertical padding that keeps it readable but not bulky. Background color is typically a soft tint (e.g., a pale blue, green, or yellow) so that it is noticeable without overpowering the rest of the layout. There is a clear gap beneath the banner before the store detail header begins (around 32–40px), ensuring that the central content still feels visually grounded and not crowded by the notification.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky strip; it pushes the main content downward when present and scrolls off the screen with the header as the user moves down the page.

name: Menu / Order Start
title: Menu & Pickup Order
description: Browse the coffee shop menu by category, choose a pickup store, and view a mini summary of the current order, with optional account login and signup.

core_features: Allow users to log in, log out, or sign up from the menu page using email and password, and load account context including favorite store and loyalty information via loginWithEmailPassword, logoutCurrentAccount, and signupAccount.
Confirm or change the pickup store using a store selection overlay that lists available stores (with favorite store highlighted when available) via getPickupStoreOptions and setCurrentPickupStore.
Honor the pickupStoreId URL parameter by preselecting that store as the current pickup location and synchronizing it with the current draft order via setCurrentPickupStore.
Display top-level menu categories for navigation using getMenuCategories and allow switching between categories, honoring the category URL parameter.
Fetch and display active menu products for the currently selected category using getMenuProductsForCategory, showing them in a product grid.
Navigate to the product detail page (product.html) when a product card is clicked, passing the product id and the current category slug in the URL.
Show a mini-summary of the current pickup order (item count, amounts, store, pickup time) using getCurrentOrderSummary and provide navigation to the full order review page order.html.
Keep the selected pickup store and menu category reflected in the URL parameters so browser navigation (back/forward and deep links) restores page state without local storage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: First-time visit to menu.html with no URL parameters
1) User navigates to menu.html.
2) Page parses URL and detects no pickupStoreId or category.
3) Page calls getCurrentOrderSummary.
   - If hasActiveOrder is true, the page treats pickupStore from this response as the current pickup store and updates the URL to include pickupStoreId.
   - If hasActiveOrder is false, no store is yet selected.
4) Page calls getMenuCategories and selects a default category (for example, the first category returned) and updates the URL with category={selectedCategory.slug}.
5) Page calls getMenuProductsForCategory with the selected category slug and renders the product grid.
6) If no active order exists (hasActiveOrder is false), the store selector overlay is opened automatically and calls getPickupStoreOptions to list available stores.
7) User selects a store in the overlay, which triggers setCurrentPickupStore with the chosen storeId.
8) On success, the page updates the URL with pickupStoreId, closes the overlay, and calls getCurrentOrderSummary again so the mini-cart and store indicator reflect the new draft order.

Workflow: Deep link to a specific store and category
1) User opens menu.html?pickupStoreId={storeId}&category={categorySlug} from another page.
2) Page parses pickupStoreId and category from the URL.
3) Page calls setCurrentPickupStore with the pickupStoreId from the URL to ensure the current draft order is tied to that store.
4) On success, page calls getCurrentOrderSummary to display the order mini-summary and confirm the active store.
5) Page calls getMenuCategories and renders the category tabs.
6) If the category URL parameter matches a returned category.slug, that category is selected; otherwise the page falls back to a default category (e.g., first in the list) and updates the URL accordingly.
7) Page calls getMenuProductsForCategory with the effective category slug and renders the product cards.
8) User can interact with products or change category/store; subsequent changes update the URL parameters without a full page reload where possible.

Workflow: Logging in from the menu page and using favorite store
1) User opens menu.html (with or without URL parameters) and sees the account-auth-panel component in the header or sidebar.
2) User submits the login form with email and password.
3) The page calls loginWithEmailPassword with the entered credentials.
4) On success, the page stores the returned account context (including loyaltyTier, loyaltyPointsBalance, and favoriteStore) in in-memory state and updates the UI (showing user name and loyalty summary, hiding signup form, etc.).
5) If there is no active order (getCurrentOrderSummary.hasActiveOrder is false) and the account response includes a favoriteStore whose canMobileOrder is true, the page can either:
   - Automatically call setCurrentPickupStore with favoriteStore.id, or
   - Prompt the user via the store selector overlay with favoriteStore highlighted.
6) After a store is set (via favorite or user choice), the page updates the pickupStoreId URL parameter and refreshes getCurrentOrderSummary.
7) The menu browsing and ordering flow continues using the now-selected store.

Workflow: Changing pickup store during menu browsing
1) User is on menu.html with an active store and category (pickupStoreId and category are present in the URL).
2) User clicks the "Change store" control in the store indicator component.
3) The store-selector-overlay opens and calls getPickupStoreOptions.
4) The overlay lists stores, highlighting the store whose id matches favoriteStoreId from getPickupStoreOptions (if any) and indicating which store is currently active (matching pickupStoreId in the URL or pickupStore.id in getCurrentOrderSummary).
5) User selects a different store and confirms the selection.
6) The page calls setCurrentPickupStore with the new storeId.
7) On success, the page updates the URL parameter pickupStoreId to the new storeId and calls getCurrentOrderSummary to refresh the mini-cart and store indicator.
8) The product categories and products remain visible; only the active store context changes for subsequent order actions.

Workflow: Browsing categories and opening a product detail
1) User is on menu.html with a selected category (category URL parameter is set) and products loaded via getMenuProductsForCategory.
2) User clicks a different category tab.
3) Page updates the category URL parameter to the selected category.slug (preserving any pickupStoreId in the URL).
4) Page calls getMenuProductsForCategory with the new category slug and replaces the product grid with the new category’s products.
5) User clicks on a product card.
6) The page navigates to product.html using a URL like product.html?id={productId}&fromCategory={currentCategorySlug}, using the id of the clicked product and the currently active category slug.
7) The user configures the product and adds it to the order on product.html (using interfaces defined for that page). When the user returns to menu.html (e.g., via browser back), the category URL parameter ensures the same category is re-selected.

Workflow: Viewing the current order from the menu page
1) User is on menu.html with an active order (getCurrentOrderSummary.hasActiveOrder is true).
2) Mini-cart component shows itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore.name, and pickupDateTime from getCurrentOrderSummary.
3) User clicks the "View Order" or mini-cart area.
4) The page navigates to order.html (no additional URL parameters are required, as the server session identifies currentOrderId).
5) User reviews or updates the order there and can later return to menu.html; when returning, getCurrentOrderSummary ensures the mini-cart still reflects the current order state.

Workflow: Logging out from the menu page
1) User is logged in and browsing menu.html.
2) User opens the account menu and clicks "Log out".
3) The page calls logoutCurrentAccount.
4) On success, in-memory account state is cleared and the UI reverts to guest mode (login/signup options become visible again).
5) Existing draft order (if any) may remain active as an anonymous order, depending on server behavior; the page continues to call getCurrentOrderSummary as usual and does not assume any account-specific data until the next login or signup.
interactions: Click on login button in the account-auth-panel: triggers onSubmitLogin handler, which validates input, calls loginWithEmailPassword, and, on success, updates account state, re-renders any account-bound UI (loyalty info, favorite store indication), and may trigger store selection logic based on favoriteStore.

Click on signup button in the account-auth-panel: triggers onSubmitSignup handler, which validates input, calls signupAccount, and, on success, treats the returned accountDashboard as an authenticated state, updating account UI and optionally prompting the user to select or confirm a pickup store.

Click on logout control in the account-auth-panel: triggers onClickLogout handler, which calls logoutCurrentAccount and, on success, clears account context and refreshes UI sections dependent on account information.

Click on the "Change store" or store name in the pickup-store-indicator: triggers onClickOpenStoreSelector handler, which opens the store-selector-overlay and calls getPickupStoreOptions to load available stores.

Select a store in the store-selector-overlay and confirm: triggers onSelectStore handler, which calls setCurrentPickupStore with the chosen storeId, updates the pickupStoreId URL parameter, closes the overlay on success, and refreshes getCurrentOrderSummary to update order context and the pickup-store-indicator.

Click on a menu category tab in the menu-category-tabs component: triggers onSelectCategory handler, which sets the selected category, updates the category URL parameter (preserving pickupStoreId), and calls getMenuProductsForCategory with the chosen category slug to update the product-grid.

Click on a product card in the product-grid component: triggers onClickProductCard handler, which navigates to product.html?id={productId}&fromCategory={currentCategorySlug} using the clicked product’s id and the active category slug.

Click on the mini-cart summary or "View Order" control in the mini-cart-summary component: triggers onClickViewOrder handler, which navigates to order.html so the user can review or finalize their order.

Initial page load or URL change (including browser back/forward): triggers onPageInit or onUrlChange handler in a state controller, which reads pickupStoreId and category from the URL, orchestrates calls to setCurrentPickupStore (if pickupStoreId is present), getCurrentOrderSummary, getMenuCategories, and getMenuProductsForCategory, and ensures all components reflect the derived state.

Manual refresh of the mini-cart (if provided via an explicit refresh action): triggers onClickRefreshMiniCart handler, which calls getCurrentOrderSummary and updates the mini-cart-summary and pickup-store-indicator components.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven primarily by the URL parameters pickupStoreId and category, combined with responses from getCurrentOrderSummary, getMenuCategories, and getMenuProductsForCategory. On initial load and whenever the URL changes (including browser back/forward), a central state controller parses the URL. If pickupStoreId is present, the controller calls setCurrentPickupStore with that value to synchronize the server-side draft order’s pickup store, then calls getCurrentOrderSummary to obtain currentOrderId, hasActiveOrder, and pickupStore data. If pickupStoreId is not present but getCurrentOrderSummary.hasActiveOrder is true, the controller treats getCurrentOrderSummary.pickupStore.id as the effective store and updates the URL to include pickupStoreId so the state can be restored via deep links. If there is no active order and no pickupStoreId in the URL, the controller does not call setCurrentPickupStore and instead triggers the store-selector-overlay, which will invoke setCurrentPickupStore once the user chooses a store; at that time, the URL is updated with pickupStoreId. For categories, the controller calls getMenuCategories, then determines the selected category: if the category URL parameter matches one of the returned category.slug values, that slug is used; otherwise, a default category is derived from the categories response (for example, the first entry), and the URL is updated with category={defaultSlug}. The selected category slug is then passed to getMenuProductsForCategory to populate the product grid. Whenever the user selects a different category through the UI, the category URL parameter is updated (preserving any existing pickupStoreId) and getMenuProductsForCategory is called again with the new slug. Navigation to product.html and order.html is stateless on this page: the product.html URL includes id and fromCategory to preserve context, while order.html relies on the server-side session and currentOrderId from getCurrentOrderSummary. No localStorage or client-side persistent storage is used; all restorable state is reflected in URL parameters and server-managed session state accessed via the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Provides primary navigation links (including a link back to menu.html for ordering) and houses the account-auth-panel trigger and mini-cart-summary indicator so users can access authentication and order overview from any scroll position.

Handles login, signup, and logout flows directly from the menu page, updating the in-memory AccountProfile context and exposing favorite store and loyalty information to other components such as the store-selector-overlay.

Displays the currently selected pickup store based on getCurrentOrderSummary.pickupStore or setCurrentPickupStore response, and offers an entry point to change the store via the store-selector-overlay.

Presents a modal overlay listing available pickup stores via getPickupStoreOptions, highlights the favorite store (if provided), indicates the currently active store, and updates the current draft order’s store via setCurrentPickupStore when a selection is confirmed.

Fetches and displays the list of menu categories using getMenuCategories, manages the active category selection based on the category URL parameter, and triggers loading of category-specific products when the user switches categories.

Shows the list of active products for the currently selected category, using the response from getMenuProductsForCategory, and allows users to click any product card to navigate to its detail page on product.html with the correct URL parameters.

Displays a compact summary of the current order using getCurrentOrderSummary, including itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime, and provides a control to navigate to order.html for full order review.

Central controller that reads and writes URL parameters (pickupStoreId and category), orchestrates calls to setCurrentPickupStore, getCurrentOrderSummary, getMenuCategories, and getMenuProductsForCategory, and notifies other components when store or category state changes so they stay synchronized with the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strong, linear, top-to-bottom flow with a single main content column centered on the page. At the very top is a thin header-navigation bar spanning full width. Directly beneath that sits a full-width pickup-store-indicator bar, aligned to the same left/right content margins as the header. Below, in the central column, come a compact page title/intro (implicit in the auth and menu context), then the optional account-auth-panel, then the menu-category-tabs, followed by the product-grid occupying most of the vertical space. The mini-cart-summary sits near the bottom edge as a horizontal summary bar, still tied to the central column. The store-selector-overlay appears on top of everything else when invoked, dimming the background. The url-state-manager is non-visual and does not affect physical placement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strong, linear, top-to-bottom flow with a single main content column centered on the page. At the very top is a thin header-navigation bar spanning full width. Directly beneath that sits a full-width pickup-store-indicator bar, aligned to the same left/right content margins as the header. Below, in the central column, come a compact page title/intro (implicit in the auth and menu context), then the optional account-auth-panel, then the menu-category-tabs, followed by the product-grid occupying most of the vertical space. The mini-cart-summary sits near the bottom edge as a horizontal summary bar, still tied to the central column. The store-selector-overlay appears on top of everything else when invoked, dimming the background. The url-state-manager is non-visual and does not affect physical placement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header-navigation and pickup-store-indicator together occupy roughly 15–20% of the viewport height. The central content band (auth, tabs, product-grid) takes up the bulk of the vertical space, with generous whitespace above and below to keep the layout light. The central column is constrained to a maximum width of about 960px overall, but key controls (auth fields, store modal content) still use a narrower inner column (~420–480px) to echo the login mockup. Horizontal space outside the central column is left as white space. The mini-cart-summary is slim in height (about the same as the store bar) and uses full width but minimal vertical space so it doesn’t compete with the primary browsing area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, spacious feel: generous vertical spacing between stacked sections (~24–40px gaps between major blocks, ~16–24px between related elements), large margins around the main central column, and minimal on-screen elements at any one time. Each component uses clear, separated blocks with breathing room. Text is concise, and controls are not crowded; the product grid has noticeable padding between cards. Non-essential details are tucked into smaller text or secondary locations (e.g., inline links) so the page feels uncluttered even though it supports browsing, store selection, and ordering.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from top to bottom: first the header-navigation (logo/back left, lightweight actions right), then quickly down to the pickup-store-indicator bar (communicating context for the order). From there, attention moves into the central column: the account-auth-panel (if shown) creates a compact welcome/auth area, then the menu-category-tabs become the main interactive guide. Below the tabs, the product-grid becomes the primary focal region where users spend most of their time. As items are added, the eye is drawn down to the mini-cart-summary bar for confirmation. When users change the pickup store, the store-selector-overlay takes over the visual flow: dimming the background, placing a centered modal with a strong top-to-bottom flow (search, list, confirm). The large blue primary actions (e.g., store confirm, checkout) act as visual anchors within their respective contexts.

layout.overall_description:
The page presents a clean, modern, and minimal interface centered within a wide, white canvas. A slim header bar runs across the very top, with the coffee shop logo and a subtle back control aligned to the left and lightweight navigation or account access on the right, all locked to a shared max-width alignment. Directly beneath, a full-width but low-height store indicator bar anchors the active pickup location; its content is centered in the same column as the header, making the top of the page feel structured but unobtrusive.

Below this, the main content is a single, centered column that stretches down the page with generous white space above and below. At the top of this column sits an optional auth panel, visually reminiscent of a compact login card: simple headline text and one or two succinct actions, aligned left but constrained to a narrow width so it feels focused and approachable. Immediately underneath, horizontal category tabs span most of the central column, acting as a clean filter strip. The core of the page is the product grid—a set of evenly spaced cards arranged in 2–3 columns within the same central width, leaving ample margins to the left and right so the grid never feels cramped. At the bottom of the viewport, a thin mini-cart summary bar ties the experience together, echoing the thinness of the top header and store bar but with slightly stronger emphasis once items are present. When the user chooses a different pickup store, the entire screen subtly darkens and a centered, modestly sized modal appears, mirroring the vertical, stacked composition and blue primary button style of the login mockup. Overall, the layout feels linear and calm, guiding the eye downward through a single column of decisions and actions, with blue accents used sparingly to highlight the most important controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation is a thin, fixed bar pinned to the very top of the viewport, spanning the full width of the page. Its content is constrained to a centered max-width container that aligns with all other main content below (roughly 960px wide on large screens). Inside that container, the layout is divided into three horizontal zones: on the left, the coffee shop logo followed by a subtle 'Back' link or icon; in the center, the page name or a minimal, low-key title label; on the right, small, text-level actions such as a generic account icon or a compact "Sign In" text link that does not compete with the main content. The bar’s height is modest (about 56–64px), with generous padding above and below its contents to maintain a clean and airy look. The background is white with a very subtle bottom divider line to separate it from the content below, mirroring the light, corporate feel and thin top header from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport; remains visible while the user scrolls. Content inside is centered and follows a three-part horizontal layout aligned to the global page margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-auth-panel sits within the main central column, positioned just below the pickup-store-indicator and the initial page heading (if present). It is horizontally centered on the page but constrained to a narrow width (approximately 420–480px) to echo the login mockup’s compact column. The panel appears as a simple, flat, white card with very soft rounded corners and a subtle outline or shadow, padding its content generously on all sides. Inside, elements are stacked vertically: a bold but modestly sized heading (e.g., "Welcome!" or "Sign in for faster checkout") at the top, a short one-line description beneath, and then one or two primary actions (such as a blue 'Sign In' button and a subtle 'Create account' link) aligned left. Vertical spacing inside the card is comfortable: ~16–24px between text and actions, and ~24–32px from the card edges. The panel does not span the full central column width; instead, it floats visually in the center with whitespace on both sides, reinforcing its optional, secondary nature. On mobile, it expands to nearly full width with consistent side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main scroll flow. It does not stick or float; it simply occupies its place near the top of the content column and pushes subsequent elements downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-store-indicator is a slim, full-width horizontal bar directly beneath the header-navigation, visually reading as a lightweight status strip. Its background is very slightly tinted (a faint gray or an extremely pale blue) to differentiate it from the pure white page while remaining unobtrusive. Inside, its contents are aligned to the same centered max-width container as the header and main content. The bar’s height is modest, roughly 48–56px, with vertical padding so that the text and icon feel comfortably spaced. To maintain a clear, left-to-right flow, the store icon and label (e.g., a pin icon and the current store name) are aligned to the left of this container; a subtle "Change" link or chevron is placed immediately to the right of the store name, indicating that tapping or clicking anywhere on this left-aligned region will open the store selector. The rest of the bar remains mostly empty white space, reinforcing the low-density feel. On scroll, this bar sits just under the fixed header, remaining visible until it scrolls off with the rest of the content, unless the store selector is triggered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow but positioned at the very top of the content area, immediately below the fixed header. When the page first loads, it appears almost like an extension of the header. Acts as the trigger for the store-selector-overlay.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The store-selector-overlay appears as a full-screen layer that sits on top of all other content when activated. The backdrop is a semi-transparent dark overlay covering the entire viewport, subtly dimming the page beneath without becoming heavy or dramatic. Centered within this backdrop is a vertically oriented modal panel, constrained to a width similar to the login mockup column (around 420–480px on desktop) and to a comfortable height (no more than 70% of the viewport height). The modal is white with soft rounded corners and generous internal padding. Inside, the layout mimics the mockup’s vertical stack: at the very top, a concise title such as "Find a store" aligned left; beneath it, a short instruction line; then a full-width store search input stretching nearly the full width of the panel. Below the input is a vertically scrollable list of store options, each with generous spacing and clear separation. At the bottom, spanning almost the full width of the modal, is a prominent brand blue primary button (e.g., "Confirm Store") with rounded corners, serving as the main visual anchor. Below the button, a single secondary text link (e.g., "Use current location") may appear with minimal spacing. The modal content is vertically centered in the viewport; on very small screens, it aligns to the top with the content scrollable inside the panel, preserving a simple, narrow column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed as a full-screen overlay above all other components, with the background content non-interactive while it is open. The modal itself is centered (or top-aligned on small screens) and scrollable internally if content exceeds available height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The menu-category-tabs sit within the main central column, directly below the account-auth-panel (or, if the auth panel is hidden, just below the top heading and a small vertical gap). They span horizontally across most of the central column width, aligning left with the column’s content but leaving consistent padding to the right. Each tab is a flat, pill-like or underlined text element with ample horizontal padding and moderate spacing between tabs, arranged in a single row. When there are too many categories to fit, the row becomes horizontally scrollable while still visually anchored to the central column. The active tab is emphasized with brand blue text and either a blue underline or a subtle filled pill background, while inactive tabs remain in neutral grays. Vertical padding around the tab row is comfortable yet compact (approximately 12–16px top and bottom), maintaining the minimal, low-density feel. A very thin divider line may appear below the tabs to separate them from the product-grid that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in its default state, sitting within the scroll flow. Optionally, on larger screens, the tab row can become sticky: once the user scrolls past it, it adheres just below the fixed header (and above the product-grid), ensuring easy category switching while browsing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid occupies the main body of the page beneath the menu-category-tabs, filling the central column from left to right while preserving generous margins to either side. The grid is responsive: on larger screens, it presents two or three product cards per row within the central 960px column, each card having consistent width and equal horizontal gaps; on smaller screens, the layout collapses to a single card per row while maintaining the same spacing pattern. Each card is a simple, flat rectangle with soft rounded corners, containing a product image (if used), name, price, and a primary 'Add' action aligned in a clean vertical stack. Vertical spacing between rows is generous (24–32px), and there is noticeable white space above the first row and below the last row, so the grid never feels cramped. Cards do not stretch edge-to-edge; they sit comfortably in the middle of the page with visible white margins around the whole grid. The overall impression is of a calm, organized menu where the user’s focal area moves row by row down the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main central scroll area. It expands vertically as products load and pushes the mini-cart-summary downward initially, though the mini-cart may later stick to the viewport bottom for better visibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart-summary is a slim horizontal bar that sits at the bottom of the central content area and, once items are in the cart, may adhere to the bottom of the viewport for easy access. Its width is tied to the main content column: on large screens, its content is centered within the same 960px max-width container, while the bar background can optionally stretch full width edge-to-edge to mirror the top header and store indicator. The bar’s height is modest (about 56–64px), with comfortable padding so the text and any iconography do not feel crowded. Inside, content is arranged left-to-right: on the left, a brief text summary (e.g., item count and subtotal) aligned left; on the right, a brand blue 'View Order' or 'Checkout' button that spans about one-third of the container width, providing a clear primary action without overwhelming the grid above. The bar appears to float slightly above the bottom edge when sticky, with subtle shadow or separation to distinguish it from the background. When the cart is empty, the bar can be minimized or hidden, leaving more white space and preserving the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially part of the normal scroll flow, located below the product-grid. Once the user adds items to the cart or scrolls beyond a certain threshold, it can become sticky to the bottom of the viewport so that the summary and action button remain visible while browsing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager does not occupy any visible space on the page. It functions behind the scenes to synchronize the current menu category, selected store, and potentially cart state with the URL, ensuring deep linking and back-button behavior work correctly. There is no visual representation; users interact indirectly through other components (tabs, store selector, cart) while this controller quietly updates the URL and interprets it on load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It does not render any UI and therefore has no layout footprint; it simply orchestrates state changes and routing logic for the visible components.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Configure a single menu item (size, milk, quantity, and pickup time) and add it to the current pickup order, with support for login and reordering from past orders.

core_features: Load product details by product ID from the URL using getProductDetails, including name, description, image, rating, available sizes, and pricing by size.
Allow selection of product configuration: size (e.g., medium, large) and milk or other customizations based on available options from getProductDetails.
Allow selection of quantity for the configured product.
Allow selection of pickup date and load available time slots for that date using getAvailablePickupTimeSlots, then choose one slot as the pickup time.
Add the configured item (productId, size, milkType, quantity, pickupDateTime) to the current pickup order via addProductToCurrentOrder and update a compact order summary.
When accessed with an orderItemId URL parameter, prefill size, milk type, and quantity using getOrderItemConfiguration to support reordering from past orders.
Display a mini current-order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary, and give access to navigate to the full order page with the newly added item highlighted.

Support account login, signup, and logout from this page using loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, and logoutCurrentAccount, updating account context and allowing the user to manage their pickup order under their account.
Provide navigation back to the menu page using the fromCategory URL parameter, and navigation to the current order page with a highlightItemId URL parameter when requested.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open product from menu and add to order
1. User navigates from menu.html to product.html with an id URL parameter (and optionally fromCategory).
2. Page-init logic reads id (productId) and calls getProductDetails(productId).
3. Once product details load, the configuration form displays default selections (defaultSize from the product, default milk option if defined, and a default quantity of 1).
4. The page obtains the current order summary via getCurrentOrderSummary to show existing item count and totals.
5. The user selects a pickup date (default can be the earliest eligible date determined by the host app) and the page calls getAvailablePickupTimeSlots(date).
6. The user selects an available time slot returned by getAvailablePickupTimeSlots, which determines the pickupDateTime that will be passed to addProductToCurrentOrder.
7. The user optionally changes size, milk option, and quantity.
8. The user submits the add-to-order form. The page calls addProductToCurrentOrder with productId from the URL, the chosen size, milkType, quantity, and pickupDateTime derived from the selected slot.
9. On success, the page updates the order summary using the orderSummary returned from addProductToCurrentOrder and stores the returned orderItem.id for potential navigation.
10. The user can click a "View Order" / "Go to Cart" action that navigates to order.html with highlightItemId set to the returned orderItem.id.

Workflow: Reorder a past item from order history
1. User navigates from account_orders.html to product.html with both id (productId) and orderItemId in the URL.
2. Page-init logic reads id and orderItemId. It calls getProductDetails(id) to load product data.
3. In parallel or subsequently, it calls getOrderItemConfiguration(orderItemId).
4. When getOrderItemConfiguration returns, the configuration form is prefilled with size, milkType, and quantity from the past order item, as long as these options are still valid for the current product (e.g., size is in product.availableSizes and milkType is in availableMilkOptions). If any stored option is no longer valid, that specific field falls back to a valid default (e.g., product.defaultSize) while the rest of the configuration remains prefilled.
5. The user can adjust any of the prefilled options (size, milk, quantity) as desired.
6. The user selects a pickup date and the page calls getAvailablePickupTimeSlots for that date.
7. If the host app has a target time (for example, closest to a specific time in a given window), it can set an initial pickup time preference and the page selects the available slot closest to that time from the returned list.
8. The user submits the add-to-order form, which calls addProductToCurrentOrder with the updated configuration and chosen pickupDateTime.
9. On success, the page updates the order summary and allows navigation to order.html with highlightItemId set to the new order item ID.

Workflow: Adjust pickup time selection
1. User views the product configuration form and sees the currently selected pickup date and available time slots for that date (loaded via getAvailablePickupTimeSlots).
2. If the user changes the pickup date, the page calls getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with the newly selected date.
3. When the slots load, the page updates the slot list to reflect the response. If a previously selected slot is no longer available, the selection is cleared and the user must pick a new slot.
4. If no slots are available for the selected date, the page displays a message indicating that pickup is unavailable on that date and disables the add-to-order submission until the user selects a different date with at least one available slot.
5. Once a valid date and slot are selected, the add-to-order form becomes fully enabled and the selected slot determines the pickupDateTime passed to addProductToCurrentOrder.

Workflow: Login or signup from product page
1. The user opens product.html (with required id) while not authenticated, and the page still allows viewing product details and configuring options.
2. If the host app requires authentication before adding items to an order, the user clicks a "Log in" or "Sign up" control in the account panel.
3. For login: the user submits a form with email and password; the page calls loginWithEmailPassword. If success is true, the page updates the account context (e.g., displaying the user's name and loyalty summary) and may refresh the current order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary so that subsequent addProductToCurrentOrder calls are associated with the authenticated account.
4. For signup: the user submits a form with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms; the page calls signupAccount. If success is true, the page updates the account context using the returned accountDashboard and may refresh the current order summary via getCurrentOrderSummary.
5. After successful login or signup, the user can proceed to select pickup date/time and submit the add-to-order form as usual.
6. If the user chooses to log out from this page, they can click a logout control, which calls logoutCurrentAccount. On success, the account context is cleared in the UI, and the host app can decide whether an existing draft order remains visible (based on server-side behavior) while the page may optionally refresh the current order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary.

Workflow: Navigate between product, menu, and order pages
1. On product.html, the page-init logic reads the fromCategory URL parameter, if present.
2. The Back to Menu link is configured to navigate to menu.html, including a category URL parameter set to fromCategory so the user returns to the same category view they came from.
3. After successfully adding an item to the order via addProductToCurrentOrder, the page retains the returned orderItem.id.
4. When the user clicks a View Order or Go to Cart action, the page navigates to order.html with highlightItemId in the URL set to the retained orderItem.id.
5. The product page itself can be re-accessed via direct links (e.g., menu cards, order history entries) with the appropriate id and optional fromCategory and orderItemId URL parameters driving its initial state.
interactions:
Page load and initialization:
- On initial load, the page reads URL parameters id (required), fromCategory (optional), and orderItemId (optional).
- If id is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state and can show a non-configurable error message plus a Back to Menu navigation control (without assuming a category), without attempting to call getProductDetails.
- If id is present, the page calls getProductDetails(productId=id) and, in parallel, getCurrentOrderSummary to populate product information and the order summary widget.
- If orderItemId is present, the page calls getOrderItemConfiguration(orderItemId) after or alongside getProductDetails and uses its response to prefill the configuration form once both product details and configuration data are available.

Product configuration interactions:
- Size selector: when the user selects a size from the available options (limited to product.availableSizes), the component updates the selected size state and recomputes any displayed unit or total price using pricingBySize and quantity.
- Milk / customization selector: when the user selects a milkType from availableMilkOptions, the component updates the selected milk type state; if the user clears the selection, the value may be set to a neutral option such as undefined or a model-defined "no_milk" value, depending on the product’s capabilities.
- Quantity selector: when the user increments or decrements quantity, the component ensures the value stays within allowed bounds (for example, positive integers; exact bounds are controlled by the host app) and recomputes the displayed total for the line item (unitPrice × quantity).

Pickup date and time interactions:
- Date selection: when the user selects or changes the pickup date, the date selector triggers a call to getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with the selected date (in the required format). While waiting, the time slot list shows a loading state.
- Time slot selection: once time slots have loaded, the user can click a slot where isAvailable is true. The selection is stored as the active slot and used to construct pickupDateTime (combining the selected date and the chosen slot’s time, typically using the slot’s startTime).
- Handling unavailable days: if getAvailablePickupTimeSlots returns an empty list or only slots with isAvailable set to false, the time slot component enters a no-availability state and the add-to-order action is disabled until a different date is chosen.

Add to order interactions:
- Add-to-order submission: when the user submits the configuration form, the page validates that a size is selected, quantity is valid, and a pickupDateTime can be derived from the selected date and time slot.
- If validation passes, the page calls addProductToCurrentOrder with productId from the URL, selected size, selected milkType (or an allowed empty/neutral value), quantity, and the computed pickupDateTime.
- While awaiting the response, the add-to-order button is disabled and may show a loading state.
- On success, the component updates the order summary widget with the returned orderSummary and stores orderItem.id in in-memory state for subsequent navigation.
- If the API returns a non-success status, the page displays a non-static error message derived from the response.message and keeps the user’s configuration selections intact so they can adjust and resubmit.

Account authentication interactions:
- Login: user opens a login form in an account panel, enters email and password, and submits. The page calls loginWithEmailPassword. If success is true, the account information (fullName, email, loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, favoriteStore) is bound to the account header and optionally used to contextualize pickup store information.
- Signup: user opens a signup form, enters fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms. The page calls signupAccount. If success is true, the returned accountDashboard is bound to the account context and the user appears logged in.
- Validation errors: if login or signup fails, the account panel shows feedback using the returned message, without hardcoding specific error text, and keeps the entered values as appropriate for correction.
- Logout: user clicks a logout control; the page calls logoutCurrentAccount. If success is true, the account context in the UI is cleared or updated to a logged-out state, and the host app may choose to refresh order summary via getCurrentOrderSummary to reflect any server-side behavior (e.g., clearing or retaining the current draft order).

Navigation interactions:
- Back to menu navigation: clicking the back link navigates to menu.html. If fromCategory is present in the current URL, the link includes category=fromCategory as a URL parameter so that the menu can restore the relevant category view.
- View order navigation: clicking a "View Order" / "Go to Cart" button after at least one successful addProductToCurrentOrder call navigates to order.html with highlightItemId set to the last successfully returned orderItem.id from addProductToCurrentOrder. If no item has been added in the current page session, this navigation can omit highlightItemId or use other host-app logic.

Order summary interactions:
- After any successful addProductToCurrentOrder call, the order summary widget updates itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, and pickupDateTime using the values returned in orderSummary.
- On manual refresh (if provided), the widget can call getCurrentOrderSummary again to sync with any changes made elsewhere (e.g., from the order page in another tab).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters and in-memory UI state, without using localStorage, to manage behavior and navigation. The id URL parameter is required and identifies the Product to load via getProductDetails; the page assumes product.html is accessed as product.html?id={productId}[&fromCategory={slug}][&orderItemId={orderItemId}]. If id is missing or invalid, the page does not call getProductDetails and instead renders an error plus a navigation option back to menu.html. The fromCategory URL parameter is optional and is only used to construct the Back to Menu navigation target (menu.html?category={fromCategory}). It does not affect which Product is loaded. The orderItemId URL parameter is optional and is used exclusively for pre-filling configuration when reordering: when present, the page calls getOrderItemConfiguration(orderItemId) and merges its returned size, milkType, and quantity into the configuration form, but only if they remain valid according to the current product details loaded from getProductDetails. If orderItemId refers to a different product than id or has values incompatible with the current product, the page safely falls back to the product’s defaults for the invalid fields. Authentication state is not stored in URL parameters by this page; instead, login and signup responses are held in memory and any global session handling is assumed to be managed by the host environment. The current draft order identity is not directly encoded in the product.html URL; instead, getCurrentOrderSummary and addProductToCurrentOrder operate against the server-side notion of the current order. For outgoing navigation, the page constructs URLs with appropriate parameters: navigating to menu.html may include category={fromCategory}, and navigating to order.html includes highlightItemId={orderItem.id} from the most recent successful addProductToCurrentOrder response. The page does not rewrite its own URL to encode configuration choices (size, milk, quantity, date, time slot); these are managed in in-memory component state that resets when the page is reloaded or the URL changes.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (id, fromCategory, orderItemId) on load, orchestrates initial API calls (getProductDetails, getOrderItemConfiguration if present, getCurrentOrderSummary), and initializes shared state for the product configuration, pickup time, and order summary.

Displays current account context (if available) such as user name and favorite store information, and provides controls to open login/signup forms or perform logout. It updates when loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, or logoutCurrentAccount are called.

Provides inline login and signup forms that call loginWithEmailPassword or signupAccount. On success, propagates account context to the account-header-menu and can trigger a refresh of getCurrentOrderSummary.

Displays core product information using data from getProductDetails: name, description, image, rating, ratingCount, and price for the currently selected size. Reflects changes when the selected size changes.

Holds the user’s selections for size, milkType, quantity, and the currently chosen pickupDateTime. It merges defaults from getProductDetails with any overrides from getOrderItemConfiguration when reordering, ensuring only valid options are selectable.
Presents available sizes derived from product.availableSizes and allows the user to choose one, defaulting to product.defaultSize or a prefilled size from getOrderItemConfiguration if valid.

Displays milk or customization options based on getProductDetails.availableMilkOptions and allows selection of one option (or a neutral choice if allowed), prefilled from getOrderItemConfiguration.milkType when valid.

Allows the user to adjust the quantity of the product. It initializes from getOrderItemConfiguration.quantity when reordering, or a default value determined by the host app when not reordering, and updates computed totals in coordination with pricingBySize.

Lets the user select a pickup date. When the date changes, it triggers getAvailablePickupTimeSlots for that date and informs the pickup-time-slot-list to update. It also clears or maintains the previously selected time slot depending on availability.

Displays the list of time slots returned by getAvailablePickupTimeSlots for the selected date, indicates which ones are available, and allows the user to select one. The selected slot is used, together with the selected date, to compute pickupDateTime for addProductToCurrentOrder.

Aggregates the selected product configuration (size, milkType, quantity) and the computed pickupDateTime, validates them, and on submission calls addProductToCurrentOrder. On success, it updates the order summary widget and stores the returned orderItem.id for navigation.

Shows a compact summary of the current pickup order including itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, and pickupStore and pickupDateTime where available. It initializes from getCurrentOrderSummary and updates in response to successful addProductToCurrentOrder calls.
Generates a Back to Menu link that navigates to menu.html, including a category URL parameter when fromCategory exists in the current product.html URL.

Provides a View Order / Go to Cart button that, when clicked, navigates to order.html with highlightItemId set to the orderItem.id returned from the most recent successful addProductToCurrentOrder call.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a full-width page with a thin, horizontal header bar at the very top, then a single, centered narrow column (approx. 420–480px max-width) that holds all primary content stacked vertically. Within this column, follow a strict top-to-bottom order: product title and basic info, optional auth prompt, product configuration form fields (size, milk, quantity, pickup date/time), then a full-width primary CTA to add the item to the order, followed by lower-priority navigation links and finally the order summary. No sidebars or multi-column layouts; any groups (like time slots) are visually arranged within the confines of the single central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a full-width page with a thin, horizontal header bar at the very top, then a single, centered narrow column (approx. 420–480px max-width) that holds all primary content stacked vertically. Within this column, follow a strict top-to-bottom order: product title and basic info, optional auth prompt, product configuration form fields (size, milk, quantity, pickup date/time), then a full-width primary CTA to add the item to the order, followed by lower-priority navigation links and finally the order summary. No sidebars or multi-column layouts; any groups (like time slots) are visually arranged within the confines of the single central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate ~10–15% of the viewport height to the header bar. The central column occupies the middle band of the viewport with generous top and bottom padding, leaving visible white space above and below to keep the layout airy. Inside the column, form inputs and the primary button span nearly the full column width with consistent internal padding. Secondary navigation links and the order summary occupy less vertical space, with tighter spacing to indicate lower priority while still maintaining comfortable readability.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, spacious feel. Use larger vertical gaps between major sections (header vs product header, product header vs configuration form, configuration vs CTA). Keep consistent but slightly smaller gaps between related controls inside the form (e.g., between size and milk selectors). Show only essential options and actions required to configure and add the drink; avoid any additional marketing or decorative content. The result is a clean, uncluttered screen where each decision (size, milk, time) is made one at a time in a clear sequence.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from left-aligned branding and account navigation in the top bar down to the centered product title, then sequentially through configuration fields to the primary call to action. The main visual anchors are the product title and the large blue 'Add to Order' button. After the button, the eye should naturally fall to secondary text links (Back to menu, View order) and finally to a compact order summary at the bottom of the column. All elements are aligned to the same vertical column, reinforcing a smooth scroll-based reading flow from top to bottom.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, minimal product configuration experience in a narrow, centered column on a white background. A thin full-width header bar spans the top, aligning its content to the same left and right margins as the central column, giving a strong frame to the page. Beneath this, ample white space leads into the core content: the product name and essentials appear first, clearly left-aligned within the central column. Immediately under this title area, a subtle auth panel offers sign-in benefits without overpowering the main task. The product configuration form follows as a vertical stack of clearly labeled controls: size, milk, quantity, pickup date, and selectable time slots, all nearly full-width and evenly spaced. Below these, a bold blue 'Add to Order' button stretches across the column, visually anchoring the experience as the primary action. Just under the button, small, understated text links let the user go back to the menu or jump to the order view, separated by light spacing and a thin divider, echoing the login pattern. At the bottom of the column, a compact order summary reinforces what’s in the order without competing with the configuration area. The entire layout feels airy, controlled, and linear, with the user’s attention guided smoothly from top header, through configuration, to confirmation and navigation options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual controller component. It does not occupy any visible space in the layout and has no direct on-screen representation. It runs before or alongside rendering to initialize state for the product detail and account context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; does not participate in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account header menu sits inside a slim horizontal bar fixed to the very top of the page, spanning the full browser width. Within this bar, content is constrained to the same max-width central alignment as the main column, using a three-part layout: on the far left, the coffee shop logo and a compact 'Back' text link; centered, a neutral page-level control such as 'Menu' or a subtle static label; on the far right, account-related items (user icon, sign in/out link). The bar is approximately 56–64px tall (roughly 10–15% of viewport height on common laptop screens), with the items vertically centered and generous horizontal padding from the page edges. The background is white with a subtle bottom divider line to visually separate it from the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document; scrolls away with the page (not sticky) while consistently defining the top margin alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth panel appears near the top of the main content column, immediately below the product-detail-header and above the product-configuration-form.
