It spans nearly the full width of the central column (about 90–100% of the 420–480px width), with a subtle light-gray or very light blue background and soft rounded corners echoing the design DNA. The panel height is relatively small, roughly the height of two short lines of text plus padding. Inside, left-aligned text briefly explains the benefit of signing in (e.g., 'Sign in to reorder faster or use saved preferences'), with a small blue text link or compact ghost button on the right side or below. Vertical spacing above and below the panel is moderate—less than the spacing before major sections but more than between individual form fields—clearly marking it as secondary to the product details yet still noticeable in the linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column, participating in normal top-to-bottom flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product detail header is the first element in the central column below the top header bar and its top padding. It is centered horizontally on the page but its internal content is left-aligned. It occupies the full width of the narrow column (420–480px), with generous white space above and below. The top line is the product name in a bold, larger font, acting as a primary visual anchor. Below it, in smaller regular text, sits a concise description or key details like roast style or flavor notes, spanning no more than 2–3 lines. If present, the base price may appear on the right side of this block, aligned with the right edge of the column. This header block occupies roughly 15–20% of the vertical space within the main content area, clearly separating the identity of the product from the configuration form that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, defining the top of the main content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product configuration form begins immediately below the auth panel (or directly below the product-detail-header if the auth panel is hidden). It is a vertically stacked group that fills the full width of the narrow central column. Each child control (size-selector, milk-selector, quantity-selector, pickup-date-selector, pickup-time-slot-list) is presented as its own block, with a clear left-aligned label followed by the control itself. Vertical spacing between each block is consistent and comfortable—enough to clearly separate each choice without creating a feeling of fragmentation. The overall form occupies the central vertical band of the page and is tall enough to require the user to scroll only slightly, if at all, on a laptop screen. There are no multi-column arrangements; any groupings happen visually (e.g., quantity beside its label) but remain within a single column width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block occupying the center of the page, flowing naturally between the header and the add-to-order form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The size selector is the first field in the product-configuration-form. It occupies nearly the full width of the central column, with a left-aligned label ('Size') at the top and the control directly beneath it. The control may appear as a segmented horizontal list of 2–3 pill-style options (e.g., Small, Medium, Large) that stretch across the width, but still stay within the column constraints, with each option taking up an equal fraction of the row. The height of each pill is modest but tappable, with subtle rounded corners and a blue outline or fill for the selected option. There is comfortable vertical padding above the label and between the selector and the next field, matching the spacing rhythm from the mockup’s form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form, anchored as the first decision in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The milk selector is placed directly below the size selector, separated by a consistent vertical gap. It occupies the same width as the size selector. A left-aligned 'Milk' label appears on top, followed by the control—a dropdown, pill row, or vertical radio group—filling the width of the column. If pill-style options are used, they form either a single or double row within the same column width, wrapping to the next line if necessary but never forming a separate column. The visual styling mirrors the size selector to reinforce consistency. Overall height is similar or slightly taller than the size selector, depending on the number of options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form, following the top-down flow under the size selector.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity selector appears under the milk selector, with the same vertical spacing as between other fields. It occupies a full-width row but the control itself is more compact within that space to keep emphasis on other choices. A left-aligned 'Quantity' label is above; below it, a small horizontal control (e.g., minus button, numeric value, plus button) is centered or left-aligned within the row, but still inside the column. The numeric value is clearly readable, while the minus/plus controls are simple, flat buttons with soft rounded corners, using the brand blue for active states. The overall row height is modest, making this field feel quicker and lighter than the size and milk selectors.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; stays in line with other fields in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup date selector sits below the quantity selector with the same consistent vertical spacing. It uses the full column width, with a label such as 'Pickup Date' aligned to the left. Beneath the label is a single-line input or button-style field that spans nearly the full width of the column, with an inline calendar icon aligned to the right inside the field. The field has a thin, neutral border, light rounded corners, and comfortable interior padding, matching the styling of text inputs from the login mockup. The height aligns visually with other form fields, maintaining the clear, orderly rhythm of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; precedes the time-slot list in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup time slot list appears directly below the pickup date selector, with a moderate gap to indicate its close relationship. A left-aligned label (e.g., 'Pickup Time') is followed by a set of selectable time chips within the same column width. The chips are arranged in a simple grid-like pattern but constrained to the single column, wrapping to additional rows as needed. Each chip is a pill-shaped button with subtle rounded corners, a light border, and a blue fill or border when selected. The list’s height grows or shrinks based on available time slots but is visually grouped as a single block with clear top and bottom spacing from adjacent fields. No horizontal scrolling is used; the list grows vertically to preserve the straight downwards reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; expands vertically as needed but remains contained within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-order form directly follows the product-configuration-form as a distinct block. It consists primarily of a single, prominent blue 'Add to Order' button that spans almost the entire width of the central column (leaving a small margin on left and right), mirroring the full-width primary CTA from the login mockup. The button is tall enough to feel substantial (roughly 48–56px high), with a solid blue background, white text, and minimal rounded corners. There is generous vertical spacing above the button to separate it from the last form field and slightly smaller spacing below to connect it visually to the lower-priority links that follow. If any price confirmation text is present (e.g., 'Total: $4.75'), it appears immediately above the CTA button in smaller text, left-aligned or right-aligned within the same column, without competing with the button’s prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; acts as the visual anchor at the end of the configuration flow within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary widget is placed near the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the navigation links and any thin divider, similar in hierarchy to the 'Don’t have an account?' line in the login mockup but with slightly more height. It spans the full width of the central column and is visually compact: a light, bordered box or minimal text area summarizing the current order ('2 items • Pickup at 9:30 AM'), with line breaks kept to a minimum. The text is small to medium size and left-aligned, with optional bolding for key numbers. Compared to the primary configuration area, the widget has reduced vertical padding and a smaller footprint, signaling that it is informative but not the main task focus. There is generous white space below it before the bottom of the page, reinforcing its tertiary status.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the column; remains at the lower end of the content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Back to Menu' navigation link appears immediately below the 'Add to Order' button, aligned to the left edge of the central column. It is a single line of blue text styled as a secondary action, using a smaller font size than the button label. The vertical gap between the button and this link is modest, mirroring the spacing between the primary CTA and 'Forgot Password?' link in the mockup, which subtly suggests it as the next obvious option after choosing not to press the button. The link occupies minimal height and does not have any enclosing box; it sits directly in the white space with plenty of padding above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; inline within the flow under the add-to-order form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'View Order' navigation link is positioned below the 'Back to Menu' link, separated by a thin horizontal rule that spans a portion of the central column width (e.g., 60–80%, centered or left-aligned) with generous white space above and below the rule. Beneath the rule, a short line of smaller text (e.g., 'Ready to check out? View your order') appears, with 'View your order' styled as an inline blue link. This closely echoes the 'Don’t have an account? Sign Up' pattern from the mockup. This block is compact, occupying only a small vertical slice of the column, and visually reads as a gentle, low-priority prompt after the back navigation and primary CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; sits between the main actions and the order-summary widget as a subtle tertiary navigation option.

name: Current Order / Cart
title: Current Order
description: Page for reviewing the current pickup order, editing items, selecting pickup time, choosing a saved payment card, and placing the order.

core_features: Load current order details (order, items, pickup store, assigned payment card) using getCurrentOrderDetails when the page initializes or when URL parameters change.
Display a list of all OrderItem entries in the current order, including product configuration such as size, milk type, quantity, and line totals.
Allow inline editing of item quantity via updateOrderItemQuantity and immediately refresh the affected item and order totals.
Allow inline editing of item options (size and milk type) via updateOrderItemOptions and immediately refresh the affected item and order totals.
Allow removal of items from the order via removeOrderItem and update totals and item count based on the API response.
Show order-level monetary summary (item count, subtotal, tax if provided, total) based on the Order data and/or getCurrentOrderSummary.
Display the selected pickup Store for the order and indicate that store-level details (such as name and basic info) come from the Store entity returned by getCurrentOrderDetails.
Enable selection of a pickup time by fetching available pickup time slots using getAvailablePickupTimeSlots for a selected date and saving the chosen time via setCurrentOrderPickupTime.
Display currently assigned PaymentCard from getCurrentOrderDetails (if any) and allow users to select a different saved card using getPaymentMethodsSummary and setCurrentOrderPaymentCard.

Handle account authentication within the page header or a modal using loginWithEmailPassword for sign-in, signupAccount for registration, and logoutCurrentAccount for sign-out, gating order placement on a logged-in state.
Allow navigation to menu.html for adding more items to the order from a dedicated action control.
Allow navigation to account_payments.html?returnTo=order.html from the payment method section to manage saved payment cards and then return to this page.
Provide a link to rewards.html so users can view available rewards that may influence their ordering decisions.
Submit the completed order using placeCurrentOrder, handling success and failure states based on the response and disabling or preventing duplicate submissions while the request is in progress.
user_workflows:
Review and edit order items:
1) User navigates to order.html (optionally with orderId and highlightItemId URL parameters).
2) The page calls getCurrentOrderDetails to load the Order, associated OrderItem list, Store, and PaymentCard.
3) The order items list displays each item with size, milk type (if any), quantity, and prices from the OrderItem data.
4) To change quantity, the user adjusts a control on an item row; the component calls updateOrderItemQuantity with the item ID and new quantity.
5) On success, the item row updates using the returned OrderItem and the summary area updates using the returned orderTotals.
6) To change size or milk type, the user modifies options on the item; the component calls updateOrderItemOptions with the item ID and new configuration.
7) On success, the item row and summary update using the response payload.
8) To remove an item, the user clicks a remove action; the component calls removeOrderItem with the item ID and then updates totals and item count using the returned orderTotals.
9) If highlightItemId is present in the URL, the list scrolls to and programmatically focuses or visually emphasizes that item after the initial data load.

Select pickup time for the order:
1) After the order is loaded via getCurrentOrderDetails, the page checks whether order.pickupDateTime exists.
2) The pickup time selector shows either the existing pickupDateTime or a prompt to choose a time.
3) The user selects a calendar date within the UI; when the date changes, the component calls getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with the chosen date.
4) The component displays the returned time slots, filtering or disabling those where isAvailable is false.
5) The user chooses an available slot; the component converts the selected slot into a full pickupDateTime value appropriate for the current date and calls setCurrentOrderPickupTime.
6) On success, the component updates the displayed pickup time and the order summary reflects the updated pickupDateTime (via local state or an optional refresh via getCurrentOrderSummary).

Choose or change payment card:
1) When the order loads via getCurrentOrderDetails, the page shows the currently assigned paymentCard (if present) based on the PaymentCard entity in the response.
2) When the user opens the payment method selector, the component calls getPaymentMethodsSummary.
3) The component lists all returned cards and marks the card whose ID matches defaultPaymentCardId and/or the order.paymentCardId as selected.
4) When the user chooses a different card, the component calls setCurrentOrderPaymentCard with the selected paymentCardId.
5) On success, the payment section updates to show the newly assigned PaymentCard for this order.
6) If the user wants to add or edit cards, they click a manage-cards action that navigates to account_payments.html?returnTo=order.html (preserving the existing query string when building the URL if the implementation supports it).
7) After managing cards and returning to order.html, the page re-calls getCurrentOrderDetails and/or getPaymentMethodsSummary so the new or updated cards and any newly assigned default are reflected.

Authenticate and place the order:
1) When the page loads, the shell or a session-aware component determines whether the user is logged in; if not logged in, the primary order placement button is disabled or opens a login/register modal instead of placing the order directly.
2) For login, the user submits email and password through a form that calls loginWithEmailPassword.
3) If the login call returns success and isLoggedIn is true, the account state (including loyalty info and favoriteStore if present) is stored in application state and the order actions refresh (if needed) to enable placement.
4) For registration, the user opens a sign-up flow that calls signupAccount with the required fields; on success, accountDashboard data is propagated to the shared account state.
5) The user ensures that items, pickup time, and payment card are set; the place-order button becomes enabled once the UI validates that the order has at least one item, a pickupDateTime, an assigned paymentCard, and the user is logged in.
6) The user clicks the place-order button; the component calls placeCurrentOrder.
7) While awaiting the response, the button enters a loading state and is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.
8) On success, the UI can navigate to an order confirmation view (not defined in this spec) or show a confirmation state based on the returned Order entity and message; on failure, an error message is displayed and any missing configuration (e.g., payment or pickup time) is highlighted.

Add more items or view rewards from the cart:
1) From the order page, the user can choose to add more items by clicking an add-more-items action.
2) This triggers navigation to menu.html (optionally preserving the current order-related query string if the implementation supports it) so the user can continue adding items.
3) To see rewards, the user clicks a rewards link in the order page.
4) The page navigates to rewards.html, where the user may select a reward that eventually contributes items to the current order; when the user returns to order.html, getCurrentOrderDetails and getCurrentOrderSummary are called again to reflect the updated order contents.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: onPageLoad.
- Actions: Read orderId and highlightItemId from the URL; call getCurrentOrderDetails to populate Order, OrderItem list, Store, and PaymentCard. Optionally call getCurrentOrderSummary to populate header or summary quick view. After items load, if highlightItemId is present, scroll to and emphasize the specified item row.

Order item quantity change:
- Event: onQuantityChange(orderItemId, newQuantity) from an item row control.
- Actions: Call updateOrderItemQuantity with orderItemId and newQuantity; on success, update the corresponding item in the local items collection with the returned orderItem data; update summary values (subtotalAmount, totalAmount) using orderTotals from the response; handle any errors by restoring the previous quantity and displaying an error state.

Order item option change (size, milk type):
- Event: onItemOptionsChange(orderItemId, newSize, newMilkType) when the user picks new options.
- Actions: Call updateOrderItemOptions with the chosen values; on success, update the item row using returned orderItem; update order summary using orderTotals; if the request fails, revert the UI to the previous options.

Order item removal:
- Event: onClickRemoveItem(orderItemId).
- Actions: Optionally confirm removal within the UI; call removeOrderItem; on success, remove the item from the displayed list and update item count, subtotalAmount, and totalAmount using returned orderTotals; if itemCount becomes zero, show an empty-order state, disable the place-order button, and encourage the user to navigate to menu.html to add items.

Pickup date selection:
- Event: onPickupDateChange(dateValue) from a date picker control.
- Actions: Call getAvailablePickupTimeSlots with the selected date; populate the available slots list; disable or clearly mark slots where isAvailable is false.

Pickup time slot selection:
- Event: onPickupTimeSlotSelect(timeSlot) when the user clicks or confirms an available time slot.
- Actions: Compose a pickupDateTime value using the chosen date and the slot’s time range; call setCurrentOrderPickupTime with this value; on success, update the displayed pickup time and ensure that the Order summary uses the updated pickupDateTime.

Payment method selection:
- Event: onOpenPaymentSelector.
- Actions: Call getPaymentMethodsSummary to fetch all saved cards; display the list with the current order.paymentCardId and/or defaultPaymentCardId marked.
- Event: onPaymentCardSelect(paymentCardId).
- Actions: Call setCurrentOrderPaymentCard; on success, update the assigned card displayed on the order page; if the call fails, preserve the previous selection and display an error.
- Event: onClickManageCards.
- Actions: Navigate to account_payments.html?returnTo=order.html (preserving any existing query string if supported).

Authentication actions:
- Event: onSubmitLoginForm(email, password).
- Actions: Call loginWithEmailPassword; on success, update global account state, close the login modal, and re-evaluate whether placing the order is now allowed; on failure, show validation errors on the form.
- Event: onSubmitSignupForm(fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, acceptTerms).
- Actions: Call signupAccount; on success, update global account state from accountDashboard and close the signup view; on failure, show error messages.
- Event: onClickLogout.
- Actions: Call logoutCurrentAccount; on success, clear account state and disable or adjust order-placement-related controls until the user logs in again.

Place order:
- Event: onClickPlaceOrder.
- Preconditions checked in UI: order has one or more items, a pickupDateTime is set, a paymentCardId is assigned in the Order, and the user is logged in.
- Actions: If preconditions are met, call placeCurrentOrder; while the request is pending, disable the button and show a busy state; on success, transition to an order confirmation flow using the returned Order data; on failure, display the message from the response and highlight any missing configuration, leaving the order editable.

Navigation to menu and rewards:
- Event: onClickAddMoreItems.
- Actions: Navigate to menu.html (optionally carrying through current query parameters if supported).
- Event: onClickViewRewards.
- Actions: Navigate to rewards.html so users can inspect and apply rewards before returning to the order page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and the results of the assigned interfaces. URL parameters:
- orderId: When present, the application treats this ID as the current order context before calling getCurrentOrderDetails and getCurrentOrderSummary. The underlying application or routing layer associates this orderId with the active session so that these interfaces return data for the specified active order. If orderId is absent, getCurrentOrderDetails and getCurrentOrderSummary are assumed to operate on the default current draft order for the session.
- highlightItemId: When present, after getCurrentOrderDetails resolves and the items list is rendered, the page attempts to locate the item whose OrderItem.id matches highlightItemId. If found, the page scrolls that item into view and applies a temporary emphasis state (for example, focus or a short-lived highlight) managed by the front-end state. If the item is not found, the page renders normally without additional emphasis.

Order and UI state updates:
- The canonical order state lives on the server and is always refreshed via getCurrentOrderDetails and/or getCurrentOrderSummary. The front-end uses responses from updateOrderItemQuantity, updateOrderItemOptions, removeOrderItem, setCurrentOrderPickupTime, setCurrentOrderPaymentCard, and placeCurrentOrder to update its local representation but does not persist any order state in localStorage or other long-lived client storage.
- After any mutating interface call that affects totals (updateOrderItemQuantity, updateOrderItemOptions, removeOrderItem), the page updates summary values directly from the returned orderTotals object. Optionally, the page can re-invoke getCurrentOrderDetails if a full refresh is needed (for example, when a large part of the order may have changed concurrently).
- Authentication state (logged-in vs guest) is maintained outside localStorage in a session layer; this page only interacts with it through loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, and logoutCurrentAccount. The ability to place the order is recomputed after each authentication-related call.
- Pickup time state is derived from order.pickupDateTime returned by getCurrentOrderDetails and from the last successful setCurrentOrderPickupTime call. The time slot list is transient and recomputed whenever getAvailablePickupTimeSlots is called for a user-selected date.
- Payment card selection state for the order uses the order.paymentCardId (from getCurrentOrderDetails) and available cards from getPaymentMethodsSummary. The page recomputes the “selected card” UI each time it receives new card data or after setCurrentOrderPaymentCard succeeds.
- Navigation to other pages (menu.html, rewards.html, account_payments.html) uses URL query parameters exclusively, such as returnTo=order.html when going to account_payments.html. Any additional routing information needed by the implementation is appended as URL parameters rather than being stored in localStorage.

components.functionality: Manages login, logout, and signup actions within the page context and exposes a simple isLoggedIn state and basic account information to other components (such as the order actions bar).
event_handlers: onSubmitSignupForm(fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, acceptTerms) -> signupAccount
onAuthStateChange() -> re-evaluate whether placing the order is allowed

components.functionality: Loads and refreshes the current order context based on URL parameters, providing Order, OrderItem list, pickup Store, and PaymentCard data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit() -> read orderId, highlightItemId from URL and call getCurrentOrderDetails
onOrderMutated() -> optionally re-call getCurrentOrderDetails or getCurrentOrderSummary for full refresh
onLoadSuccess(order, items, store, paymentCard) -> propagate data to order-items-list, order-summary-panel, pickup-time-selector, payment-method-selector
onLoadError() -> display load error and disable mutating actions

components.functionality: Displays all items in the current order with controls for adjusting quantity, size, and milk type, and for removing items. Responds to highlightItemId by emphasizing the target item.
event_handlers: onRender(items, highlightItemId) -> scroll and emphasize matching item if present
onItemUpdateSuccess(orderItem, orderTotals) -> update local item entry and notify order-summary-panel
onItemRemoveSuccess(orderTotals) -> remove item from list and notify order-summary-panel
onItemActionError() -> show inline item-level error state

components.functionality: Allows the user to view and modify the pickup time for the current order by selecting a date and time slot from available options.
event_handlers: onInit(order.pickupDateTime) -> display current pickup time or prompt for selection
onTimeSlotsLoaded(timeSlots) -> render available and unavailable slots
onSetPickupTimeSuccess(pickupDateTime) -> update displayed pickup time and notify order-summary-panel
onSetPickupTimeError() -> show error and revert UI to previous selection

components.functionality: Displays the currently assigned payment card for the order and lets the user select a different saved card or navigate to manage payment methods.
event_handlers: onInit(order.paymentCardId) -> show currently assigned card if available
onCardsLoaded(cards, defaultPaymentCardId, order.paymentCardId) -> mark selected card in UI
onSetCardSuccess(paymentCard) -> update assigned payment card display and notify order-summary-panel
onSetCardError() -> show error and restore previous selection

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the order, including item count, subtotal, tax if provided, total, pickup store name, and pickup time. Updates in response to changes in items, pickup time, and payment method.
event_handlers: onInit() -> getCurrentOrderSummary or derive summary from getCurrentOrderDetails
onItemsChanged(orderTotals) -> update subtotalAmount and totalAmount display
onPaymentCardChanged(paymentCard) -> update payment indication (e.g., masked card details)
onRefreshSummary() -> call getCurrentOrderSummary if needed for accuracy

components.functionality: Provides primary actions for the order: place order, add more items, and view rewards. Evaluates whether the order is ready to be placed and triggers the placement flow.
event_handlers: onInit(order, items, paymentCard, accountState) -> compute whether place-order button should be enabled
onDependenciesChange(order, items, paymentCard, isLoggedIn) -> re-compute button enabled/disabled state
onClickPlaceOrder() -> validate preconditions in UI, then call placeCurrentOrder
onPlaceOrderSuccess(order) -> transition to confirmation flow or emit event to page shell
onPlaceOrderError(message) -> display error and keep order editable

components.functionality: Displays an empty-state message and navigation controls when there are no items in the current order.
event_handlers: onItemsLoaded(items) -> if items length is zero, show empty order state and hide order-specific controls

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, narrow, centered column for all cart content, mirroring the login page’s linear stack. A thin global account/session bar runs full-width at the very top. Below it, all order-related components are stacked vertically inside a centered column (max-width ~440–480px): order context/loading state at the top of the column, then the 'Current Order' heading and order-items list, followed by pickup time selector, payment method selector, order summary, and finally a full-width primary order action area at the bottom. The empty-order-state conditionally replaces the main stack, occupying the same central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, narrow, centered column for all cart content, mirroring the login page’s linear stack. A thin global account/session bar runs full-width at the very top. Below it, all order-related components are stacked vertically inside a centered column (max-width ~440–480px): order context/loading state at the top of the column, then the 'Current Order' heading and order-items list, followed by pickup time selector, payment method selector, order summary, and finally a full-width primary order action area at the bottom. The empty-order-state conditionally replaces the main stack, occupying the same central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top account/session bar occupies a small slice of vertical space (~10–15% of viewport height at most), visually light. The central column is vertically padded so it visually dominates the page while still leaving generous white space above and below, especially on taller viewports. Each block (items list, pickup time, payment method, summary, actions) spans nearly the full width of the central column with consistent inner padding, behaving like subtle cards without heavy borders. Footer-like or tertiary text within components uses tighter spacing to convey lower hierarchy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, airy feel. Elements within each component are well-separated vertically (roughly 1–1.5 line-heights between related items, more above section titles and below blocks). Avoid cramming controls; each selector and summary block feels like its own clean section. Only essential information is visible: items, time, payment, totals, and the primary CTA. No sidebars or marketing content appear, keeping cognitive load low and focusing the user on reviewing and placing the order.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows from the thin top bar (account / session) down into the centered column. Within the column, the path is strictly vertical: order context / heading at the top, then down through the item list, then pickup time, then payment, then the order summary, culminating in the large, full-width primary 'Place Order' action bar as the main visual anchor. Supporting or conditional content like loading states and empty states occupy the same vertical positions, preserving flow. Secondary details (e.g., small helper text inside selectors) are visually subdued and placed below their primary labels, similar to the login page’s 'Forgot password?' and 'Sign Up' lines.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, centered column of cart content on a white background, framed by a thin, full-width account/session bar at the top. Below this bar, the main content snaps into a narrow, single column aligned to the same horizontal margins, with generous white space above and below so the cart feels focused and uncluttered. At the top of this column, the user sees the current order context and heading, then scrolls smoothly down through a neatly stacked sequence of blocks: the list of items in the order, a simple pickup time selector, a payment method selector, and a concise order summary. Each block nearly fills the column width, with consistent horizontal padding and soft rounded corners echoing the minimalist login design. Anchoring the bottom of the column is a wide, brand-blue 'Place Order' action bar reminiscent of the login page’s primary button, visually drawing the eye and signaling the final step. If the order is empty or still loading, those states occupy the same central column instead of introducing extra layout complexity. The result is a calm, linear flow that makes reviewing and placing a pickup order feel as straightforward as logging in.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global account session bar is positioned at the very top of the page, spanning the full browser width edge-to-edge. It is relatively shallow in height, occupying roughly 10–15% of the viewport height at most, mirroring the thin header bar from the mockup. Internally, it follows a three-part horizontal layout constrained to the same content width as the central column: a left-aligned section (logo and back navigation), a centered section (e.g., current store or page name such as 'Current Order'), and a right-aligned section (account avatar or status). These three regions are aligned to the same left and right margins used by the main column below. There is modest vertical padding so the bar feels light, with ample white space around the elements instead of heavy backgrounds. The rest of the page content begins just below this bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document; not sticky or floating. It defines the top edge of the layout, and the main content scrolls beneath it in a standard flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-context-loader sits at the top of the main content column, directly below the global account session bar and aligned to the same centered max-width (~440–480px). When active (i.e., while fetching the current order), it occupies the same vertical space where the order heading and items list would normally appear. It spans approximately 100% of the central column width with equal left and right margins relative to the viewport, and uses generous vertical padding so any skeleton rows or loading indicator feel comfortably spaced rather than cramped. It pushes subsequent components (order-items-list, pickup-time-selector, etc.) downward in the vertical stack. When loading is complete, the loader collapses entirely with no residual height, letting the order-items-list move up to the top of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the vertical content flow, expanding or collapsing based on loading state. It does not overlay the header or other sections; it simply takes their space while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-items-list forms the topmost major block inside the central content column once loading is complete. It is centered on the page, constrained to the same max-width (~440–480px), with generous top margin separating it from the header bar (approximately 10–15% of the viewport height so the central content feels visually centered on taller screens). At its top, a left-aligned section title such as 'Current Order' and a short, lighter description sit, similar in hierarchy to the 'Welcome!' and instruction text on the login page. Below this, the list of items is stacked vertically, each item taking the full available width of the column minus consistent internal padding. Items are separated by comfortable vertical spacing—roughly one item’s height of whitespace between rows—to maintain a low-density feel. Controls like quantity steppers or edit/remove links sit within each item row but are aligned cleanly, without breaking the single-column perception. The block has subtle rounded corners and possibly a faint boundary (shadow or light border) to read as a card while remaining visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the central column. It expands vertically based on the number of items, pushing the pickup-time-selector downward while preserving the single-column flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup-time-selector sits directly below the order-items-list with a noticeable but not extreme vertical gap, similar to the spacing between form fields in the login mockup (roughly 24–32px of vertical space). It spans the same central column width, centered on the page with matching left/right margins. A left-aligned label such as 'Pickup Time' appears at the top of this block, followed by the interactive control (dropdown, pill options, or time slots) filling roughly 90–100% of the column width. The selector’s height is sufficient to feel comfortable to tap, with internal padding and soft rounded corners consistent with input fields on the login page. Any helper text (e.g., 'Ready in about 10 minutes') appears beneath the control in smaller, subdued text, with tight vertical spacing so it reads as secondary. The block is visually distinct but relatively compact, ensuring it doesn’t challenge the order summary or action bar for focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the central column, maintaining its relative position under the items list and above the payment method selector. It grows minimally based on content but otherwise keeps a stable height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment-method-selector follows directly beneath the pickup-time-selector, separated by consistent vertical space similar to the gap between other sections in the column. It is aligned and sized identically to the pickup-time block—same max-width, centered, using the full column width with internal padding. A left-aligned label such as 'Payment Method' appears at the top, with saved cards or options stacked vertically or shown as a primary selector occupying almost the full width. Each option uses a minimalist style with clear selection state but minimal decoration, echoing the clean, flat inputs from the mockup. This entire section is moderately tall but kept visually quiet, ensuring that while it is clearly visible, it doesn’t overshadow the order summary or the final action button below. Tiny microcopy, such as 'Manage cards in your account', if present, sits beneath the main control in smaller type and subtle color, mirroring the login page’s lower-priority links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline in the vertical stack, it expands vertically based on the number of payment options but maintains the central column alignment and spacing rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel is placed immediately below the payment-method-selector, with a slightly more pronounced vertical gap to signal a transition into the final review area—similar to how the primary button on the login page stands out after the form fields. It spans the full width of the central column (within the ~440–480px max), with clear internal padding on all sides and subtle rounded corners. Within the panel, summary lines (subtotal, taxes, pickup fee, total) are arranged in a clean vertical list aligned to the left, with amounts right-aligned on the same line, but everything still contained within the single-column card. The 'Total' line is given a bit more visual weight (stronger type or more space above), echoing the prominence of the login page’s primary label before the CTA. Overall height is moderate; it’s tall enough to feel substantial but not as tall as the items list. There is a small vertical gap below this panel before the order-actions-bar, visually grouping summary and action together as the final decision zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-sticky. It maintains its position directly above the action bar, expanding vertically depending on the number of summary rows but preserving the central alignment and clean stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-actions-bar anchors the bottom of the main content column, occupying the final position in the vertical flow. It follows immediately under the order-summary-panel with a bit less vertical separation than between larger sections, so it reads as tightly connected to the summary, similar to how the 'Sign In' button closely follows the password field. The primary 'Place Order' button spans nearly the full width of the central column (about 90–100% of its width), centered horizontally, with generous vertical padding and robust height, matching the visual weight of the mockup’s main blue CTA button. It uses the brand blue as a solid fill on a white background, with soft rounded corners. If secondary actions (like 'Cancel' or 'Modify Order') are included, they are rendered as smaller, text-only or low-emphasis controls directly below the primary button, in a single line or two, with tighter vertical spacing and smaller font—similar in hierarchy to 'Forgot Password?' and 'Don’t have an account?' on the login page. The entire actions bar feels like the final, most prominent element of the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the bottom of the content column. It is not sticky by default; it scrolls naturally with content. On very short pages it may sit roughly in the lower third of the viewport due to the generous top and bottom padding of the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-order-state replaces the usual cart stack within the same central column when there are no items in the order. It is centered horizontally and vertically biased toward the middle of the viewport, similar to how the login content dominates the center of the screen. The state occupies roughly the same width as the other components (max ~440–480px) and sits below the global account session bar with generous top margin to feel visually centered. Inside this block, a concise heading (e.g., 'Your cart is empty') appears at the top, with a short explanatory line below and a single primary action (e.g., 'Browse Menu') styled like a full-width button. The primary action spans most of the column’s width, mirroring the login CTA size and prominence. Any tertiary text or links are placed beneath this button, small and lightly spaced, mimicking the login page’s bottom-most text. When active, this component pushes or completely replaces the order-items-list, selectors, summary, and action bar, preserving the simple, uncluttered, single-column experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional and mutually exclusive with the main cart content. It appears in the same central column region and participates in the normal scroll flow, but when shown, the other cart components are hidden or collapsed so only this state occupies the core of the page.

name: Account Dashboard
title: Account Dashboard
description: Central page for authenticated customers to view their account overview, including favorite store, loyalty status, current order summary, and quick access to key account and ordering actions.

core_features: Load and display a high-level account dashboard summary using getAccountDashboardSummary, including customer name, email, loyalty tier, loyalty points balance, favorite store, and quick links.
Load and display a compact current order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary, showing item count, amounts, pickup store, and pickup time when an active order exists.

Provide quick navigation actions (e.g., Start an Order, Order History, Rewards, Payment Methods, Notification Preferences, Security / Password) driven by the quickLinks data returned from getAccountDashboardSummary and mapped to the defined outgoing pages.
Handle unauthenticated or expired sessions by presenting an inline login form that uses loginWithEmailPassword to authenticate and then load the dashboard data.
Optionally allow account creation from this page via a signup form that uses signupAccount, and on success immediately initialize the dashboard state from the returned accountDashboard data.
Allow the user to log out via logoutCurrentAccount and then either redirect away from the dashboard or show the login/signup gate again.
Use the source URL parameter to adjust contextual messaging or focus (for example, highlighting that the account was just created when source=signup) without relying on localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Access dashboard with active session:
1) User navigates to account_dashboard.html (possibly via the header Account link).
2) On page init, the dashboard context loader component calls getAccountDashboardSummary.
3) In parallel, it calls getCurrentOrderSummary to obtain current order data.
4) If getAccountDashboardSummary returns account data successfully, the page renders the greeting, loyalty summary, favorite store card, quick links, and any current order widget.
5) The source URL parameter (if present) is read to determine whether to show a contextual status banner (e.g., after signup) or to focus a specific quick link.
6) User can then click quick links or actions to navigate to related pages (orders, payments, rewards, etc.).

Workflow 2 – Direct access without active session (login on dashboard):
1) User opens account_dashboard.html directly without an active session.
2) On init, the dashboard context loader calls getAccountDashboardSummary.
3) If the call fails with an authentication error or no account context is returned, the loader sets the page into an unauthenticated state.
4) The auth gate panel becomes visible, showing the login form (and optionally a sign-up tab or toggle).
5) User enters email and password and submits the login form, which calls loginWithEmailPassword.
6) If loginWithEmailPassword returns success and isLoggedIn=true, the auth gate hides, and the returned account object is used to seed initial dashboard state.
7) The page optionally re-calls getAccountDashboardSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary to ensure complete, up-to-date dashboard data is loaded.
8) The dashboard sections then render as in the authenticated workflow.

Workflow 3 – Account created on another page and redirected here:
1) User completes sign-up on a separate page that calls signupAccount.
2) That page, on success, navigates to account_dashboard.html?source=signup (passing the source parameter).
3) On load, account_dashboard.html calls getAccountDashboardSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary.
4) If account data is available, the dashboard renders normally.
5) The source-status banner component reads source=signup and displays a contextual confirmation message (e.g., that the account is ready) without additional marketing elements.
6) The user proceeds to use the dashboard, e.g., by clicking Start an Order or Rewards.

Workflow 4 – Sign up directly on the dashboard when not logged in:
1) User navigates to account_dashboard.html without a session and lands in the unauthenticated state.
2) In the auth gate panel, the user switches to the sign-up mode (e.g., via a tab or toggle).
3) User fills in fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms, then submits the sign-up form, which calls signupAccount.
4) On success, signupAccount returns an accountDashboard object; the sign-up form passes this object to the dashboard context loader.
5) The loader populates dashboard UI directly from accountDashboard without requiring an immediate additional getAccountDashboardSummary call (or it may optionally refresh via that interface).
6) The URL may be updated to include source=signup (e.g., via history.replaceState) to reflect that this session originated from sign-up and to drive contextual banners.
7) The dashboard proceeds in the authenticated state, and the user can interact with quick links and actions.

Workflow 5 – Using quick links and starting an order:
1) From a loaded dashboard, user reviews their loyalty points and favorite store.
2) User clicks the Start an Order quick link, which navigates to menu.html (no additional parameters per the outgoing connection definition).
3) Alternatively, user clicks Order History, which navigates to account_orders.html?sortBy=date_desc.
4) Clicking Payment Methods navigates to account_payments.html, Notification Preferences to account_notifications.html, Security / Password to account_security.html, and Rewards to rewards.html.
5) Navigation is performed via normal URL changes; any state needed after navigation is passed exclusively by URL parameters (where defined), not via localStorage.

Workflow 6 – Reviewing current order and logging out:
1) When an active order exists, getCurrentOrderSummary returns hasActiveOrder=true and associated summary fields.
2) The current-order widget displays the item count, subtotal/total amounts, and pickup store/time.
3) User may click an action in this widget (e.g., Edit Order) that navigates to menu.html to update the order.
4) When the user is done, they click the Logout control in the dashboard header.
5) The logout button calls logoutCurrentAccount.
6) On success, the page either redirects to index.html or reloads account_dashboard.html without authenticated context, causing the auth gate panel to show again (via the same unauthenticated logic as in Workflow 2).
interactions: On page load: The dashboard-context-loader component initializes and immediately calls getAccountDashboardSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary. It sets internal loading states until responses return and then triggers rendering or auth gating depending on the results.

On authentication error from getAccountDashboardSummary: The loader sets a 'needsAuthentication' state and displays the auth-gate-panel instead of the dashboard content; it may also show a generic error message if the failure is not authentication-related.

Login form submission: When the user submits email and password, the login-form component calls loginWithEmailPassword. While awaiting the response, it shows a submitting state (e.g., disabling the submit button). On success, it passes account and isLoggedIn to the dashboard-context-loader, hides the auth gate, and triggers a refresh of dashboard data (or uses the returned account directly). On failure, it displays the message field returned and keeps the user on the form.

Sign-up form submission: When the user submits the sign-up form fields, the signup-form component calls signupAccount. During the call, it shows a submitting state. On success, it uses the returned accountDashboard object to seed the dashboard state and optionally updates the URL to include source=signup using history APIs. On failure, it exposes the message returned by the interface and keeps them in sign-up mode.

Logout interaction: When the user clicks the Logout control, the dashboard-header component calls logoutCurrentAccount. While waiting, it can disable the button. On success, the component either redirects to index.html or reloads account_dashboard.html so that getAccountDashboardSummary is called again and falls into the unauthenticated path. On failure, it can surface the message from logoutCurrentAccount and keep the user logged in.

Clicking Start an Order: When the user clicks the Start an Order quick link or button, the quick-links-list component navigates the browser to menu.html using a standard link. No additional client-side interface calls occur on click.
Clicking Order History: When the user clicks Order History in the quick-links list, the component navigates to account_orders.html?sortBy=date_desc, matching the outgoing connection definition.
Clicking Payment Methods: When the user clicks Payment Methods, the component navigates to account_payments.html.
Clicking Notification Preferences: When the user clicks Notification Preferences, the component navigates to account_notifications.html.
Clicking Security / Password: When the user clicks Security / Password, the component navigates to account_security.html.
Clicking Rewards (from loyalty card or quick link): When the user clicks the Rewards shortcut or loyalty summary card, the component navigates to rewards.html.

Interacting with quickLinks driven by API data: The quick-links-list component reads the quickLinks array from getAccountDashboardSummary. For each entry, it uses the key property to decide which outgoing navigation to perform. Recognized keys (e.g., representing order history, payments, notifications, security, rewards, start_order) are mapped to the specified target URLs and parameters; unrecognized keys may be shown as generic links or ignored depending on implementation rules.

Selecting auth mode in auth gate: In the unauthenticated state, the user can switch between login and sign-up modes within the auth-gate-panel (for example, via tab buttons). This interaction only affects which form (login-form or signup-form) is visible and does not trigger any interface calls until a form is submitted.

Source-aware behavior: On each render, the source-status-banner reads the source URL parameter. If it equals specific values such as 'signup', it displays context-appropriate, purely functional status information and may focus or scroll to particular components (for example, highlighting quick links that make sense immediately after account creation). If source is absent or unrecognized, the banner remains hidden.

Clicking current-order widget actions: If getCurrentOrderSummary indicates hasActiveOrder=true, the current-order-widget shows one or more actions. Clicking an action such as 'Edit Order' navigates to menu.html so the user can modify the current order. Navigation uses a simple link; additional order details are fetched on that page via its own interfaces.

Manual refresh or retry: When an interface call fails for network or generic reasons, affected components (e.g., dashboard-context-loader, current-order-widget) may render a Retry control. Clicking Retry re-calls the corresponding interface (getAccountDashboardSummary or getCurrentOrderSummary) and re-renders the UI upon completion.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state driver for this page is the URL and its query parameters, particularly the 'source' parameter. On every page load, the application parses account_dashboard.html?source=<value>. If 'source' is 'signup', the source-status-banner component displays a post-signup status indicator and may adjust focus to the quick-links relevant for new users. If 'source' originated from index.html or contact.html (passed by those pages), the banner or other components can adapt behavior (e.g., emphasize starting an order or reviewing notifications) based solely on this parameter. The page does not use localStorage for any state persistence. Instead, all personalized account data (account owner identity, favorite store, loyalty, quickLinks) is retrieved from getAccountDashboardSummary, and current order information is retrieved from getCurrentOrderSummary. Authentication state is inferred from the success or failure of these calls and from the results of loginWithEmailPassword and signupAccount. In-memory component state (e.g., isLoading, needsAuthentication, currentAuthMode, errorMessage) is transient and reset on each full page load or URL navigation. After sign-up within this page, the URL can be updated using the browser history API to include source=signup so that the page's behavior remains URL-driven. Navigation to other pages uses URL parameters only where explicitly defined (such as 'sortBy=date_desc' for account_orders.html); no cross-page state is stored outside the URL or the server-side session accessible via the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: On initialization, orchestrates loading of the account dashboard data and current order summary. It calls getAccountDashboardSummary to retrieve fullName, email, loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, favoriteStore, and quickLinks, and calls getCurrentOrderSummary to retrieve any active order information. Based on the responses, it sets global page state flags such as isLoading, isAuthenticated, hasActiveOrder, and passes normalized data to child components. If getAccountDashboardSummary fails due to authentication issues, it marks isAuthenticated=false so that the auth-gate-panel is shown instead of the dashboard.

Reads the 'source' query parameter from the current URL and displays a small contextual status message or callout above the main dashboard content. For example, when source=signup, it may indicate that the account was just created and is ready to use. If no 'source' parameter is present or the value is not recognized, the banner remains hidden. It does not call any interfaces directly but depends on URL state and optionally on authenticated/unauthenticated state from the dashboard-context-loader.

Displays the functional page title 'Account Dashboard' along with a compact greeting using the account holder's name and email when authenticated. It also exposes a Logout control that allows the user to end their session via logoutCurrentAccount. When logged out successfully, it triggers navigation away from the dashboard or a soft reload that causes the auth gate to show.

Controls whether the user sees login/sign-up forms or the main dashboard content. When notified by the dashboard-context-loader that the user is not authenticated (e.g., because getAccountDashboardSummary could not return account data), this panel displays either the login-form or signup-form based on its internal mode (e.g., a toggle). When authentication succeeds via loginWithEmailPassword or signupAccount, it hides itself and notifies the loader to reinitialize or update dashboard state.

Provides fields for email and password and submits them via loginWithEmailPassword. On submission, it validates inputs on the client side, calls the interface, and reacts to the response: if success and isLoggedIn=true, it emits a loginSuccess event carrying the returned account object and message; if not, it displays the message returned and keeps the form visible. It also handles loading states and disables multiple simultaneous submissions.

Provides fields for full name, email, password, confirm password, and a terms acceptance control. On submission, it validates that required fields are present and that passwords match, then calls signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms. If signupAccount returns success, it uses the returned accountDashboard object to seed the dashboard view, emits a signupSuccess event, and optionally updates the URL to source=signup. On failure, it shows the message from the response and keeps the user on the form.

Shows a concise overview of the logged-in customer, including their full name, email address, and loyalty tier as provided by getAccountDashboardSummary or signupAccount. This card is purely informational and does not perform navigation itself, but it may provide a link or control to adjust account settings on other pages if required by future designs.

Displays the favorite store information from getAccountDashboardSummary, including store name, city, and state when available. If no favoriteStore is present in the response, it shows a neutral informational state indicating that a favorite store is not set, without using hardcoded store data. The card can be used later to navigate to a store-selection experience (not defined in the current outgoing connections), but at this stage it primarily acts as read-only context.

Summarizes the user’s loyalty status using loyaltyPointsBalance and loyaltyTier from getAccountDashboardSummary or from the accountDashboard returned by signupAccount. It also provides a clear call-to-action to manage or redeem rewards by linking to rewards.html, and it may visually react when loyaltyPointsBalance changes (e.g., after redemptions handled on other pages).

Renders the quickLinks array from getAccountDashboardSummary as a set of actionable links or buttons. Each quickLinks item includes key, label, and description. The component maps known key values to specific outgoing navigation targets: for example, an item whose key corresponds to order history navigates to account_orders.html?sortBy=date_desc; a payments key navigates to account_payments.html; notifications to account_notifications.html; security to account_security.html; rewards to rewards.html; and a start-order key to menu.html. It uses only the label and description provided by the API for display text and avoids hardcoding business data.

Displays a compact view of the current pickup order based on getCurrentOrderSummary. When hasActiveOrder=true, it shows itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore name, and pickupDateTime. If hasActiveOrder=false, it displays a neutral state encouraging the user to start an order via the Start an Order link (which navigates to menu.html). It does not modify the order directly; it only surfaces summary information and navigational actions.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All dashboard content sits inside a single, narrow, vertically stacked central column that is horizontally centered on the page, following the login mockup. The page uses a strong top-to-bottom reading order: (1) a thin global header (outside this spec), (2) an optional full-width status banner just under the header, (3) the dashboard page header at the top of the central column, (4) an auth-gate region that either shows login/sign-up forms or, when authenticated, reveals the dashboard summary cards and widgets, (5) lower-priority quick links at the bottom. No sidebars or secondary columns are introduced; even dashboard cards are stacked one above another within the same central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All dashboard content sits inside a single, narrow, vertically stacked central column that is horizontally centered on the page, following the login mockup. The page uses a strong top-to-bottom reading order: (1) a thin global header (outside this spec), (2) an optional full-width status banner just under the header, (3) the dashboard page header at the top of the central column, (4) an auth-gate region that either shows login/sign-up forms or, when authenticated, reveals the dashboard summary cards and widgets, (5) lower-priority quick links at the bottom. No sidebars or secondary columns are introduced; even dashboard cards are stacked one above another within the same central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header area occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height, with the main dashboard column sitting in the central band of the page and generous vertical padding above and below to maintain the airy feel. The central column is constrained to a max-width of about 420–480px and spans 90–100% of width on smaller screens. Each card, form, and widget takes nearly the full width of this column with consistent internal padding. Top-of-page structural elements (banner, header) are visually thinner, while the main card stack in the middle occupies the visual focus. Lower-priority text and links at the bottom of the column use tighter vertical spacing and smaller font size to minimize their visual weight.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a low content density similar to the login mockup: only essential blocks are shown at once, spaced with generous vertical gaps (roughly 24–32px between major blocks, 12–16px between related elements inside a block). Each card or form has ample internal padding and breathing room, ensuring the dashboard still feels clean and uncluttered despite having more information than a simple login screen. There are no decorative elements or dense multi-column tables; every component is simple, flat, and easy to scan.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves in a clear vertical flow: first across the thin top header, then to any active status banner, then to the central page header near the top of the main column. From there, the auth state determines the flow: unauthenticated users are guided straight down into the login form (primary CTA), followed by a secondary sign-up option. Authenticated users see a friendly greeting summary first, then the current order widget, followed by favorite store and loyalty cards, and finally a compact quick-links list. Primary CTAs (like 'Start an Order' or 'View Details') use the brand blue and occupy the full column width, acting as strong anchors similar to the original 'Sign In' button.

layout.overall_description: The Account Dashboard sits within a full-width white page, but all meaningful content is funneled into a single, centered column that never grows wider than a compact card on a mobile screen. A slim global header runs across the very top, and just beneath it an optional, equally slim status banner may appear as a full-width bar. Below this, the page’s main content begins: near the upper-middle of the viewport, the dashboard header introduces the page with a clean, left-aligned title and short description inside the narrow central column. Beneath this, the column is either filled by a tall, comfortable login/sign-up panel (for unauthenticated users) or, for authenticated users, by a vertical stack of flat, rounded cards: a greeting summary, a current-order snapshot, a favorite-store tile, and a loyalty-status card. Each card stretches nearly the full width of the column, separated by generous white space so each feels distinct yet part of the same rhythm. At the bottom of the column, a slimmer quick-links list offers secondary navigation options with tighter spacing and smaller text. Throughout, the design remains minimalist, with the brand blue reserved for key actions and accents, preserving the clear, relaxed flow of the original login mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context loader appears only while the dashboard data is being fetched. When active, it overlays the entire viewport with a semi-transparent white layer, subtly dimming all underlying components including the central content column and header. At the vertical and horizontal center of the screen, a compact loader element (e.g., spinner and short 'Loading your dashboard…' text) sits within a small rounded rectangle, about 320px wide, aligned to the same central axis as the main column. There is ample whitespace around the loader, echoing the low-density style and ensuring the user’s eye is drawn directly to this central indicator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the center of the viewport, covering and disabling interaction with underlying content until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The source status banner is a thin horizontal bar positioned directly beneath the global site header, spanning the full viewport width from left to right. It occupies roughly 40–56px in height, making it visually slimmer than main content but tall enough to be legible. Its content (icon, short status text, optional inline link) is centered within the same max-width content area as the main column, but the bar’s background color stretches edge-to-edge across the screen. On large screens, this keeps the banner aligned with the central column margins; on small screens it effectively fills the width. There is modest vertical padding inside the banner, and a comfortable gap (16–24px) separates it from the top of the dashboard header content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that sits beneath the global header and above the main content column, pushing content down when visible. It scrolls away with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard header sits at the top of the main central column, starting roughly 10–15% down from the top of the viewport, preserving generous white space above it. The column itself is centered horizontally and constrained to about 420–480px max width, with equal left and right margins. Inside this block, the page title ('Account Dashboard') is left-aligned and set as the most prominent text at this level, with a short descriptive line directly below it in a lighter weight and smaller size. Vertical space between the title and description is around 8–12px, while a larger gap of approximately 24–32px separates this header from whatever follows (auth gate panel or first card). This mirrors the 'Welcome!' title block in the mockup, serving as an introduction without crowding the elements that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the central column, scrolling with the page. Acts as the top anchor of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth gate panel occupies the position immediately below the dashboard header within the central column, spanning nearly the full width of the column with consistent horizontal padding inside (about 16–24px). Vertically, it forms a unified card-like area with a subtle white background and soft, rounded corners, visually separated from the page background by a light border or shadow consistent with the minimalist style. When the user is not authenticated, this panel stretches downward to contain the login form as the primary content, with the signup form as a secondary, visually subordinate option; it may take up roughly one-third to one-half of the visible viewport height depending on fields. Generous vertical spacing inside keeps labels, fields, and buttons from feeling cramped. When the user is authenticated, this panel effectively collapses to a minimal wrapper or becomes visually absent, allowing the first summary card to sit in the same vertical slot, preserving the linear flow and spacing of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the central column that conditionally displays either authentication UI (when logged out) or remains minimal/hidden (when logged in), maintaining the same vertical position in the stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The login form is the primary content inside the auth gate when the user is not authenticated. It is arranged as a simple vertical stack that fills the width of the auth gate’s inner content area. At the top of the form, a concise heading (e.g., 'Sign In') is left-aligned, followed by a one-line instruction or hint separated by about 8–12px. Below this, form fields are laid out one per row: Email label, email input, then a 12–16px gap, then Password label and password input. Inputs span nearly 100% of the available width, with 12–16px internal padding and subtle rounded corners. Immediately below the password field, after a 16–20px gap, a full-width primary CTA button in the brand blue is placed; it spans almost the entire width of the column (minus the card’s internal padding) and is about 44–48px tall, acting as a strong visual anchor similar to the 'Sign In' button in the mockup. A smaller gap (8–12px) below the button holds a single secondary link (e.g., 'Forgot Password?') left-aligned or slightly centered, using a lighter typographic treatment. Overall, the block is tall enough to feel substantial but remains visually simple and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained, standard block within the auth gate panel; scrolls with the page and does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The signup form is positioned directly below the login form within the auth gate panel but is visually treated as secondary. It begins with a thin horizontal rule or subtle divider line spanning the inner width of the panel, with a vertical gap of about 16–20px separating it from the login form above. Below the divider, a short text line such as 'Don’t have an account? Sign Up' appears, left-aligned and set in a smaller font size than the main heading, with the 'Sign Up' portion styled as an inline link or secondary CTA in brand blue. Depending on interaction design, the full signup fields either expand beneath this line within the same card or open in a separate state; when expanded, fields follow the same vertical, full-width, low-density pattern as the login form but with slightly tighter vertical spacing to emphasize that this is an alternative action. The overall vertical footprint of the signup block is noticeably smaller than the login form when collapsed, ensuring the primary focus remains on signing in.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block nested within the auth gate panel, appearing directly after the login form area. It scrolls with the page and may expand/collapse but does not change its horizontal positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user is authenticated, the greeting summary card becomes the first major card in the central column, sitting in the same vertical region where the auth gate would otherwise appear, directly below the dashboard header and separated from it by approximately 24–32px. The card spans the full width of the central column with 16–24px interior padding and subtly rounded corners. The greeting (e.g., 'Good morning, Alex') is left-aligned at the top and visually prominent, acting as the primary text focal point. Below it, a short line summarizing the account (e.g., 'You have 120 points and 1 active order.') appears with a small 8–12px gap. If a key CTA is included (such as 'Start an Order'), it appears as a full-width brand-blue button at the bottom of the card, separated from the text by about 16–20px and echoing the form CTA style. The card itself takes up roughly one quarter to one third of the central column’s visible height, depending on content, and has generous whitespace above and below to distinguish it as the dashboard’s welcome anchor.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card in the central column, scrolling with the page. It does not stick but acts as the first card in the authenticated state stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite store card appears below the greeting summary and current-order widget (if present), maintaining the vertical stacking order in the central column. It is separated from the component above by about 24px of whitespace, maintaining the dashboard’s low-density rhythm. The card spans the full column width with consistent internal padding and rounded corners matching other cards. Content inside is left-aligned: a short title like 'Your Favorite Store' at the top, followed by the store name and key details on the next line(s) with a small 8–12px gap. If there is an action such as 'Change Store' or 'View Store Details,' it appears as either a subtle text link aligned to the right within the card’s footer area or as a compact button aligned to the left; in either case it is visually less dominant than the primary CTA buttons used elsewhere. The overall height of this card is moderate, roughly similar to the loyalty card, helping the middle section of the dashboard feel balanced and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card in the central column, scrolling with the page, sitting in the mid-stack of summary cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loyalty summary card is stacked directly below the favorite store card, separated by another 24px vertical gap. It shares the same full-width card treatment—flat white background, subtle rounded corners, and 16–24px interior padding. At the top, a left-aligned title like 'Loyalty Status' appears, followed by a concise, easily scannable line or two summarizing the user’s points, tier, or rewards. A simple visual indicator (such as a progress bar or small numeric highlight) sits beneath the text with about 8–12px spacing, but remains minimalist and flat to match the design DNA. An optional secondary CTA like 'View Rewards' may appear as a small text link or low-emphasis button along the bottom edge of the card, aligned left. The card’s height is modest, similar to or slightly taller than the favorite store card, ensuring it feels important but not as dominant as the greeting or current-order card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card within the central column. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the content and does not have special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick links list is the final major component in the central column, placed below the last summary card (typically the loyalty card) with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. Rather than a heavy card, it can feel lighter: either a low-emphasis card with minimal elevation or simply a text block with a subtle top divider line matching the style used in the login/sign-up divider. The title (e.g., 'Quick Links') is left-aligned and smaller than the main card titles, signaling lower priority. Below the title, 3–6 links are arranged in a vertical list, each taking the full column width with 8–12px vertical spacing between them. Links are left-aligned, text-only, and styled in brand blue with no icons, preserving the clean, minimal look. The overall block height is relatively small compared to other components, allowing it to sit comfortably near the bottom of the scroll without competing for attention with the primary summary and action cards above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the bottom of the central content column, scrolling with the page. It has no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current order widget sits directly below the greeting summary card in the authenticated view, separated from it by a generous 24–32px gap. It spans the full width of the central column with consistent interior padding and rounded corners. The top of the card features a left-aligned title like 'Current Order' with slightly less emphasis than the greeting but still prominent.
