Below, concise order details (items, status, pickup time) are arranged in short lines, with 8–12px spacing between them to keep the information legible and not crowded. A strong primary CTA—such as 'View Order' or 'Add Items'—is placed at the bottom of the card as a full-width brand-blue button echoing the login button style, ensuring it stands out as a key action area. The widget’s height is similar to or slightly taller than the greeting card, making it one of the most visually substantial blocks and naturally drawing the eye as the main functional area of the dashboard.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card in the central column, positioned high in the authenticated state stack. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

name: Payment Methods / Wallet
title: Payment Methods
description: Manage saved payment cards for your account: view existing cards, add new cards, set a default card, and deactivate cards used for pickup orders.

core_features: Authenticate the user (login, signup, logout) so only logged-in customers can manage payment methods, using loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, and logoutCurrentAccount.
Load and display all saved payment cards with a clear indication of which card is the default, using getPaymentMethodsSummary.
Add a new payment card by submitting card number, expiration date, CVV, cardholder name, and optional billing ZIP/label, using addPaymentCard.
Set any active saved card as the default payment method for future orders, using setDefaultPaymentCard.
Deactivate an existing payment card so it is no longer available for new orders, using deactivatePaymentCard.
Show a compact summary of the current pickup order (if any) to give context while editing payment methods, using getCurrentOrderSummary.
Provide context-aware navigation back to either the order checkout or the account dashboard based on the returnTo URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Managing payment methods during checkout (returnTo=order.html)
1. User is on order.html and decides to change or add a payment method.
2. User navigates to account_payments.html with the URL parameter returnTo=order.html.
3. On page load, the controller component parses returnTo and calls getPaymentMethodsSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary.
4. The page shows the list of saved cards and a mini order summary widget, plus a navigation control labeled to return to checkout.
5. User clicks to add a new card, completes the add card form, and submits.
6. The form calls addPaymentCard with the entered card details.
7. On success, the new PaymentCard is appended to the list and the default card indicator is refreshed based on the returned card and/or a subsequent getPaymentMethodsSummary call.
8. Optionally, the user marks the new card as default by clicking a 'Set as default' control on that card.
9. The list component calls setDefaultPaymentCard with the selected paymentCardId and updates the default indicator when the call succeeds.
10. The user returns to order.html via the contextual navigation control, preserving any existing query parameters of returnTo where appropriate as defined by the router.

Workflow 2: Managing payment methods from the account dashboard (no returnTo, or returnTo=account_dashboard.html)
1. User clicks a Payment Methods link from account_dashboard.html or a footer link, navigating to account_payments.html (optionally with returnTo=account_dashboard.html).
2. On load, the controller parses the URL and calls getPaymentMethodsSummary.
3. The page shows the list of saved cards and a breadcrumb or back link labeled to return to the account dashboard.
4. User reviews cards; from this list they can click 'Set as default' for any active card.
5. The list component calls setDefaultPaymentCard and, on success, updates which card is marked as default.
6. User can click deactivate on any non-default card.
7. The list component shows a confirmation interaction; if the user confirms, it calls deactivatePaymentCard with that paymentCardId.
8. On success, the deactivated card is removed from the active list (or marked inactive, depending on display rules), and any errors are displayed via a shared feedback component.
9. When finished, the user clicks the back navigation to return to account_dashboard.html.

Workflow 3: Logged-out user accessing the Payment Methods page
1. A non-authenticated user navigates to account_payments.html (from any source).
2. The controller attempts to determine that there is no active account context (mechanism is handled by the broader app); in this state, payment management components stay disabled.
3. The page shows an authentication panel with login and signup tabs.
4. If the user already has an account, they complete the login form and submit.
5. The auth panel calls loginWithEmailPassword; on success, it updates the page to the authenticated state and triggers getPaymentMethodsSummary (and getCurrentOrderSummary if relevant).
6. If the user needs a new account, they switch to the signup section, complete the signup form, and submit.
7. The auth panel calls signupAccount; on success, it transitions to the authenticated view and triggers getPaymentMethodsSummary.
8. Once authenticated, the user can add, set default, or deactivate cards as in Workflows 1 and 2.
9. The user may log out by clicking a logout control in the header or account area; this calls logoutCurrentAccount, and on success returns the page to the logged-out state, re-showing the auth panel.
interactions: On page load: The controller component parses the returnTo query parameter from the URL. It then calls getPaymentMethodsSummary. If the app determines the user is authenticated, it also may call getCurrentOrderSummary to populate the mini order summary. During these calls the page shows loading states for the card list and the order summary widget.

Login form submit: When the login form in the auth panel is submitted, the component calls loginWithEmailPassword with the entered email and password. On success, it hides the login form, may surface account-related details (such as the email), and triggers a refresh of payment methods and the order summary. On failure, it displays the returned message in an error state without hardcoding the message content.

Signup form submit: When the signup form is submitted, the component calls signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms. On success, it transitions to the authenticated view and triggers getPaymentMethodsSummary. On failure, it displays the returned message and keeps the form data so the user can correct it.

Logout click: When the user clicks the logout control, the component calls logoutCurrentAccount. On success, it clears or hides sensitive payment information, shows the auth panel again, and stops calling payment-related interfaces until the user logs in.

Add card form input: The user enters cardNumber, expMonth, expYear, cvv, cardholderName, optional billingZip, and optional label. Client-side validations (such as required fields and allowed input formats) are performed without embedding static values such as sample card numbers.

Add card form submit: When the add card form is submitted, the component calls addPaymentCard with the input values. While waiting, the submit control is disabled and a loading state is shown. On success, the newly returned PaymentCard object is added to the list, and the default indicator is updated using the returned card data and/or a refreshed getPaymentMethodsSummary call. Any errors returned are shown via the shared feedback component.

Set default card click: In the card list, each active non-default card exposes a 'Set as default' interaction (e.g., a button or selection control). When activated, the component calls setDefaultPaymentCard with that card's id. On success, the component updates the default visual state using the returned defaultPaymentCardId, marking that card as default and clearing the previous default.

Deactivate card click: For each active card that is allowed to be deactivated, the card row provides a 'Deactivate' interaction. When clicked, a confirmation step appears. If the user confirms, the component calls deactivatePaymentCard with that card's id. On success, the card is removed from the active list (or visually marked as inactive based on design), and the component may call getPaymentMethodsSummary again to ensure consistency.

Order summary interactions: If getCurrentOrderSummary indicates hasActiveOrder is true, the order summary widget displays the orderId, itemCount, amounts, pickupStore name, and pickupDateTime. Clicking a 'View order' or contextual navigation control triggers a navigation to order.html, using the existing routing scheme and preserving any necessary identifiers as defined elsewhere.

Breadcrumb / back navigation click: The breadcrumb or back link determines its destination based on the parsed returnTo parameter. If returnTo is equal to order.html, it navigates to order.html; if returnTo is equal to account_dashboard.html or not provided, it navigates to account_dashboard.html. This navigation is handled by constructing the destination URL with any necessary query parameters (excluding local storage usage).

Feedback message interactions: When any interface call returns success: false, the shared feedback component displays the message to the user. The user can dismiss these messages via a close control, which clears the message state but does not alter any data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state control is the 'returnTo' URL parameter. On each load of account_payments.html, the page controller inspects window.location.search to extract 'returnTo'. If 'returnTo' equals 'order.html', the page:
- Renders navigation controls that send the user back to order.html when clicked.
- Prioritizes calling getCurrentOrderSummary so that the order summary widget is visible, providing context while managing payment methods.
If 'returnTo' equals 'account_dashboard.html' or is missing/empty, the page:
- Renders navigation that returns the user to account_dashboard.html when clicked.
- Treats payment management as a standalone account management task; the order summary widget can still be shown if getCurrentOrderSummary indicates there is an active order, but it is not emphasized as a checkout step.

Internal page state (managed in memory, not via localStorage) includes:
- Authentication state: whether the user is considered logged in. When the loginWithEmailPassword or signupAccount calls succeed, the state transitions to authenticated and the controller triggers getPaymentMethodsSummary (and optionally getCurrentOrderSummary). When logoutCurrentAccount succeeds, the state transitions to logged-out, and card/order data is cleared from the UI.
- Loading and error states for each interface call: separate flags track whether getPaymentMethodsSummary, addPaymentCard, setDefaultPaymentCard, deactivatePaymentCard, getCurrentOrderSummary, loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, and logoutCurrentAccount are in progress or have failed. These flags drive disabled controls and feedback rendering.
- Card list state: an in-memory collection of PaymentCard objects from getPaymentMethodsSummary, plus the defaultPaymentCardId returned. This state is updated whenever addPaymentCard, setDefaultPaymentCard, or deactivatePaymentCard succeed. No card data is hardcoded; all card properties such as cardBrand, last4, expMonth, expYear, cardholderName, billingZip, isDefault, isActive, and label are taken directly from API responses.
- Form state: current values of login, signup, and add card form inputs live in memory and are reset after successful submissions or when the user manually clears them.
- Feedback state: a list or queue of messages derived from API responses, rendered by the feedback component and cleared when the user dismisses them or when the page is reloaded.

All navigations to and from this page use URL-based routing (e.g., account_payments.html?returnTo=order.html). No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used to manage functional state; navigation context is determined solely via URL parameters and live API responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the Payment Methods page: parses URL parameters (including returnTo), determines authentication state (as provided by the broader app), triggers initial data loading via getPaymentMethodsSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary (when appropriate), manages global loading/error states, and exposes callbacks to refresh data after mutations such as adding, setting default, or deactivating a card.

Handles login and signup directly on the Payment Methods page when the user is not authenticated. Provides a login form (email and password) and a signup form (name, email, password, confirm password, terms acceptance). On successful authentication, hides itself and notifies the controller so payment data can be loaded.
event_handlers: onAuthSuccess (notifies payment-page-controller to load getPaymentMethodsSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary)

components.functionality: Displays a simple account header for this page: the page title, an optional indication of the signed-in account (e.g., email or name if available), and a logout control. Also hosts a compact link to the account dashboard or other account-level navigation as defined by the app.
event_handlers: onLogoutClick (calls logoutCurrentAccount and, on success, clears payment data and shows account-auth-panel)

components.functionality: Renders breadcrumbs or simple back navigation for the Payment Methods page. Uses the parsed returnTo query parameter to decide whether the back action sends the user to order.html or account_dashboard.html.
event_handlers: onBackClick (navigates to order.html when returnTo=order.html, otherwise to account_dashboard.html)

components.functionality: Displays a compact summary of the current pickup order when getCurrentOrderSummary indicates there is an active order. Shows item count, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore name, and pickupDateTime, and can provide a link to view or continue the order.

Shows all active saved payment cards returned by getPaymentMethodsSummary, clearly indicating which one is the default. Each card row may show cardBrand, last4, expMonth/expYear, cardholderName, optional billingZip, isDefault, isActive, and label. Provides per-card controls to set default and deactivate, with appropriate disabling logic (for example, preventing deactivation in certain conditions if required by the product rules).
event_handlers: onLoadPaymentMethods (uses data from getPaymentMethodsSummary via payment-page-controller)
onSetDefaultClick (calls setDefaultPaymentCard with the selected paymentCardId and updates the default indicator on success)
onDeactivateClick (after confirmation, calls deactivatePaymentCard, then removes or updates the card entry on success)
onAfterCardAdded (receives a PaymentCard from payment-card-add-form and inserts it into the list before or after a refresh)

components.functionality: Provides a form for adding a new payment card. Collects cardNumber, expMonth, expYear, cvv, cardholderName, optional billingZip, and optional label. Performs input validation, then submits data to addPaymentCard. On success, passes the returned PaymentCard object to the payment-methods-list component and optionally resets the form.
event_handlers: onSubmit (calls addPaymentCard with current form values)
onAddCardSuccess (emits the created PaymentCard to payment-methods-list and triggers a refresh in payment-page-controller if needed)

components.functionality: Sub-component used within each card row in payment-methods-list that visually indicates which card is currently set as default. It listens for changes to defaultPaymentCardId and updates the indicator accordingly.
event_handlers: onDefaultChanged (updates indicator when setDefaultPaymentCard returns a new defaultPaymentCardId)

components.functionality: Centralized component for displaying success, warning, and error messages resulting from interface calls on this page. It receives messages from all other components and presents them in a dismissible list or stacked format.
event_handlers: onShowMessage (invoked after responses from loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, logoutCurrentAccount, getPaymentMethodsSummary, addPaymentCard, setDefaultPaymentCard, deactivatePaymentCard, getCurrentOrderSummary)
onDismissMessage (clears a specific message from the UI state)

components.functionality: Provides footer-level navigation actions relevant to this page, such as links to the account dashboard, order page, and other account sections, depending on the app’s global navigation rules. It may mirror the behavior of payments-breadcrumbs-nav regarding the returnTo destination.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same single, centered main column pattern as the mockup: a full‑width, thin header bar at the very top, and below it a narrow central content column (max-width ~440–480px) aligned to the same left/right margins as the header’s inner content. Inside this column everything is stacked in a strictly vertical order: breadcrumbs, a compact account/auth panel, feedback messages (when present), a small current order summary, the main payment methods list, the add‑card form, and finally a low‑priority footer navigation block. No sidebars or multi‑column layouts are introduced; even the order summary and payment cards sit in the same single column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same single, centered main column pattern as the mockup: a full‑width, thin header bar at the very top, and below it a narrow central content column (max-width ~440–480px) aligned to the same left/right margins as the header’s inner content. Inside this column everything is stacked in a strictly vertical order: breadcrumbs, a compact account/auth panel, feedback messages (when present), a small current order summary, the main payment methods list, the add‑card form, and finally a low‑priority footer navigation block. No sidebars or multi‑column layouts are introduced; even the order summary and payment cards sit in the same single column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar uses a shallow vertical height (~10–12% of the viewport), while the central column sits in the middle band of the page with generous top and bottom padding, leaving noticeable white space above the first content block and below the last. Each major section inside the column (order summary, payment list, add form) spans nearly the full width of the column with consistent horizontal padding. Vertical spacing between major sections is larger (24–32px) to keep them visually distinct, while spacing between related elements inside a section (labels, inputs, captions) is tighter (8–16px) to group them. Footer nav text at the bottom uses smaller vertical padding to emphasize its low priority.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains intentionally low‑density: only essential wallet features are visible—breadcrumbs, a brief account/auth block, a short order summary, a concise list of saved cards, and a clean add‑card form. Each block is given breathing room, and the number of visible interactive elements at once is limited. The design avoids explanatory paragraphs or promotional content; text is short and functional. This preserves a calm, corporate feel and reduces cognitive load, echoing the login mockup’s simplicity.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s strong vertical path. The eye lands first on the header bar at the very top, then drops into the central column: it reads the breadcrumbs, then the contextual account/auth state, then any feedback messages (error/success), and quickly reaches the current order summary. From there attention moves to the dominant wallet area: the list of saved cards and its associated controls. The primary visual anchors are the brand‑blue buttons for key actions (e.g., 'Add Card', 'Set as default'), which sit near the center of the column. After that, the eye naturally continues downward to lower‑priority controls and helper text in the footer navigation. Links are understated but clearly blue, consistent with the main CTA color.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, modern wallet view framed within a white, full‑width canvas. A thin, full‑width header bar sits at the top, using the brand blue only for key accents and maintaining a flat, minimal appearance. Below it, the entire experience is focused into a single narrow column centered on the page, roughly 440–480px wide, leaving generous white space to the left and right. At the top of this column a small breadcrumbs row subtly orients the user without demanding attention. Directly beneath, a compact account/auth panel confirms who is signed in or prompts for quick re‑authentication, but visually stays quiet so the main wallet content can dominate. A reserved stripe for feedback messages appears next, ensuring any card‑related errors or confirmations are noticed before interacting further. The current order mini summary follows as a light card, providing just enough context about how payment methods are used without becoming a second layout column. Below this, the core wallet area unfolds: a tidy, vertically stacked list of payment cards, each in a flat, rounded rectangle spanning the column width, with a small default indicator badge aligning to the right. Immediately under the list, an add‑card form mirrors the login form’s structure: left‑aligned labels, full‑width inputs, and a large brand‑blue primary button that becomes the central visual anchor. Finally, a compact footer navigation line at the bottom of the column offers secondary actions or links, with minimal spacing and smaller typography, closing the vertical flow gently instead of abruptly. Everything aligns to the same invisible grid, reads top‑to‑bottom without distraction, and feels consistent with the original minimalist login design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the structural container for the entire wallet page. It occupies the full browser viewport width and height, providing a plain white background. Inside it, a centered main content column is defined: this column uses the implicit 12‑column grid but effectively spans a narrow central band (approximately 4–6 grid columns wide), capped at a max-width of about 440–480px, and is horizontally centered. The column starts a comfortable distance below the header bar (around 48–64px of top margin/padding) and ends with a similar buffer above the bottom of the viewport when content is short, preserving the spacious feel. All other main components (breadcrumbs, auth panel, feedback, order summary, payment list, add‑card form, footer nav) are stacked vertically in this column, left‑aligned within it, with consistent horizontal padding (e.g., 16–24px inside any card/form).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural wrapper that defines the centered column and vertical stacking order; it scrolls normally with the page and does not itself appear as a distinct visual block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header bar spans the full width of the page at the very top, forming a thin horizontal strip roughly 56–72px tall, corresponding to about 10–12% of the viewport height on typical screens. Within it, content is constrained to the same left and right margins as the central column, creating a visually aligned vertical edge. The internal layout follows a three‑part horizontal structure: a left section for the logo and a subtle 'Back' or 'My Account' control, a center section that may host a compact location or navigation control, and a right section for account/profile access. All three segments align vertically within the bar, with generous horizontal padding on either side. The header is visually lighter than the main CTAs—using a white or very light background with the brand blue reserved mainly for the logo and key text—so that the user quickly moves their attention down into the wallet content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element positioned at the top of the document flow; it is the first block on the page and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs navigation sits at the very top of the centered content column, immediately below the header bar’s bottom edge. It is a single, slim horizontal line of text, left‑aligned within the column and spanning roughly 90–100% of the column width. Vertically, it is separated from the header bar by a modest gap (about 24px) to clearly signal a transition from global navigation to page context, but it uses tight internal line height to remain compact. The breadcrumb text uses smaller typography than section titles and lighter weight, ensuring it reads as a quiet orientation aid rather than a headline. There is a small vertical gap (12–16px) below the breadcrumbs before the account/auth panel begins, keeping it visually grouped with the top of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline block within the central column; it participates in the normal vertical flow between the header bar and the account/auth panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account auth panel is placed directly beneath the breadcrumbs within the central content column, occupying the full width of that column with a subtle card-like container (flat white background, light gray border or faint shadow, and slightly rounded corners to match the inputs/buttons style). Vertically, it is given moderate padding (around 16–20px top and bottom) and internal vertical spacing of 8–12px between its elements. If it presents a login or re‑authentication form, the labels and inputs inside it follow the same vertical stacking pattern as the main forms: left‑aligned labels above full‑width inputs, all aligned to the column’s left edge. The panel is not as tall as the main wallet content; it should feel compact and unobtrusive, roughly 1/4–1/3 of the central column’s height when visible on a typical screen. A small gap of about 16–24px separates its bottom edge from the feedback messages center below, maintaining a clean grouping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card-like block in the central column, participating in the normal vertical stacking; it can collapse or reduce height when not needed, but does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback messages center appears directly below the account auth panel within the same central column, stretching to match the column’s width. When inactive, it may be represented only by minimal reserved space or be entirely absent; when active, it forms a slim horizontal band or compact card (around 40–64px tall) containing one or more lines of feedback text. Messages are left‑aligned, and the block’s background may use a very light tinted color (soft green, red, or blue) with a thin border to maintain the minimalist look while making feedback noticeable. Vertical padding within the block is restrained (8–12px) to keep it compact. There is a comfortable yet modest gap (16–24px) below this element before the current order mini summary begins, so that feedback is read in sequence but does not merge visually with the following card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow; it expands in height when displaying messages and collapses or disappears when no feedback is present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current order mini summary is positioned below the feedback area, still within the single centered column, and spans the full width of that column. It appears as a shallow card with a flat white background, subtle border, and rounded corners consistent with other UI elements. The card’s height is modest—roughly 1/6–1/5 of the viewport height on typical screens—so it does not compete with the payment list, but it is given enough vertical padding (16–20px) for content to breathe. Inside, the order title and key details are left‑aligned and stacked vertically, with a small vertical spacing (4–8px) between lines. A tiny label or pill‚ such as 'This order will be charged to…', may align near the top of the card, but all content stays within this single column. There is a slightly larger gap beneath the card (around 24–32px) before the payment methods list begins, clearly separating context (current order) from editable wallet content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card block that scrolls with the rest of the column, sitting naturally between feedback and the payment methods list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment methods list sits just below the current order mini summary and becomes the dominant content block in the central column. It starts with a left‑aligned section title (e.g., 'Saved cards') that spans the column width, followed by vertically stacked card entries. The entire list area uses the full width of the central column, with consistent internal horizontal padding so that each payment card aligns perfectly with form fields and buttons below. Each individual payment method entry is represented as a flat, rounded rectangle row—roughly 56–80px tall—with card brand, masked number, and expiry details aligned to the left, and key actions (like 'Set as default' or 'Deactivate') either aligned to the right or directly beneath the card details, but still within the same single column width. Vertical spacing between card entries is comfortable (12–16px) to keep the list airy, even when multiple cards are present. The list block overall can occupy approximately 1/3–1/2 of the column’s visible height when populated, anchoring the user’s focus before they reach the add‑card form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable section as part of the central column; its height grows as more cards are added, pushing subsequent content downward in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add‑card form is positioned directly under the payment methods list, separated from the last card by a slightly larger vertical gap (around 24–32px) or a very thin horizontal rule to indicate a new task area. It mirrors the original login form’s structure: labels and fields are arranged in a clean vertical sequence, left‑aligned, and spanning nearly the full width of the central column. Each label sits directly above its input with a tight but readable gap (4–8px), and each input uses consistent height and internal horizontal padding (e.g., 12–16px), with slightly rounded corners and a subtle border. There is more vertical space (12–16px) between input rows and the primary CTA button at the bottom of the form. That button runs almost edge‑to‑edge within the column (e.g., 100% of column width minus side padding), is safely finger‑tappable, and is rendered in the brand blue, making it the main visual anchor of the entire page below the header. A small helper line (if needed) could appear below the button with very tight spacing, but overall the form is compact enough to occupy around 1/4–1/3 of the visible column height, maintaining the page’s airy quality.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form block anchored in the central column; it stays in the normal vertical flow and scrolls with the page, often visible alongside the bottom of the payment list on medium-height screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The default card indicator is a small, inline status element embedded within each relevant card in the payment methods list rather than a standalone block. Within a card row, it is positioned toward the right side, horizontally aligned with the main card details and vertically centered in the card’s height. Visually, it appears as a compact pill or tag with slightly rounded corners, using the brand blue as either a border or fill and small, uppercase or bold text like 'Default'. It occupies only a small fraction of the card’s width (roughly 15–20%) and height, so it does not disrupt the card’s overall layout. Generous white space is maintained around it within the card, with a margin of at least 8–12px from the card’s right edge, keeping it clearly separated from any action buttons. Its compactness preserves the clean, low‑density feel while still making the default status quickly scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status element positioned relative to the payment card entry; it moves with the card when the list scrolls and does not affect the surrounding components’ placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback messages center appears directly below the account auth panel within the same central column, stretching to match the column’s width. When inactive, it may be represented only by minimal reserved space or be entirely absent; when active, it forms a slim horizontal band or compact card (around 40–64px tall) containing one or more lines of feedback text. Messages are left‑aligned, and the block’s background may use a very light tinted color (soft green, red, or blue) with a thin border to maintain the minimalist look while making feedback noticeable. Vertical padding within the block is restrained (8–12px) to keep it compact. There is a comfortable yet modest gap (16–24px) below this element before the current order mini summary begins, so that feedback is read in sequence but does not merge visually with the following card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow; it expands in height when displaying messages and collapses or disappears when no feedback is present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payments page footer nav is the final element in the central column, positioned below the add‑card form. It is separated from the form’s primary button by a sizeable vertical gap (around 32–40px), signaling that all core wallet actions are above and that this area contains only secondary links or informational text. The footer nav typically consists of one or two short lines of smaller, muted text (for example, 'Back to account settings' with an inline link) that are either centered or subtly left‑aligned within the column; in both cases, the text does not exceed the column width and maintains the same horizontal padding as other content. Vertical padding inside the footer nav block is minimal (8–12px), and there is generous white space below it before the end of the viewport, preserving the airy bottom margin seen in the login mockup. Links within this block use the brand blue but are not styled as buttons, staying visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, low-profile block at the bottom of the main content column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport bottom.

name: Rewards Overview
title: Rewards Overview
description: Page for signed-in and guest users to view loyalty points context, recent reward activity, and browse filterable rewards with navigation to detailed reward pages.

core_features: Display the current user’s loyalty points balance and loyalty tier by calling getRewardsOverview.
Show a condensed list of recent reward redemptions using getRewardsOverview.recentRedemptions to give context to loyalty activity.
Retrieve and display available reward filter options (categories, rating thresholds, price ranges) using getRewardFilterOptions.
Browse rewards in a list or grid using getRewardList with URL-driven filters for category, minimum rating, maximum price, and sort order.
Allow users to adjust filters and sort order; changes update URL parameters and trigger a new getRewardList call without reloading the entire page.
Provide navigation from each reward card or link to reward_detail.html with the selected reward ID and the current category filter passed as fromCategory.
Offer a contextual call-to-action to navigate to menu.html with a bakery filter when viewing bakery-related rewards.
Show a compact current-order summary widget using getCurrentOrderSummary so users understand how rewards browsing relates to any in-progress order.

Allow users to log in, sign up, or log out directly from this page using loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, and logoutCurrentAccount, and then immediately reflect the updated loyalty context via getRewardsOverview.
user_workflows:
View and filter rewards as a logged-in user:
1. User navigates to rewards.html (optionally with URL parameters such as category, minRating, maxPrice, sortBy).
2. On page load, the application reads the URL parameters and determines the initial filter state.
3. The page calls getRewardsOverview to obtain loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, and recentRedemptions and renders the overview summary and recent activity.
4. The page calls getRewardFilterOptions to load categories, ratingOptions, and priceRanges for the filter controls.
5. The page calls getRewardList with the current URL parameters (category, minRating, maxPrice, sortBy) to render the rewards list or grid.
6. The user adjusts one or more filters (for example, changes category or minRating). The page updates the URL query string accordingly and calls getRewardList again with the new values.
7. The user clicks a reward card or its View Reward link. The page navigates to reward_detail.html?id=<rewardId>&fromCategory=<currentCategory>.

Log in from the Rewards Overview page:
1. A user who is not authenticated lands on rewards.html.
2. The page displays rewards browsing and filter controls normally, but the loyalty overview section indicates that the user should sign in to see their personal points and tier.
3. The user opens the login form (for example, via a header account control) and submits email and password.
4. The page calls loginWithEmailPassword with the provided credentials.
5. If the response indicates success and isLoggedIn is true, the application updates in-memory session state with the account data.
6. The page then calls getRewardsOverview again to fetch the user-specific loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, and recentRedemptions.
7. The updated loyalty information is shown without changing the current filters or URL parameters; the rewards list remains based on the current URL state.

Sign up for a new account and view rewards context:
1. A new user on rewards.html opens the sign-up form from the account controls.
2. The user fills in fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms.
3. The page calls signupAccount with the provided values.
4. If signupAccount returns success, the application sets the user as logged in with the returned accountDashboard information.
5. The page then calls getRewardsOverview to load the initial loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, and recentRedemptions for the new account.
6. The rewards filters and list remain based on the current URL parameters; only the loyalty overview and recent redemptions segment updates.

Browse bakery rewards and navigate to the menu:
1. User opens rewards.html with category=bakery in the URL or selects the bakery filter in the UI.
2. The page reads category=bakery from the URL and calls getRewardList with category set accordingly.
3. After rewards load, a contextual control (such as a button) for browsing bakery items on the main menu becomes available.
4. When the user activates this control, the page navigates to menu.html?category=bakery.
5. URL parameters for rewards browsing remain on the rewards page (for back/forward navigation), while the user sees bakery items on the menu page.

Use rating and price filters to narrow rewards:
1. User arrives on rewards.html without rating or price filters in the URL.
2. The page calls getRewardFilterOptions and renders rating and price controls based on returned options.
3. The user selects a rating threshold and a price range.
4. On filter change, the page updates minRating and maxPrice in the URL query string to match the selected values.
5. The page calls getRewardList with the updated minRating and maxPrice (and any existing category or sortBy) and rerenders the rewards list.
6. The user can clear filters; this removes the corresponding URL parameters and triggers another getRewardList call with the reduced set of filters.

View recent reward activity from overview:
1. A logged-in user opens rewards.html.
2. The page calls getRewardsOverview and displays recentRedemptions in a list.
3. For each redemption, the UI may display its associated reward information (using embedded data included in recentRedemptions, if available) or a generic entry.
4. The user selects a recent redemption entry associated with a specific reward.
5. The page navigates to reward_detail.html?id=<rewardId>&fromCategory=<currentCategoryFilterIfAny> to allow the user to see full details or redeem again.

Understand current order context while browsing rewards:
1. User opens rewards.html while having an in-progress order.
2. The page calls getCurrentOrderSummary.
3. If hasActiveOrder is true, the page shows a compact summary with itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime.
4. The user can use this information to decide which rewards might be relevant to the current order, while filters and browsing continue to operate normally based on the URL parameters.
interactions: On initial page load, read category, minRating, maxPrice, and sortBy from the URL; use them as the single source of truth for the initial filter state before making any interface calls.
Call getRewardsOverview after login or signup and on initial load (when the user is already authenticated) to populate the loyalty summary and recent reward activity.
Call getRewardFilterOptions on page load and use the returned categories, ratingOptions, and priceRanges to render filter controls dynamically, without hardcoding specific options.
Call getRewardList with the current filter state whenever URL parameters for category, minRating, maxPrice, or sortBy change, and rerender the rewards list or grid from the returned data.
Update URL parameters (using pushState or replaceState) whenever the user changes filters or sort order, so that reloads and back/forward navigation restore the same rewards view.
Handle click on a reward card or its View Reward link by navigating to reward_detail.html with id set to the reward’s ID and fromCategory set to the current category URL parameter, if present.
Handle click on the Browse Bakery Items CTA by navigating to menu.html?category=bakery, preserving the rewards page state in the browser history.
Handle login form submission by calling loginWithEmailPassword; on success, reflect the new session, then refresh the loyalty section using getRewardsOverview without changing the current filters.
Handle signup form submission by calling signupAccount; on success, treat the user as logged in, then load loyalty context via getRewardsOverview while preserving existing URL parameters.
Handle logout action by calling logoutCurrentAccount and, on success, clearing or anonymizing loyalty-related sections while preserving the rewards browsing state from the URL.
Handle filter reset or clearing actions by removing corresponding URL parameters (for example, removing minRating or maxPrice) and then calling getRewardList with the updated parameter set.
Handle browser back/forward navigation events by re-reading the URL parameters and re-calling getRewardList, ensuring the UI reflects the URL state rather than any internal-only state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for the rewards list is encoded in the URL query parameters category, minRating, maxPrice, and sortBy. On page load, the application parses these parameters to construct the current filter/sort state. If a parameter is absent, the component uses its internal default (for example, no category filter, no rating threshold, or a default sort order) but does not immediately alter the URL unless the user interacts with filters. Whenever the user changes filters or sorting, the page updates the URL query string to match the new values and then calls getRewardList using those values. This ensures that reloading the page or using browser navigation restores the same filtered view, because the UI always binds to the URL parameters as the source of truth. The loyalty context (loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, recentRedemptions) and the current order summary are not encoded in the URL; they are loaded via getRewardsOverview and getCurrentOrderSummary respectively, based on the current authenticated session. Authentication state itself is managed via loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, and logoutCurrentAccount, and is not stored in URL parameters. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used for filters or session-related data; only the URL and in-memory state within the current page lifecycle are used.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same single, centered main column as the login mockup, constrained to a narrow max-width (~440px) and aligned to the global page margins. Keep a thin horizontal header bar at the very top spanning full width, with three-part alignment (logo/brand on the left, page-level control in the center, utility action on the right). Beneath the header, stack content strictly in a top-to-bottom order: page title and short description (explaining Rewards Overview), then a compact points summary block, followed by recent activity, then the filter controls, and finally the list/grid of rewards. Any secondary text (fine print, helper copy) appears below the main interactive area, using smaller text and tighter spacing. No sidebars, no multi-column splits inside the main content; everything remains vertically sequenced and left-aligned within the central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same single, centered main column as the login mockup, constrained to a narrow max-width (~440px) and aligned to the global page margins. Keep a thin horizontal header bar at the very top spanning full width, with three-part alignment (logo/brand on the left, page-level control in the center, utility action on the right). Beneath the header, stack content strictly in a top-to-bottom order: page title and short description (explaining Rewards Overview), then a compact points summary block, followed by recent activity, then the filter controls, and finally the list/grid of rewards. Any secondary text (fine print, helper copy) appears below the main interactive area, using smaller text and tighter spacing. No sidebars, no multi-column splits inside the main content; everything remains vertically sequenced and left-aligned within the central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~10–15% of the viewport for the thin header bar with minimal height and subtle padding. The middle band (~60–70% of the viewport height) is dedicated to the main rewards content: title, summary, activity, filters, and rewards list, all within the narrow centered column and surrounded by generous white space above and below. The points summary and key CTAs occupy the most visual weight within this column but still stay within its width, with comfortable interior padding. The bottom band (~15–20% of the viewport, variable) is for secondary content: helper text, disclaimers, or small links, kept visually light and spaced closer together to signal lower priority while not feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low content density with clear breathing room between logical blocks. Each section (intro, points summary, activity list, filters, rewards listing) is separated by consistent vertical spacing that is clearly larger than the spacing between items inside a section. Text blocks are short; descriptions are one or two lines. Lists such as recent activity and rewards cards are visually simplified: minimal borders, subtle dividers or soft card outlines, and no heavy decorative graphics. Inputs and buttons (e.g., filters, view details) use consistent padding and match the form-field proportions from the login page, preserving the calm, uncluttered feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a straight vertical flow: first to the header's center element (e.g., a compact control or section label), then down to the bold page title in the central column. From there, the eye moves to a clearly emphasized points summary (e.g., current points and status), then flows downward to a concise recent activity section, and then to filter controls. The primary visual anchor on the page is a strong blue primary button or key action near the top of the rewards listing (such as 'Redeem' or 'View All Rewards'), visually similar in weight to the original 'Sign In' button. Below it, the eye naturally follows the list of rewards downward. Secondary actions and informational links appear under the main content, visually lighter, so they are seen after the primary actions have been considered.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, centered Rewards Overview screen framed by generous white space on both sides. At the very top, a thin full-width header bar spans the page, with the coffee shop logo left-aligned, a concise contextual control or label centered (such as a subtle location or section indicator), and a single utility action (like profile or logout) right-aligned. This bar aligns precisely with the same left and right margins that define the central content column below. From the bottom edge of the header, the main content begins after a comfortable gap, forming a narrow vertical column (roughly 440px wide) centered in the viewport. At the top of this column sits a bold, left-aligned 'Rewards Overview' title with a short, single-line description underneath. Below that, a simple points summary block occupies most of the column width, showing current points and status, styled as a lightly outlined or subtly elevated panel with internal padding but no heavy decoration. Directly under the summary, a recent activity area lists a few key transactions in a minimal vertical list, each row full-width within the column with modest spacing between rows. Next, one or two slim filter controls or dropdowns appear, spanning most of the column width but staying visually quiet, with the primary brand blue used for focus or selection only. Immediately following, the central call-to-action for exploring or redeeming rewards appears as a wide blue button, consuming nearly the full column width and echoing the dominance of the login form's 'Sign In' button. Beneath this button, the rewards themselves are shown in a simple vertical list of cards or rows, each occupying the full column width with consistent vertical spacing and minimal outlines. At the very bottom of the content column, after another subtle divider or line, low-priority text such as terms, earning rules, or a small inline link for 'Learn more about rewards' sits in smaller type, tightly spaced and visually light. The entire composition feels airy, with clear hierarchical steps from title to points to actions, all flowing naturally from top to bottom without sidebars or competing visual clusters.

name: Reward Detail
title: Reward Detail
description: Displays a specific loyalty reward, shows points required and availability, lets the user redeem points for one unit, and optionally add the redeemed item to the current pickup order.

core_features: Load reward details using the reward ID URL parameter via getRewardDetail and display name, description, category, rating, price, pointsRequired, availability, and currentPointsBalance.
Reflect whether the reward can be redeemed by using canRedeem and reasonNotRedeemable from getRewardDetail, and show an appropriate primary action (Redeem or disabled state with explanation).
Allow the user to authenticate directly on this page using loginWithEmailPassword and signupAccount, then refresh reward details and points information to enable redemption when possible.
Redeem a single unit of the reward via redeemReward, update the displayed loyalty points using updatedPointsBalance, and show a clear redemption confirmation state.
Add a successful reward redemption to the current pickup order via addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder, then refresh the mini order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary.
Display a compact current order summary bar using getCurrentOrderSummary, including itemCount and totalAmount, and provide navigation to the order page.
Provide contextual navigation back to the rewards listing using the fromCategory URL parameter to set the category filter on rewards.html.
Support logging out of the current account via logoutCurrentAccount, then clearing account-specific state and reloading reward eligibility to reflect the logged-out state.
user_workflows: View reward details and eligibility (logged-in or logged-out): 1) Page initializes and reads the id and fromCategory URL parameters. 2) If id is present, call getRewardDetail with rewardId=id. 3) Render reward information (name, category, rating, price, pointsRequired, isAvailable). 4) Use canRedeem and reasonNotRedeemable to determine if the Redeem button is enabled. 5) Independently call getCurrentOrderSummary to populate the current order summary bar. 6) If id is missing or getRewardDetail returns no reward, show an error state with only the Back to Rewards link enabled.

Authenticate and then redeem reward: 1) User arrives not logged in and sees reward details with canRedeem=false and reasonNotRedeemable (for example, indicating login is required). 2) User opens or uses the inline authentication panel and chooses either login or sign up. 3) On login form submit, call loginWithEmailPassword; on success, store returned account context in page state. 4) On sign-up form submit, call signupAccount; on success, store accountDashboard data as the current account context. 5) After either success, re-call getRewardDetail with the same rewardId to refresh canRedeem and currentPointsBalance. 6) If canRedeem is now true, the Redeem button becomes enabled and shows the pointsRequired value.

Redeem one unit of the reward: 1) User clicks the enabled Redeem button. 2) Page calls redeemReward with rewardId=id and quantity=1. 3) While the call is in progress, disable the Redeem button and show a loading state to prevent duplicate submissions. 4) If redeemReward fails, display the message from the response and re-enable the Redeem button (and optionally re-fetch getRewardDetail to sync state). 5) If redeemReward succeeds, update the displayed points balance using updatedPointsBalance, store the returned redemption object in page state, and transition the UI into a redemption confirmation state for this reward.

Add redeemed reward to current order and view it: 1) After a successful redeemReward call, the confirmation panel shows actions related to the new redemption. 2) User selects the option to add the redeemed item to the current order. 3) Page calls addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder with rewardRedemptionId equal to the id from the successful redeemReward response. 4) While adding, disable the Add to Order action to avoid duplicates. 5) If addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder fails, show the error message and allow the user to retry or leave the redemption un-applied to an order. 6) If it succeeds, store the returned orderItem in page state, refresh the order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary, and enable a View Order action. 7) When the user clicks View Order, navigate to order.html with highlightItemId set to the stored orderItem.id so the item can be highlighted on the order page.

Use the Back to Rewards navigation: 1) Page reads fromCategory from the URL on load. 2) The Back to Rewards link builds its target URL as rewards.html if fromCategory is absent, or rewards.html?category={fromCategory} if present. 3) When the link is clicked, navigate to this computed URL without additional state.

Log out from the reward detail page: 1) User clicks a logout action in the account header. 2) Page calls logoutCurrentAccount. 3) On success, clear any in-memory account context and loyalty points display that were derived from login or signup responses. 4) Immediately re-call getRewardDetail for the current rewardId to update canRedeem and currentPointsBalance for the logged-out state. 5) Update the authentication panel to show login/sign-up options again and adjust any logged-in specific actions (e.g., disabling Redeem if login is required).
interactions: On initial page load, the page reads the id URL parameter; if present, it calls getRewardDetail with rewardId=id and, in parallel, calls getCurrentOrderSummary to populate the current order bar.
Clicking the Back to Rewards link constructs a URL to rewards.html, optionally appending the category query parameter using the fromCategory URL parameter, and then navigates to that URL.

Submitting the login form triggers loginWithEmailPassword with the entered email and password; on success, it updates account state and re-fetches reward details via getRewardDetail; on failure, it shows the returned message and keeps the user in the login view.

Submitting the sign-up form triggers signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms; on success, it uses the accountDashboard object as the new account context and re-fetches reward details; on failure, it displays the message and keeps the user in the sign-up view.

Clicking the Redeem button (when enabled) triggers redeemReward with rewardId from the URL and quantity=1; during the call the button is disabled; on success, it stores the redemption, updates points display, and shows the redemption confirmation panel; on failure, it displays the error and re-enables the button.

Within the redemption confirmation panel, clicking Add to Order triggers addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder with the currently stored rewardRedemptionId; during the call the button is disabled; on success, it stores the returned orderItem, updates the order summary via getCurrentOrderSummary, and shows a View Order button tied to that orderItem.

Clicking View Order in the confirmation or in the order summary bar navigates to order.html with highlightItemId set to the latest orderItem.id added via addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder (if any); if no such item exists, it navigates to order.html without highlightItemId.

Clicking the logout action triggers logoutCurrentAccount; on success, the page clears account-related state and re-calls getRewardDetail for the current rewardId so that canRedeem and currentPointsBalance reflect the logged-out state.

If getRewardDetail indicates the reward is not available or cannot be redeemed (canRedeem=false), the UI disables the Redeem button and displays reasonNotRedeemable as an explanation, without calling redeemReward until conditions are satisfied.
If the id URL parameter is missing or invalid, the page shows an error and only exposes the Back to Rewards navigation, preventing calls to redeemReward or addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the id URL parameter, which is required to identify which reward to display. On load, if id is present, the page calls getRewardDetail(rewardId=id) and getCurrentOrderSummary(); if id is missing, the page enters an error state and only offers Back to Rewards navigation. The fromCategory URL parameter is optional and is only used to build the target URL for the Back to Rewards link; it does not affect API calls or business logic. Account state is derived exclusively from loginWithEmailPassword and signupAccount responses, and can be cleared by logoutCurrentAccount; when account state changes (login, signup, logout), the page re-calls getRewardDetail with the same rewardId to refresh canRedeem, reasonNotRedeemable, and currentPointsBalance. Redemption state is transient and stored in-memory: after a successful redeemReward(rewardId=id, quantity=1), the page keeps the returned redemption object and updatedPointsBalance; this enables showing a redemption confirmation panel and allows addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder(rewardRedemptionId=redemption.id). After addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder succeeds, the latest created orderItem.id is stored in page state as lastAddedRewardItemId and is used to construct the navigation URL to order.html?highlightItemId={lastAddedRewardItemId} when the user chooses to view the order. The page does not use localStorage or persistent client storage; all interactive state (account context, reward details, redemption, and lastAddedRewardItemId) exists only in memory for the duration of the page session, and URL parameters are the only cross-page state channel.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, manages URL parameter parsing, orchestrates calls to getRewardDetail and getCurrentOrderSummary, and provides shared state (reward, account, redemption, lastAddedRewardItemId) to child components.

Displays the core reward information returned from getRewardDetail, including reward.name, reward.description, reward.category, reward.price, reward.rating, reward.ratingCount, reward.pointsRequired, and reward.isAvailable. It also reflects eligibility to redeem using canRedeem and reasonNotRedeemable.

Shows the current loyalty points balance (from getRewardDetail.currentPointsBalance or account context), indicates whether the user can redeem this reward, and exposes the primary Redeem action. If canRedeem is false, it disables the Redeem button and surfaces reasonNotRedeemable.

Implements the redeem flow for a single unit of the reward. When the Redeem button from the eligibility panel is clicked, this component calls redeemReward with rewardId from the URL and quantity=1, manages loading and error states, updates the points balance using updatedPointsBalance, and stores the successful redemption object in page state.

Displays a confirmation once redeemReward succeeds, showing that one unit of the reward has been redeemed and reflecting the updated points balance. Provides actions to add the redeemed item to the current order and to navigate away if the user is done. It becomes visible only when a valid RewardRedemption object exists in state.

Handles applying a successful reward redemption to the current pickup order by calling addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder with the stored rewardRedemption.id. On success, it stores the returned OrderItem, updates the latest added reward order item ID in page state, and triggers a refresh of the order summary via getCurrentOrderSummary.

Calls getCurrentOrderSummary on page load and after a successful addRewardRedemptionToCurrentOrder to display whether there is an active order, along with itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, and pickupStore information. It exposes a View Order button that, when clicked, navigates to order.html using highlightItemId if a reward order item was added from this page.

Provides inline login and sign-up flows without leaving the reward detail page. It presents tabs or toggles between login and sign-up modes, calls loginWithEmailPassword or signupAccount on submit, and on success updates account context and triggers a refresh of reward details via getRewardDetail to update canRedeem and currentPointsBalance.

Shows high-level account information when the user is logged in, including account.fullName, account.email, loyaltyPointsBalance, and loyaltyTier from the latest login or signup response. It also exposes a logout control that calls logoutCurrentAccount and, on success, clears account state and requests fresh reward details.

Reads the fromCategory URL parameter and builds a Back to Rewards link that navigates to rewards.html or rewards.html?category={fromCategory}. It is available regardless of authentication or reward state, and is the primary way to return to the rewards listing.

Handles error conditions such as missing id URL parameter or getRewardDetail returning an error. In these cases, it hides redeem-specific actions, shows an explanatory error message, and leaves only safe navigation options like Back to Rewards.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a full-width page shell with a thin top header strip and a single, centered content column constrained to roughly 420–480px. Inside this central column, stack reward detail elements in a strict top-to-bottom order: reward title and meta first, then eligibility and primary redemption controls, then secondary actions and any inline authentication or messaging. Navigation (back link) sits in the top header strip area, and a compact current-order summary lives at the bottom edge of the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a full-width page shell with a thin top header strip and a single, centered content column constrained to roughly 420–480px. Inside this central column, stack reward detail elements in a strict top-to-bottom order: reward title and meta first, then eligibility and primary redemption controls, then secondary actions and any inline authentication or messaging. Navigation (back link) sits in the top header strip area, and a compact current-order summary lives at the bottom edge of the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the top 10–15% of the viewport to the account header strip (including the back navigation on the left). The main reward detail column occupies the central band of the page with generous top and bottom padding, visually dominating the experience while still leaving white space above and below. The current order summary is a slim bar anchored to the bottom, taking up minimal vertical space. Within the central column, form controls and buttons extend almost the full column width with consistent horizontal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low content density: a single narrow column with generous vertical spacing between groups (header, eligibility, primary CTA, confirmations, secondary links). Avoid extra marketing content; show only information necessary to understand and redeem the reward. Keep labels short, text blocks compact, and avoid multi-column groupings to preserve clarity and focus.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top header strip (back navigation and account status) straight down into the centered reward title. From there, move down to the eligibility block explaining points and availability, then into the primary redemption controller (the main CTA area). After that, guide the user to the confirmation or error states, followed by the optional 'add to order' action. Finally, allow the gaze to drop to secondary text and the persistent current-order summary at the bottom. The main redemption button should be the strongest blue element and the primary visual anchor in the column.

layout.overall_description: The Reward Detail page appears on a clean white canvas with a thin, full-width top header strip and a single, centered column of content below it. The header strip uses a simple three-part horizontal layout aligned to the main page margins: a subtle back-to-rewards link on the left, a centered account or section label, and compact account status on the right. Beneath this, the main content is vertically centered within the remaining viewport, constrained to a narrow 420–480px column that floats in the middle of the page with ample white space to its left, right, and above. At the top of this column, the reward name and brief description are left-aligned but visually centered within the page’s width. Directly below, eligibility details and the primary redemption controls are stacked with comfortable vertical spacing, with a bold blue redemption button spanning almost the full width of the column. Any confirmation or error panels appear immediately under the relevant control, occupying the same column width but with lighter styling to keep the button dominant. Optional inline authentication appears within the same column when needed, still respecting the linear flow. At the very bottom of the viewport, a slim current-order summary bar stretches edge to edge, lightly separated from the central column, providing context without competing visually with the central CTA. Overall, the page feels modern and minimal, with a calm vertical flow that makes it easy to understand the reward and redeem it in a single glance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell spans the full browser viewport width and height with a solid white background and no decorative framing. It acts as a canvas containing three vertical zones: the thin top header strip (account-header-strip) aligned to the full width, the central content band with a single centered column capped to roughly 420–480px in width, and a slim full-width current-order summary bar at the bottom. Horizontal margins for the centered column align with the internal margins of the header strip so that all core content feels anchored to the same invisible grid. The shell ensures ample vertical white space above and below the central column so that the reward content sits comfortably in the middle third of the viewport on typical laptop screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-viewport container that scrolls naturally; it defines the background and general layout grid but does not itself move or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the central column, the reward-detail-header sits at the top of the main content stack, starting roughly one comfortable vertical spacing step below the bottom edge of the account-header-strip. It is left-aligned within the column, occupying its full width, and includes the reward name as the primary text (larger, bold) with an optional short description or category label beneath in regular weight. The headline block visually occupies about the top 20–25% of the column’s content height. There is generous vertical space below this header before the eligibility-and-actions block, making the reward title the first major focal point once the user’s eye drops below the header strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a normal block at the top of the central column; it scrolls with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reward-eligibility-and-actions panel sits directly beneath the reward-detail-header, separated by a moderate vertical gap. It spans the full width of the central column and is left-aligned, but its internal content remains clean and linear (for example, a bold points-required line on top, and smaller detail lines such as 'You have X points available' or availability conditions below). There is no heavy card framing; at most, a very subtle bounding box or light grey separator line at the top to distinguish it from the header. This panel visually occupies about 15–20% of the total central column height and leads the eye directly into the redemption controller that follows. The bottom padding of this block aligns with the spacing used above the primary button below, reinforcing the vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow panel; it moves with scroll and expands vertically as needed based on text length while maintaining consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reward-redeem-controller is positioned immediately below the eligibility-and-actions panel, with a clear but not oversized vertical gap, making it feel directly connected to the eligibility information. It occupies nearly the full width of the central column—about 90–100%—with equal left and right padding inside the control if it uses a button or input elements. The main redemption button (e.g., 'Redeem for 120 points') is the strongest visual element in this section: a solid brand-blue rectangle with slightly rounded corners, height similar to a comfortable large touch target, and centered horizontally within the column while aligning text to the center of the button. Any small helper text or a single quantity selector appears immediately above or below the button, left-aligned, but kept compact. Vertically, this controller sits roughly in the middle of the column’s content stack and becomes the main focal anchor the eye naturally lands on after reading the eligibility text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal in-flow form section; no sticky behavior. Its height can grow slightly if additional controls are added, but it maintains a single-column, stacked layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reward-redemption-confirmation-panel appears directly beneath the reward-redeem-controller, separated by a small to medium vertical gap, and uses the same central column width. It reads as a slim, unobtrusive confirmation strip or card: a white or very light-tinted background with a thin border or left-accent in the brand blue, an icon on the left (optional), and a single line of confirmation text to the right. The text is left-aligned, and the panel height is compact—roughly the height of one and a half lines of text plus vertical padding—so it does not compete visually with the blue redemption button above. It extends from left to right within the column, maintaining equal horizontal padding as other panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal document flow beneath the redeem controller; it does not overlay other content and pushes subsequent elements downward when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reward-add-to-order-controller is placed below the redemption confirmation panel (or directly below the redeem controller when confirmation is not shown), with a slightly larger vertical gap than between the button and its confirmation, indicating that adding to the pickup order is a follow-up step. It spans the full width of the central column and mirrors the form/button styling pattern: a prominent but visually subordinate button relative to the main redeem button—either using an outlined brand blue style or a lighter-filled variant. The button is centered horizontally within the column and maintains the same width and vertical padding as the primary CTA to preserve rhythm. Any short explanatory text (e.g., 'Add this redeemed drink to your current pickup order') appears as a single line directly above the button, left-aligned and in smaller body text. This section occupies the lower third of the central column’s content stack, leading the eye downwards toward the bottom of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow action controller; scrolls with content and does not stick. Its visibility may be conditional on a successful redemption but its spatial position remains consistent when shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current-order-summary-bar forms a thin, full-width horizontal band anchored at the very bottom edge of the viewport, visually distinct from but aligned with the central column above. It occupies roughly 8–12% of the viewport height or enough height for a single line of summary text and a small secondary button or chevron on the right. Internally, it uses the same left/center/right alignment logic as the header strip: order name or status on the left, a compact total or pickup time in the center or right, and possibly a subtle action affordance on the far right. The background is either a very light grey or a white with a top shadow/border, ensuring it is visible but does not overpower the central column. When content in the main column scrolls, this bar remains visible, providing persistent context about the user’s order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport, remaining fixed as the user scrolls the main content. Internally, its content scrolls only if truncated on very small screens, otherwise it stays static.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth-inline-panel appears in the same central column space where the reward-redeem-controller normally sits, effectively replacing or preceding it when authentication is required. It is constrained to the same 420–480px column width, centered horizontally, and follows the same stacked form layout as the original login mockup: a short title or instruction at the top (e.g., 'Sign in to redeem this reward'), followed by vertically stacked form fields (email and password) with clear labels, then a full-width blue button aligned with the column. Spacing between labels, fields, and the button mirrors the generous, low-density feel of the mockup login form. Below the button, small secondary links like 'Forgot password?' or 'Create account' can appear with reduced vertical spacing and lighter weight. The panel occupies the central vertical band of the page so that the login step feels integrated into the reward flow rather than like a separate page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow form that replaces or is inserted above the redeem controller; it scrolls normally. It does not overlay other content but reorders the vertical flow when present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-header-strip sits at the very top of the page shell, spanning the full width of the viewport as a slim horizontal bar approximately 10–15% of the viewport height. It follows a three-part horizontal layout matching the mockup: the left third aligns with the central column’s left edge and contains navigation elements (including the back-to-rewards-link), the center third holds a concise section title or brand element (e.g., 'Rewards' or the coffee shop logo), and the right third shows compact account/status information such as total points or a user icon. The strip has a white or very light background with minimal chrome—possibly a single bottom border or subtle drop shadow—to separate it from the main content area without adding clutter. Content inside the strip is vertically centered, and there is modest yet comfortable padding above and below text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Can be either static at the top of the document or optionally sticky to the top of the viewport; in both cases it remains a thin, non-intrusive bar that stays visually separate from the central column beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-rewards-link is positioned inside the account-header-strip, aligned to the left edge of the main content grid. It occupies a small horizontal area in the left third of the header, typically rendered as a left-pointing chevron icon followed by a concise 'Back to rewards' label. The text is left-aligned and vertically centered within the header strip, with enough padding around it to remain tap-friendly without feeling heavy. It does not have a background block; it relies on blue text color (or underlined on hover) against the white header to remain visible yet subtle. Its location at the top-left makes it the first actionable element the eye can identify, while still being subordinate to the main reward title in the central column below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header strip; it remains visible as long as the account-header-strip is visible (and sticky if the strip is sticky). It does not scroll independently.
