component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reward-detail-error-state panel appears within the central column, typically directly beneath the reward-detail-header and above the eligibility-and-actions block, so that any issues (e.g., insufficient points, reward unavailable) are seen before the user attempts to redeem. It spans the full width of the central column, with a compact height driven by one or two lines of text. Visually, it uses a light error styling: a white background with a thin red border or a left-side red accent bar, a small error icon on the left, and concise error copy to the right. The text is left-aligned, and vertical padding matches that of the confirmation panel so both share a consistent appearance. The panel maintains generous whitespace above and below to avoid visual clutter, ensuring it is clearly noticeable but does not overpower the primary blue CTA below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal vertical flow, pushing other content down when visible and leaving a clean gap when hidden; no fixed or overlay behavior.

name: Order History
title: Order History
description: Page for signed-in customers to view past ordered items, sort them for easy price scanning, identify the cheapest Large drink among the visible results, and reorder items or open their product details with previous configuration preselected.

core_features: Authenticate access to order history using email/password login or account signup and allow logout from the account context.
Fetch and display a paginated list of past ordered items using getOrderHistoryItems, including item name, size, price, quantity, and order date.
Allow users to change sorting (for example, by price or date) and pagination settings via URL parameters (sortBy, page, pageSize), then reload order history data accordingly.
Within the currently loaded items, automatically scan for and identify the cheapest item whose size is Large, and surface it in a dedicated control for quick review and reorder.
From each item row, provide an "Order Again" action that calls reorderPastItem to add the item to the current order and then navigates to order.html with the new order item highlighted.
From each item row, provide a "View Details" navigation link to product.html, passing both the productId and the orderItemId so the product can be shown with the previous configuration preselected.
Show a compact current-order summary (if any) using getCurrentOrderSummary so users can see the number of items and totals while browsing history.
Support logout using logoutCurrentAccount and return the page to an unauthenticated state where the user can log in or sign up again.
user_workflows: View and scan order history items: 1) User navigates to account_orders.html optionally with sortBy, page, and pageSize URL parameters; 2) On load, the page checks whether the user is authenticated based on backend responses and, if permitted, calls getOrderHistoryItems using the current URL parameters; 3) The list of items returned in items[] is rendered with key fields such as orderDate, productName, size, milkType, unitPrice, quantity, and totalPrice; 4) The user visually scans the list, optionally using the sort controls to make prices easier to compare; 5) Pagination controls allow moving to other pages, updating the page URL parameters and triggering new getOrderHistoryItems calls.

Change sorting and pagination using URL state: 1) The user interacts with a sort control (for example, choosing to sort by price ascending); 2) The page updates the sortBy URL parameter while preserving pageSize and resetting page to the initial page, then reloads or refetches data using getOrderHistoryItems with the new parameters; 3) The user can change pageSize (items per page), which updates pageSize in the URL, resets page, and triggers another getOrderHistoryItems call; 4) Clicking pagination controls (next, previous, specific page number) updates the page URL parameter and refetches data; 5) The visible list and the cheapest Large drink indicator are recalculated from the newly loaded items.

Identify and act on the cheapest Large drink: 1) After getOrderHistoryItems returns items for the current sort/page, the page filters those items in-memory to those whose size field indicates a Large size; 2) Among those filtered items, the page finds the one with the lowest unitPrice; 3) If such an item exists, its key attributes (productName, size, unitPrice, and orderDate) are surfaced in a small summary component; 4) The component offers a dedicated "Order Again" control for that item, which triggers the same reorderPastItem flow as other rows; 5) If no Large-sized item is present on the current page, the component clearly indicates that no Large items are available in the current results, prompting the user to navigate to other pages or adjust sorting.

Reorder an item directly from history: 1) The user finds a desired past item in the history list; 2) The user clicks the item's "Order Again" button; 3) The page calls reorderPastItem with the selected orderItemId; 4) If reorderPastItem returns success, the response includes a currentOrderId, the newly created orderItem, and orderSummary; 5) The page uses the returned orderItem.id as highlightItemId in a navigation to order.html, constructing a URL such as order.html?highlightItemId=<new item id>; 6) The order.html page can then emphasize that item in the current order; 7) If the reorder API call fails, the page displays an error message and does not navigate away.

Open product details with previous configuration: 1) The user selects an item in the history list and clicks its "View Details" link; 2) Without calling additional APIs, the page navigates to product.html while appending the productId and the orderItemId from the row as URL parameters (e.g., product.html?id=<productId>&orderItemId=<orderItemId>); 3) The product.html page can use orderItemId to pre-populate size and options with the historical configuration before the user adds it to the current order.

Use current order summary while browsing history: 1) When the page loads for an authenticated user, it calls getCurrentOrderSummary; 2) If hasActiveOrder is true, the page displays orderId, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, and pickupStore/pickupDateTime in a compact widget; 3) While the user browses order history, this widget remains visible to indicate current order progress; 4) If the user reorders an item but the design keeps them on this page (for example, in a non-navigating variant), the page can, after a successful reorderPastItem call, either use the returned orderSummary or call getCurrentOrderSummary again to refresh the widget; 5) If hasActiveOrder is false, a simplified message indicates there is no active order.

Authenticate, sign up, and log out on the same page: 1) If getOrderHistoryItems or getCurrentOrderSummary responds in a way indicating the user is not authenticated, or if the page is opened without a known session, the page shows a login/signup component instead of history data; 2) To log in, the user submits an email and password, which triggers loginWithEmailPassword; 3) On a successful login response with isLoggedIn true, the page hides the auth form and calls getOrderHistoryItems and getCurrentOrderSummary using the current URL parameters; 4) To create an account from this page, the user fills in fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms, then the page calls signupAccount; 5) On successful signup, the page treats the user as authenticated, hides the auth form, and proceeds to load order history and the current-order summary; 6) The user can log out at any time via a logout control that calls logoutCurrentAccount; 7) On successful logout, the page clears any in-memory account context and returns to the login/signup state, removing any sensitive history data from view.
interactions: Login form interactions: Users enter email and password and submit. onSubmitLogin calls loginWithEmailPassword; on success, the component emits an onAuthSuccess event to the page, which then triggers getOrderHistoryItems and getCurrentOrderSummary. On failure, the error message from the API is displayed inline without altering URL parameters.

Signup form interactions: Users enter fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms. onSubmitSignup calls signupAccount; on success, the page assumes the user is authenticated, hides auth forms, and loads order history via getOrderHistoryItems. Any validation or error messages from the API are presented inline; URL parameters remain unchanged so the user’s intended sortBy/page/pageSize context is preserved after signup.

Logout interactions: When the user clicks a logout button in the header, onClickLogout calls logoutCurrentAccount. On successful response, the page clears any account-specific in-memory state (such as cached order history items), stops rendering order history components, and shows the login/signup component. Optionally, it may also remove or reset URL parameters that only apply to authenticated views.

Sort option interactions: A sort control (e.g., dropdown or segmented control) is bound to the sortBy URL parameter. When the user chooses a new sort option, onChangeSort updates the browser URL to reflect the new sortBy value and resets page to an initial page index while leaving pageSize unchanged. After the URL is updated, getOrderHistoryItems is called with the new sortBy/page/pageSize, and the results refresh the list and the cheapest Large drink indicator.

Pagination interactions: Pagination controls are bound to the page URL parameter. Clicking "next", "previous", or a specific page number triggers onChangePage which updates page in the URL and refetches data via getOrderHistoryItems. The component uses totalItems, page, and pageSize from the API result to compute the available pages and to disable navigation beyond the first/last page.

Page size interactions: A page size selector is bound to the pageSize URL parameter. When the user selects a new pageSize, onChangePageSize updates pageSize in the URL and resets page to an initial page index. The page then calls getOrderHistoryItems using the updated parameters; the list refreshes with the new number of items per page.

Order Again interactions from a list row: Each item row includes an "Order Again" button. When clicked, onClickOrderAgain reads the orderItemId from that row, calls reorderPastItem with that orderItemId, and waits for the response. If the response indicates success, the handler reads the returned orderItem.id and navigates to order.html with highlightItemId set to that id. Errors are surfaced as inline messages or alerts, and no navigation occurs if the call fails.

Order Again interactions from the cheapest Large drink indicator: The cheapest Large drink component, when it identifies a qualifying orderItemId, exposes a dedicated "Order Again" or equivalent control. onClickOrderCheapestLarge calls reorderPastItem with that orderItemId. On success, the same navigation to order.html?highlightItemId=<id> occurs, sharing the same handler logic as row-level reorder actions.

View Details interactions: Each item row includes a "View Details" link. When activated, onClickViewDetails reads productId and orderItemId from the row and performs a navigation to product.html with URL parameters id=<productId> and orderItemId=<orderItemId>. No additional interfaces are called during this interaction; product.html is responsible for any further data retrieval.

Cheapest Large drink scanning interactions: After each successful getOrderHistoryItems call, onItemsLoaded in the cheapest Large drink component runs a client-side computation over items[] to filter those with size indicating Large, then finds the minimum unitPrice among them. The component then updates its displayed state: either showing the identified item and its actions or indicating that no Large items exist in the current result set. This computation is repeated automatically whenever sortBy, page, or pageSize changes and new data are loaded.

Current order summary interactions: On page load for authenticated users, onLoadSummary triggers getCurrentOrderSummary. If hasActiveOrder is true, the widget displays a non-interactive summary of itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, and pickupStore/pickupDateTime. If the design keeps the user on this page after reorders (for example, in a variant where navigation to order.html is not immediate), an onOrderUpdated event can trigger a refresh by calling getCurrentOrderSummary again. If hasActiveOrder is false, the widget shows an appropriate empty-state message.

Error and empty state interactions: If getOrderHistoryItems returns an empty items array, the list shows an empty-state message while keeping sort and pagination controls available. If getOrderHistoryItems returns an error (such as due to an expired session), the page can show an error message and prompt the user to log in again, revealing the auth components. All such states rely solely on API responses and URL parameters, without hardcoded placeholder data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s main state is controlled through URL parameters and API responses. On every load, the page reads sortBy, page, and pageSize from the current URL query string. If any of these are missing, implementation-time defaults may be applied before calling getOrderHistoryItems; these defaults are not hardcoded in the design and are determined by the back-end and configuration. The page then calls getOrderHistoryItems with the effective values of sortBy, page, and pageSize, and renders order history items from the returned items array. The page and pageSize values from the response are treated as the authoritative values for the pagination component, along with totalItems, which determines how many pages exist and whether next/previous links are enabled. Changes to any of the following controls—sort selection, page selection, or page size—update the corresponding URL parameters and then trigger a new getOrderHistoryItems call. This means the page URL always reflects the current view state; reloading the browser or sharing the URL reproduces the same list, sort order, and pagination. The cheapest Large drink indicator does not introduce new URL state; it is purely derived from the current getOrderHistoryItems.items array on each load or refresh. Authentication state is not stored in the URL; instead, it is managed by the back-end session established via loginWithEmailPassword or signupAccount, and cleared via logoutCurrentAccount. The getCurrentOrderSummary result is transient and reflects the current order at the moment of each call; it is not persisted beyond the current page lifetime. No state is persisted via localStorage or other browser storage: repeatable views are reconstructed solely from URL parameters and fresh interface calls.

components.functionality: Controls whether the user sees authentication forms or the order history content. When the user is not authenticated or when the back end indicates that access to order history is not allowed, this component shows login and signup forms, uses the loginWithEmailPassword and signupAccount interfaces, and, upon successful authentication, hides itself and triggers loading of order history and current order summary.

Displays basic account context (such as the user’s name and email from the authentication response) and provides a logout control. Also provides a container for the current order summary widget. When logout is requested, it calls logoutCurrentAccount and, upon success, signals the page to reset to an unauthenticated state and clear any order history data from view.

Shows a compact summary of the current pickup order if one exists. On page load after successful authentication, it calls getCurrentOrderSummary. When hasActiveOrder is true, it renders orderId, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore.name, and pickupDateTime. It can also be refreshed in response to reorder events if the user remains on this page after adding items.

Manages sort, pagination, and page size controls that directly manipulate URL parameters and drive getOrderHistoryItems calls. It reads current sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL on initialization and exposes UI controls to change them. Whenever the user changes any of these, it updates the URL accordingly and asks the page to reload order history items.
data_binding: OrderItem (indirectly, through getOrderHistoryItems metadata: page, pageSize, totalItems)

components.functionality: Displays the list of past ordered items returned by getOrderHistoryItems for the current sortBy, page, and pageSize. Each row shows orderDate, productName, size, milkType, unitPrice, quantity, totalPrice, and potentially the orderId. Each row provides an "Order Again" button that calls reorderPastItem and then navigates to order.html with highlightItemId set to the newly created order item id, plus a "View Details" link that navigates to product.html with id (productId) and orderItemId as URL parameters.

Analyzes the currently loaded order history items to identify the cheapest Large-sized drink within the current result set. After each getOrderHistoryItems response, it filters items for those whose size indicates Large, then finds the item with the lowest unitPrice. If found, it displays that item’s key information and provides a dedicated "Order Again" action that calls reorderPastItem for that specific orderItemId and then navigates to order.html with highlightItemId equal to the new order item id. If no Large item exists in the current page, it displays a suitable message indicating that no Large items are available in the current results.
data_binding: OrderItem (from getOrderHistoryItems.items, used for in-memory filtering by size and price)

components.functionality: Provides next/previous and page-number navigation based on page, pageSize, and totalItems from getOrderHistoryItems. It updates the page URL parameter when the user navigates, then triggers a new getOrderHistoryItems call through the parent page logic. It also disables navigation where there are no further pages.

Displays an empty-state message when getOrderHistoryItems returns an empty items array and a suitable error message when the interface call fails. It listens to the success or failure of the getOrderHistoryItems call and renders accordingly, without relying on any hardcoded sample data.
data_binding: OrderItem (items array length to detect emptiness)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same strict, top-to-bottom, single-column flow as the login mockup. A thin full-width header bar sits at the very top. Below it, a vertically centered main content column (max-width around 420–480px) is horizontally centered in the viewport. Inside this column, components are stacked in a linear order: first the order-history-controls (serving the role of page title + controls), then the current-order-summary-widget, then the cheapest-large-drink-indicator, followed by the order-history-list, and finally the order-history-pagination. The account-auth-gate conceptually wraps this central column and can replace its content with an auth prompt if needed. The order-history-empty-or-error-state occupies the same vertical slot as the list + pagination when there is no data or an error.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same strict, top-to-bottom, single-column flow as the login mockup. A thin full-width header bar sits at the very top. Below it, a vertically centered main content column (max-width around 420–480px) is horizontally centered in the viewport. Inside this column, components are stacked in a linear order: first the order-history-controls (serving the role of page title + controls), then the current-order-summary-widget, then the cheapest-large-drink-indicator, followed by the order-history-list, and finally the order-history-pagination. The account-auth-gate conceptually wraps this central column and can replace its content with an auth prompt if needed. The order-history-empty-or-error-state occupies the same vertical slot as the list + pagination when there is no data or an error.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar uses a shallow vertical slice at the top (~10–15% of viewport height or a fixed 56–64px). The remaining vertical space is dominated by the central content column, which has generous top and bottom padding to create a floating, focused band of content in the middle of the viewport. The main column width is constrained to roughly 420–480px and centered, with all interactive elements (controls, summary widget, list items, pagination) spanning nearly the full width of this column while maintaining consistent inner padding. Lower-priority elements like the cheapest-large-drink-indicator text and pagination occupy minimal vertical space with tighter spacing beneath primary elements, mirroring the secondary links beneath the login button in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, breathable layout. Leave ample white space above the first central component (order-history-controls) and below the last (pagination or empty/error state). Between stacked components, use consistent, comfortable vertical gaps (roughly equivalent to 1–2 line heights). Within each component, inputs, buttons, and list items have generous padding and clear separation, avoiding dense tables or compact multi-column grids. The list shows a limited amount of information per item, with clear hierarchy and spacing, so that scanning prices and actions feels calm and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the mockup’s vertical reading path. The eye first catches the header bar at the top, then quickly moves to the centered main column. The first focal point in that column is the order-history-controls, which functions like the 'Welcome!' block (page title plus key controls). From there, the eye moves downward to the current-order-summary-widget (recent/active order context), then to the small but informative cheapest-large-drink-indicator (computed highlight), then into the vertically stacked order-history-list (the core content). After reading through the list, attention naturally moves to the pagination controls at the bottom. In empty or error states, the list area is replaced by a focused status panel, keeping the vertical flow intact. When unauthenticated, the account-auth-gate foregrounds a login-like message in the same central column, keeping the same flow but simplifying the content.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, modern, and calm experience dominated by a narrow, centered column of content on a white background. A slim header bar runs full-width across the top with brand blue accents, while the rest of the viewport is mostly open white space. In the middle of the screen, a single column about 420–480px wide is vertically padded so it feels like a floating strip of content. At the top of this column, the order history controls and heading set the context, followed by a compact summary of the current or most recent order. Just beneath that, a concise line highlights the cheapest Large drink among the visible orders, before the eye drops into a clean vertical list of past orders, each occupying the full column width. At the bottom of the column, compact pagination controls anchor the list. When there are no orders or an error, a simple centered status panel replaces the list area, preserving the same overall structure. The entire composition feels light and intentional, with every element stacked in a clear top-to-bottom order and surrounded by consistent white space, mirroring the disciplined, minimalist login mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-auth-gate conceptually wraps the entire main content area beneath the header bar. It does not appear as a visible element when the user is authenticated; instead, it simply allows the centered content column (controls, summary widget, list, etc.) to render normally. When the user is not authenticated, the gate replaces the entire central content column with a single, vertically centered message block that follows the same width and alignment as the login mockup: a narrow column (max-width ~420–480px), centered horizontally, with generous white space above and below. This gated view aligns itself just below the header bar, so the top edge of the message block starts roughly in the upper-middle of the viewport, similar to the login form position. The message and any action buttons are stacked vertically and occupy almost the full width of the central column, with comfortable padding and spacing, preserving the same clean, minimalist look.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally overlays or replaces the main central content area beneath the header bar. When active (unauthenticated), it effectively becomes the primary visible content within the central column. When inactive (authenticated), it takes no visible space and allows the rest of the page to render as if it were not present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-header-bar sits anchored at the very top of the page, spanning the full width of the viewport from left to right. It is a thin horizontal strip approximately 10–15% of the viewport height (or a fixed height in the 56–64px range), with a white or very light background and a subtle bottom border or shadow to separate it from the main content. Inside this bar, content is aligned following a three-part horizontal layout that matches the mockup: a logo or brand mark on the left edge aligned to the page margin, contextual navigation or page name in the center, and account-related actions (profile, sign out) on the right, all aligned to the same overall side margins as the central column. The header bar remains visually light, with flat elements and minimal decoration. Below the bar, there is a noticeable vertical gap before the start of the central column, reinforcing its separation as a distinct, but not bulky, top section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document (non-sticky by default), occupying a fixed height and always spanning the full viewport width. As the user scrolls, it scrolls out of view together with the content below unless a sticky behavior is later applied at implementation time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the central column, the current-order-summary-widget appears near the top, directly beneath the order-history-controls. It spans nearly the full width of the centered column (100% of the column width with inner horizontal padding), giving it the appearance of a compact card or panel. The widget’s top edge starts roughly 16–24px below the bottom of the controls, preserving the generous but consistent vertical spacing from the mockup. The card has subtly rounded corners and a light border or soft shadow, but remains flat and minimal. Content inside the widget (e.g., most recent order date, total price, key items, and a quick re-order button) is arranged in a simple vertical stack or minimal two-row layout, but not in a dense multi-column table. The overall height of the widget is moderate—it feels important but not overwhelming—and there is a similar vertical gap (16–24px) below it before the cheapest-large-drink-indicator line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the central column content; it scrolls naturally with the page. It does not fix or float, and it always maintains its position between the order-history-controls and the cheapest-large-drink-indicator.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-history-controls component is the first element in the main central column beneath the header bar, occupying the role that the 'Welcome!' block plays in the login mockup. It is horizontally centered with a fixed max-width of about 420–480px and spans the full width of that column. It starts a generous distance below the bottom of the header bar, roughly 48–72px, so it appears in the upper-middle of the viewport rather than hard against the top. Within this component, a primary page title (e.g., 'Order History') is left-aligned at the top of the column, with a short explanatory line or subtext optionally beneath it. Below the title and subtext, filter and sort controls (such as a dropdown for sorting by price, date, or size, and possibly a simple search input) are arranged in a minimal vertical stack or a single horizontal row that still respects the single-column feel—controls align to the left edge and do not extend beyond the column width. Vertical spacing between the title, any helper text, and the controls follows the same comfortable spacing rhythm as labels and inputs in the login form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing block-level element in the central column, anchoring the top of the main content. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-history-list sits directly below the cheapest-large-drink-indicator and forms the main vertical body of the page. It fully occupies the width of the central column (up to the ~420–480px max-width), with a modest vertical gap (16–24px) above it. The list is composed of stacked order entries, each rendered as a flat, card-like row with subtle rounding and consistent internal padding. Each item aligns its content to the left, with key information (date, items, Large drink details, total price) arranged in a concise vertical or lightly structured horizontal pattern but always within the same single-column constraint. Between list items, there is a small but clear vertical separation (8–16px), preventing them from visually blending together while keeping the column continuous. The list can extend vertically and scroll as needed, effectively occupying the bulk of the central column’s height. Primary actions per order (such as 'Reorder' or 'View Details') are placed consistently near the bottom or right edge of each item but kept visually simple and flat, similar to the single prominent CTA in the login form but scaled down in impact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A scrollable part of the main document flow. It expands vertically as many items as needed, pushing the pagination controls downward. It always remains centered horizontally within the constrained column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-history-pagination component is positioned directly beneath the last visible item in the order-history-list, separated by a modest vertical gap (16–24px) that echoes the distance between the sign-in button and the secondary links in the login mockup. It is centered within the same narrow column and typically occupies only a small vertical slice—just enough for a row of page numbers and previous/next controls. The pagination elements themselves (page numbers, arrows) form a single horizontal row, but the entire row is visually anchored to the center of the column. The controls are low-profile and flat, using the brand blue sparingly to highlight the current page or hover states. Additional white space below the pagination (at least 32–48px) reinforces that this is the end of the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows directly after the order-history-list within the central column. It scrolls with the content and never fixes to the bottom of the screen; instead, it marks the natural end of the list section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cheapest-large-drink-indicator appears between the current-order-summary-widget and the order-history-list, occupying a similar structural position to the small secondary text links beneath the main button in the login mockup. It is left-aligned within the central column and takes up the full column width but only a small vertical space—essentially a single line or two of text with perhaps a highlighted price. Its top edge begins about 12–16px below the bottom of the summary widget, keeping it visually close but clearly separated. The tone is informative and subtle: something like 'Cheapest Large drink in these results: $X.XX' with the price emphasized in brand blue. Because it is inline and text-based, it does not introduce a heavy card; instead, it reads as a small, helpful annotation before the list. Below it, another modest gap (12–16px) leads into the first item of the order-history-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, flowing text block that scrolls naturally with the column. It remains anchored between the summary widget and the list, without any sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-history-empty-or-error-state occupies the same central column region where the order-history-list and pagination would normally appear. When active (no orders or an error), it replaces those components entirely, keeping the overall vertical structure intact. It begins at the same vertical position where the list would, directly beneath the cheapest-large-drink-indicator (or directly beneath the current-order-summary-widget if the indicator is suppressed in empty states), with a comfortable gap of 16–24px. The panel spans the full width of the column, styled as a simple, flat card with subtle rounding and plenty of internal padding. Inside, a concise headline (e.g., 'No orders yet') and one or two short lines of explanatory text are stacked vertically, centered within the column but left-aligned text for consistency. Optional actions (like a 'Start your first order' button) appear below the text, aligned to the left and taking up most of the width of the column, reminiscent of the primary CTA button in the login screen but slightly less dominant. There is ample white space above and below this panel, emphasizing its status-like nature instead of dense content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed block that replaces the order-history-list and order-history-pagination in the normal flow. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay other components; instead, it occupies their space when they are not present.

name: Notification Preferences
title: Notification Preferences
description: Page where signed-in customers view and adjust their email, push, and SMS notification settings, including a shortcut to enable SMS-only order updates and saving all changes for future communications.

core_features: Authenticate the user (if needed) using email and password before allowing access to notification settings via the loginWithEmailPassword interface.
Load the current notification preferences (email, push, SMS) using getNotificationPreferences and bind them to editable controls.
Allow granular control over email notifications, so users can independently toggle promotional emails and order-related emails.
Allow users to disable all push notification categories with a single control while still permitting granular toggles for each push category.
Allow users to configure SMS preferences, including enabling SMS only for order updates while disabling other channels with a single shortcut action.
Persist all notification preference changes using updateNotificationPreferences, with clear feedback on success or failure.
Display a compact current-order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary to give context about why order-related notifications matter.
Support navigation back to the account dashboard (account_dashboard.html) with URL parameters that indicate the source page and whether preference changes were saved.
Allow the user to log out from this page using logoutCurrentAccount, clearing session context before redirecting away.
Offer access to account creation via signupAccount from this page when accessed directly by unauthenticated users.
user_workflows: Access notification settings from account dashboard: (1) User clicks a "Notification Preferences" or "Settings" link on account_dashboard.html that navigates to account_notifications.html?from=dashboard. (2) The page checks if the user is authenticated; if not, it displays a login form. (3) Once authenticated, the page requests current preferences via getNotificationPreferences. (4) The loaded preferences populate the notification controls for email, push, and SMS.

Login on this page before editing preferences: (1) User lands on account_notifications.html without a valid session or with URL parameter account_notifications.html?mode=login. (2) The page displays the login form component bound to loginWithEmailPassword. (3) User submits their email and password. (4) On success, the page updates state to isLoggedIn and reloads the notification view (e.g., navigating to account_notifications.html?from=login or updating internal state). (5) The notification preferences loader calls getNotificationPreferences and fills the form.

Create an account from this page: (1) An unauthenticated user on account_notifications.html sees a link or tab for account creation. (2) User switches to the sign-up form component. (3) User submits full name, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms. (4) The signupAccount interface is called. (5) On success, the page transitions to the notification preferences view and triggers getNotificationPreferences for the new account; URL may switch to account_notifications.html?from=signup.

Update email notification categories: (1) After preferences are loaded via getNotificationPreferences, the email section displays separate controls for promotional emails and order update emails. (2) User adjusts one or both toggles according to their preferences. (3) The form component updates its internal preferences object in real time as each control is changed. (4) User clicks the Save button. (5) The form composes a preferences payload based on the current state of all toggles and sends it to updateNotificationPreferences. (6) On success, the page may update the URL to account_notifications.html?status=saved&channel=email and optionally show a confirmation message.

Turn off all push notifications: (1) After preferences load, the push section shows granular toggles plus a master control for disabling all push notifications. (2) User activates "Turn off all push". (3) The component updates its local preferences object so that all push-related settings reflect the master choice. (4) User reviews the changes and clicks Save. (5) The form calls updateNotificationPreferences with the updated preferences object. (6) On success, user sees confirmation and may navigate back to the account dashboard via a link to account_dashboard.html?from=notifications&status=saved_push.

Configure SMS-only order updates: (1) User navigates to account_notifications.html?focus=sms or scrolls to the SMS section. (2) User clicks an action such as "Use SMS only for order updates". (3) The component updates its preferences object so that SMS order updates are enabled, SMS promotions are disabled, and other channels (email and push for order updates and promotions) are disabled according to the SMS-only behavior. (4) User clicks Save; updateNotificationPreferences is called with the resulting preferences object. (5) On success, the page reflects the new settings, may update the URL to account_notifications.html?status=saved&mode=sms_only, and offers navigation back to account_dashboard.html.

Save preferences and return to account overview: (1) User makes any combination of changes to email, push, and SMS settings. (2) User clicks the Save and Return button. (3) The form calls updateNotificationPreferences. (4) If the update succeeds, the page navigates to account_dashboard.html?from=notifications&status=saved. (5) If the update fails, the page remains on account_notifications.html, updates the URL to account_notifications.html?status=error and displays error messaging without losing the current form values.

View current order summary: (1) When the page loads in authenticated state, the order summary widget calls getCurrentOrderSummary. (2) If the response indicates an active order, the widget shows the order count, monetary summary, and pickup store information. (3) User can click the widget to navigate to an order-related page using a URL that includes orderId as a parameter (e.g., an order details page) without affecting notification preferences. (4) If there is no active order, the widget hides or shows a minimal placeholder state.

Log out from notification preferences: (1) User clicks a Logout control in the header or account navigation component. (2) The logoutCurrentAccount interface is called. (3) On success, page state is updated to reflect a logged-out user. (4) The user is redirected to an appropriate page such as a login or landing page using URL parameters like account_notifications.html?mode=login or another predefined route. (5) Notification preference components are hidden until the user logs back in.
interactions: Toggling individual notification channels: User clicks or taps on controls for each preference (e.g., email promotions, email order updates, push promotions, push order updates, SMS order updates, SMS promotions). Each change updates the in-memory preferences object bound to the form component.

Using a master switch for push notifications: User interacts with a "Turn off all push" control. When activated, it sets all push-related fields in the preferences object to a disabled state; when deactivated, it allows individual toggles to control each push category separately.

Triggering SMS-only configuration: User clicks a dedicated control to apply "SMS-only for order updates" behavior. This interaction updates the preferences object to enable SMS order updates, disable SMS promotions, and disable or adjust email/push order and promotional notifications to achieve SMS-only behavior for order updates.

Submitting the notification preferences form: User clicks Save or Save and Return. The form validates its local preferences object (e.g., ensures a phone number exists if SMS options are enabled, using only available data) and then calls updateNotificationPreferences with that object. UI state shows loading and then success or error states based on the response.

Navigating back to account dashboard: After saving or at any time, user clicks a "Back to Account" or "Account Overview" link. This navigation sends the user to account_dashboard.html with URL parameters that can include from=notifications and a status flag that indicates whether preferences were just saved.

Viewing and interacting with current order summary: On page load, the order summary widget calls getCurrentOrderSummary. If an active order exists, clicking the summary navigates to a dedicated order page (e.g., order_details.html?orderId=...) without modifying notification preferences.

Logging in via embedded form: When not authenticated, the login form captures email and password and on submit calls loginWithEmailPassword. On success, it replaces the login view with the notification preferences view and may update the URL to remove mode=login or change it to mode=edit.

Signing up via embedded form: From the same page, a user can switch to a sign-up view that uses signupAccount. Submission returns accountDashboard context; upon success, the page transitions into authenticated state and triggers loading of notification preferences.

Logging out: User clicks a logout control which calls logoutCurrentAccount. On success, UI clears authenticated-only components (preferences form, order summary, account nav) and sets or preserves URL parameters such as mode=login to show the login form again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters to manage navigation and UI states rather than localStorage. Key parameters include: (1) from: indicates the source page (e.g., from=dashboard or from=login). When coming from account_dashboard.html, setting from=dashboard lets the "Back to Account" link know where to send the user. (2) status: reflects the result of the last operation, such as status=saved or status=error. After a successful updateNotificationPreferences call, the page can navigate to account_notifications.html?status=saved or account_dashboard.html?from=notifications&status=saved. On error, the page remains at account_notifications.html?status=error and maintains form state in memory. (3) mode: controls which major view to show. mode=edit displays the preferences form when authenticated; mode=login forces the login view; mode=sms_only can pre-select the SMS-only configuration when loading preferences by applying the SMS-only transformation to the preferences object before displaying it. (4) focus: determines which section should be brought into view or emphasized on load, such as focus=email, focus=push, or focus=sms. This helps deep-linking from other pages (e.g., a link from order confirmation may navigate to account_notifications.html?focus=sms to highlight SMS order updates). (5) returnTo: a URL-encoded target used for post-save navigation, such as returnTo=account_dashboard.html%3Fsection%3Doverview. If present, the Save and Return button will redirect to this target after a successful updateNotificationPreferences call. (6) orderContext: if present (e.g., orderContext=current), the page may prioritize showing the order summary widget and scroll to it after loading getCurrentOrderSummary. All component states (e.g., which section is open, which view is visible) are controlled by these URL parameters and in-memory state derived from API responses; no notification preference values are persisted on the client outside these mechanisms.

components.functionality: Determines whether the user is authenticated before exposing notification preferences. If the user is not authenticated, shows the login and optional signup components and hides settings. It also interprets URL parameters like mode=login to force the login view.

Provides fields for email and password, and on submission calls loginWithEmailPassword. On successful login, it informs the auth-gate and triggers loading of notification preferences, optionally updating the URL (e.g., removing mode=login). On failure, it displays an error state but keeps the user inputs.

Allows new users to create an account directly from this page by submitting fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and terms acceptance, calling signupAccount. On success, transitions the page to authenticated state and initiates loading of notification preferences.

Fetches the current NotificationPreferences for the authenticated user by calling getNotificationPreferences when the page enters authenticated edit mode. It manages loading and error states and passes the retrieved preferences object to the notification-preferences-form component.

Displays and edits granular notification settings for email, push, and SMS using the preferences provided by preferences-loader. It maintains an in-memory preferences object that mirrors the NotificationPreferences model. On Save or Save and Return, it calls updateNotificationPreferences with the current preferences object and handles success or error states, optionally updating the URL or navigating based on returnTo and status parameters.

Nested within the notification-preferences-form, this section exposes separate controls to enable or disable emailPromotionsEnabled and emailOrderUpdatesEnabled. Any change updates the shared preferences object that will be submitted via updateNotificationPreferences.

Manages push notification settings, including pushPromotionsEnabled and pushOrderUpdatesEnabled. Also includes a master control that turns off all push notifications at once. The master control updates the underlying preferences object to reflect the all-on or all-off state for push categories.

Controls SMS settings related to smsOrderUpdatesEnabled and smsPromotionsEnabled. It validates that a phoneNumber exists on the AccountProfile when SMS options are enabled (using only available AccountProfile data) and updates the shared preferences object accordingly.

Provides a one-click action to configure SMS-only order updates. When activated, it modifies the in-memory preferences object so that SMS order updates are enabled, SMS promotions are disabled, and other channels for order updates and promotions are adjusted to reflect an SMS-only strategy for order updates. The component does not persist these changes until the user submits the main form.

Shows success or error messages based on the outcome of updateNotificationPreferences and URL parameters such as status=saved or status=error. It reads the current URL state and form submission results to display appropriate feedback without storing any static messages about specific data.

Calls getCurrentOrderSummary on load to display a compact view showing whether there is an active order and, if so, a summary of its items and pickup information. Clicking the widget navigates to an order-related page with the orderId passed as a URL parameter. It does not modify notification preferences.

Provides navigation links relevant to the account context, including a "Back to Account" or "Account Overview" link that navigates to account_dashboard.html with URL parameters derived from the current page (e.g., from=notifications, status=current status, focus section). It also includes access points for logout and possibly other account sections.

Triggers logoutCurrentAccount when clicked. On success, it clears authenticated-only UI, may update the URL to mode=login, and delegates to auth-gate to show the login or signup forms instead of the notification settings.

Parses URL parameters (from, status, mode, focus, returnTo, orderContext) on load and whenever the URL changes, and then updates page-level state and component props accordingly. It ensures that the correct section is focused, the correct view (login vs edit) is shown, and that After-save navigation uses returnTo where applicable.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain a single, centered content column constrained to ~420–480px, aligned to the same horizontal margins as a thin full-width top header bar. Vertically stack elements in a strict top-to-bottom order: global header (account-navigation-bar with logout-control), then the main page title and short description, then the preferences-status-banner (when present), followed by the notification-preferences-form. Inside the form, stack sections linearly: email-preferences-section, push-preferences-section, sms-preferences-section (containing the sms-only-order-updates-control), then a full-width primary "Save" button and any small secondary text. Auth states reuse this same central column, swapping in login-form or signup-form in place of the preferences form under the same header.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain a single, centered content column constrained to ~420–480px, aligned to the same horizontal margins as a thin full-width top header bar. Vertically stack elements in a strict top-to-bottom order: global header (account-navigation-bar with logout-control), then the main page title and short description, then the preferences-status-banner (when present), followed by the notification-preferences-form. Inside the form, stack sections linearly: email-preferences-section, push-preferences-section, sms-preferences-section (containing the sms-only-order-updates-control), then a full-width primary "Save" button and any small secondary text. Auth states reuse this same central column, swapping in login-form or signup-form in place of the preferences form under the same header.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate a shallow vertical slice at the top (~10–15% of viewport height) for the account-navigation-bar that spans full width but keeps its content constrained to the same centered max-width as the main column. Reserve the middle band of the viewport for the central content column with generous top and bottom padding so that the notification-preferences-form visually dominates while still floating in white space. Form fields, toggle groups, and buttons use nearly the full width of this central column (about 90–100% of the column), with consistent internal padding. Lower-priority items like helper text, the order-summary-widget, and inline links sit under the main actions with tighter vertical spacing and compact sizing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the page low-density and uncluttered: only core account controls, no imagery or promotional content. Maintain comfortable vertical spacing between all stacked elements (roughly 16–24px between related items and 24–32px between major blocks). Group related toggles within each section with slightly tighter spacing to visually bind them, but preserve clear separation between sections. Use white space rather than lines or decoration to create hierarchy, with only occasional subtle dividers where necessary.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve a strong vertical, center-aligned visual flow. The eye moves from the logo/nav and logout in the slim top bar down to the central page heading in the main column, then to the status banner (if present), then sequentially through Email, Push, and SMS sections, and finally to the large full-width primary "Save Preferences" button, which is the main visual anchor. Beneath this button, the eye naturally drops to any smaller secondary text or widgets like the order-summary-widget. When unauthenticated, the same flow applies but with the login or signup form replacing the notification-preferences-form, mirroring the original login mockup order of heading → brief instruction → fields → primary button → secondary links.

layout.overall_description: The page is a clean, minimalist account screen framed by a thin, full-width header bar at the very top and a narrow, centered content column floating in ample white space beneath it. The header bar holds the coffee shop logo and a subtle back/account link on the left, a small, centered account navigation label indicating "Notification Preferences", and a discreet logout button aligned to the right, all constrained within a max-width column so the edges align perfectly with the main content below. Beneath this, in the middle of the viewport, a single vertical column (~420–480px wide) is centered horizontally and padded generously from the top header. At its top is the page title "Notification Preferences" with a short explanatory line, followed by a slim status banner when there are unsaved changes or recent updates. Below, the notification preferences form stacks three clearly separated sections for Email, Push, and SMS notifications, each spanning the width of the column with left-aligned labels and right-aligned toggles. Within the SMS section, a prominent row offers a quick "SMS-only order updates" preset. At the bottom of the form, a large blue full-width "Save" button anchors the layout, with any small supporting text and an optional, compact order summary widget placed beneath it using tighter spacing. On unauthenticated visits, the central column switches to a login or signup form while keeping the same overall spacing, alignment, and button treatment, so the page still feels like the original login mockup but dedicated to notification settings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth-gate is a non-visual wrapper that conceptually sits around the entire central content column beneath the top header. It does not occupy its own visible space, but determines whether the login-form/signup-form or the notification-preferences-form stack is rendered in the main column area. Spatially, it encloses everything from just below the account-navigation-bar down to the bottom of the page, managing which set of components appear in that linear vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible controller; it does not render any visible UI and does not affect spacing directly, only toggles which child layout state is active within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user is not authenticated and the login view is active, the login-form occupies the central content column directly beneath the account-navigation-bar, mirroring the original mockup. The top edge of the form content starts roughly 64–96px below the header bar, centered horizontally inside a fixed-width column (~420–480px) with equal left and right margins. Within this column, the form is stacked vertically: a bold "Welcome back" heading aligned left, a short instruction line below it, then the email label and input, followed by the password label and input, each input spanning about 100% of the column width with consistent horizontal padding and gently rounded corners. Below the fields, a full-width primary blue "Sign In" button runs edge to edge of the column (minus standard internal padding), creating the main focal point. Beneath the button, a single "Forgot Password?" link sits left-aligned with a small vertical gap, followed by a thin horizontal rule, and then a compact line of text "Don’t have an account? Sign Up" with an inline link, all left-aligned and using smaller vertical spacing to signal lower priority. The overall block is vertically balanced so that there is generous white space above the heading and below the final text, keeping the form feeling light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it scrolls with the page if content height exceeds the viewport, but generally fits within the central vertical band with generous top and bottom white space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The signup-form replaces the login-form in the exact same central column area when the URL or auth state calls for it. It is centered horizontally in the same ~420–480px wide column beneath the account-navigation-bar, with its top content starting at the same vertical offset as the login-form for consistency. The internal structure follows the same stacked pattern: a left-aligned heading such as "Create an account", a brief explanatory sentence, then a sequence of labeled input fields (e.g., name, email, password) each spanning nearly the full column width. Below the fields is a full-width primary blue "Sign Up" button matching the login button’s scale and style. Under this button, a compact text line like "Already have an account? Sign In" is left-aligned with tighter vertical spacing, mirroring the login form’s lower-priority area. There are no additional panels or columns; everything remains in a single vertical flow within the narrow central column with plenty of white space around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, swapping one-for-one with the login-form or notification-preferences-form depending on state, while preserving the same overall spacing and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferences-loader appears only over the notification preferences content region. It occupies the exact footprint of the central column area reserved for the notification-preferences-form: starting just below the page heading and any status banner, spanning the full width of the column, and extending vertically to cover the height of the form content. Visually, it can appear as a light, semi-transparent overlay card with a centered spinner and short loading message, aligned both horizontally and vertically within the central column, while the underlying form layout is dimmed. It does not extend into the top header; its bounds are visually contained within the main content column, preserving the clean edges and white space around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the notification-preferences-form within the main column; positioned relative to that column and blocking interaction beneath while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user is authenticated, the notification-preferences-form is the dominant block inside the central content column. It begins beneath the main page title and any preferences-status-banner, starting roughly 24–32px below those elements. The form spans nearly the full width of the central column (~100% of the 420–480px width), with consistent inner padding on all sides and subtle rounded corners on inputs and buttons, but no heavy borders. Internally, the form content is arranged in a clean vertical stack: a short one-line description at the top, then the email-preferences-section, followed by the push-preferences-section, and then the sms-preferences-section, each separated by generous vertical spacing (about 24px) and optionally a very light divider line to suggest grouping. At the bottom of the form, a large blue "Save Preferences" button stretches across the full column width, serving as the main visual anchor. A small, muted line of helper text (e.g., "Changes apply to future orders") sits just below the button with tighter spacing and smaller font, signaling its tertiary role. The entire form sits in the middle band of the viewport with ample white space above and below, evoking the same gentle, uncluttered feel as the original login mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column, growing vertically as sections are added but always maintaining the centered column alignment and stacked flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The email-preferences-section is the first major section inside the notification-preferences-form. It occupies the full width of the form column and sits approximately 16–24px below the form’s introductory text. At the top of the section, a left-aligned subheading like "Email Notifications" appears, with a medium-weight style slightly smaller than the main page title. Under this header, individual email-related toggles (e.g., order updates, promotions) are stacked vertically, each on its own row: label and short description aligned left, with a switch-style toggle aligned right in the same row. Each row spans the width of the column and has consistent vertical padding, giving the section a tidy, list-like structure. There is generous white space below the last email toggle (about 24px) before the next section begins, so the section reads as a distinct block without needing heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; it expands vertically as more options are added, but always remains part of the linear top-to-bottom section stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The push-preferences-section appears immediately below the email-preferences-section, separated by roughly 24px of vertical space (and optionally a subtle hairline divider). It also spans the full width of the notification-preferences-form column. A left-aligned subheading such as "Push Notifications" sits at the top of the section, aligned to the same vertical grid as the email heading above. Below, push notification options are arranged in rows similar to the email section: each row contains a descriptive label and any brief explanatory text on the left, with a right-aligned toggle at the same vertical center. Rows are vertically spaced at about 12–16px apart, making the section feel cohesive but not cramped. The bottom of this section leaves another generous gap before the SMS section to keep the overall density low.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing directly after the email-preferences-section within the single vertical column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sms-preferences-section is the third major block within the notification-preferences-form, appearing below the push-preferences-section with a similar vertical gap (~24px). It spans the full width of the central form column. At the top, a left-aligned subheading such as "SMS Notifications" is placed, matching the visual style and alignment of the Email and Push headings. Directly under this title, the sms-only-order-updates-control is placed as a prominent first row, visually slightly more emphasized than the individual SMS toggles that follow. Beneath this preset row, additional SMS-specific options are stacked vertically in the same label-left, toggle-right pattern as the other sections. The section has a comfortable amount of white space at its bottom (around 24–32px) before the full-width primary Save button, maintaining the same airy, uncluttered feel as the rest of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the form; acts as the last major section before the primary CTA in the linear vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sms-only-order-updates-control is a highlighted row located at the top of the sms-preferences-section, spanning the full width of the form column. It appears immediately beneath the "SMS Notifications" section title with a modest vertical gap (~8–12px). The row follows the same basic left-right pattern but with slightly more vertical padding, making it feel like a compact preset card: on the left, a concise label such as "SMS-only order updates" with a brief, muted description stacked directly underneath; on the right, a single prominent toggle switch aligned to the row’s vertical center. The edges of this row align perfectly with the rest of the form elements, without extruding beyond the column. A small amount of extra whitespace separates this control from the individual SMS toggles below, visually indicating that it is a higher-level preset that can override or quickly configure detailed SMS settings.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row embedded within the sms-preferences-section, remaining anchored at the top of that section as part of the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferences-status-banner sits within the central content column, just below the main page heading block and above the notification-preferences-form. It spans the full width of the column but is visually thin—roughly the height of one or two lines of text plus vertical padding—so it does not dominate the page. The banner is aligned left within the column with subtle background shading and slightly rounded corners, visually reminiscent of a slim alert bar. There is a comfortable vertical gap (~16–24px) between the heading/description above and the banner, and another similar gap between the banner and the top of the form below, so it feels like an inline status indicator rather than part of the form fields. Its horizontal edges align exactly with the form and inputs, preserving the clean single-column alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static informational strip within the main column that appears or disappears based on state; it pushes form content downward when visible but does not overlay anything.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-widget is a compact, secondary block placed near the bottom of the central content column so it does not compete with the notification preferences themselves. It appears directly below the primary "Save Preferences" button and any small helper text, separated by a modest vertical gap (~24px). The widget spans the same width as the form column and is styled as a light, borderless card or subtly outlined box with a concise heading and a few key summary lines (e.g., "Your default cafe" or last order info). Its height is relatively small compared to the main form, and it occupies only a small portion of the lower vertical band (~10–15% of the page height when visible). This placement ensures it is easily discoverable after saving changes but clearly secondary to the main notification settings and primary CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, positioned after the form content in the linear stack; it scrolls off-screen like other content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-navigation-bar occupies a thin horizontal bar at the very top of the page, spanning the full browser width but with its internal content constrained to the same centered max-width as the main column (~420–480px, or a slightly larger container that still aligns with it). Inside this bar, the content is arranged in a three-part horizontal layout: on the left, the coffee shop logo and a subtle "Back to account" or home link; in the center, a small, understated text label or control indicating the current section (e.g., a pill-shaped "Notification Preferences" item or a minimal nav strip); on the right, the logout-control. The bar itself is relatively shallow—about 10–15% of the viewport height or less—with comfortable but not exaggerated vertical padding. There is ample white space around these elements and no heavy borders, matching the minimalist, corporate-like feel. The bottom edge of this bar visually aligns with the top margin of the main content column below, creating a clear yet gentle separation between navigation and page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document; it sits above the scrollable main content but does not remain fixed when scrolling unless specifically configured to do so by the product (the default here is non-sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The logout-control is positioned inside the account-navigation-bar at the top right of the centered header content area. It sits on the same horizontal baseline as the logo and nav label, aligning vertically with them and horizontally flush with the right edge of the constrained header content column. The control appears as a compact text link or subtle button with minimal styling—smaller and less visually heavy than the main form’s primary CTA. There is a small horizontal margin between it and the right edge of the header content to avoid feeling cramped. Its presence is clearly visible but not attention-grabbing, maintaining the focus on the central page content while remaining easy to reach.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the top navigation bar; it does not scroll independently or stick separately from the bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is an invisible controller that conceptually sits behind the main content area, coordinating which primary view is shown in the central column—login-form, signup-form, or notification-preferences-form—based on the current URL or query state. It does not render any visual element and hence occupies no explicit space on the page. Its effect is observed in how the central column swaps content while maintaining the same alignment, width, and vertical spacing patterns regardless of which view is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state controller; it does not affect layout dimensions directly, only determines which of the existing column layouts is active at any time.

name: Security / Password Settings
title: Account Security & Password
description: Manage account security by reviewing basic security information, changing the account password using the current password, and accessing logout controls.

core_features: Load and display a security summary (account email and passwordLastChangedAt) using getSecuritySettingsSummary when the page initializes.
Provide a password change form that accepts currentPassword, newPassword, and confirmNewPassword, validates them on the client, and submits them to updatePassword.
Display server-side success or error messages from updatePassword and reflect updated passwordLastChangedAt in the security summary on success.
Expose a security-focused logout control that triggers logoutCurrentAccount and then navigates the user to a login or entry page, preserving a return URL via query parameters.
Display a compact current order summary widget using getCurrentOrderSummary so users can see any in-progress pickup order while managing security settings.
Handle unauthenticated access to this page by showing an inline login gate that uses loginWithEmailPassword and, on success, re-initializes security data.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View security information
1) User navigates to account_security.html, possibly with parameters such as account_security.html?from=account_dashboard.
2) On page load, the controller component reads URL parameters and calls getSecuritySettingsSummary.
3) If the call succeeds, the security summary panel displays the returned email and passwordLastChangedAt values.
4) In parallel or via shared state, the current order summary widget is populated using getCurrentOrderSummary.
5) The user can review their security info and optionally navigate back to account_dashboard.html via the breadcrumb.

Workflow 2: Change password
1) User arrives on account_security.html and sees the password change form.
2) User fills in currentPassword, newPassword, and confirmNewPassword fields.
3) On form submit, client-side validation checks for required fields, that newPassword matches confirmNewPassword, and that the new password meets the globally configured password policy (no hardcoded policy values in this page; validation relies on shared, configurable rules).
4) If client validation fails, the form displays inline validation messages without calling updatePassword, and the URL parameter status is set to status=validation_error.
5) If validation passes, the form calls updatePassword with currentPassword, newPassword, and confirmNewPassword.
6) If updatePassword returns success=true, the page:
   - Updates the security summary panel with the returned passwordLastChangedAt.
   - Shows a success message using updatePassword.message.
   - Updates the URL to account_security.html?status=success&source=password_change (or merges with existing params) so that a reload preserves the success state.
   - Shows or emphasizes a "Back to Account" breadcrumb that navigates to account_dashboard.html.
7) If updatePassword returns success=false, the page displays the server-provided message and updates the URL to include status=error.

Workflow 3: Logout from security page
1) While on account_security.html, user clicks a security-focused logout control.
2) The control calls logoutCurrentAccount.
3) If logoutCurrentAccount returns success=true, the page redirects the user to the login experience (e.g., login page) with a query parameter specifying the return URL, such as login.html?returnTo=account_security.html.
4) If logoutCurrentAccount returns an error, the page displays the server-provided message and does not redirect, allowing the user to retry.

Workflow 4: Access when not logged in (inline login gate)
1) User navigates directly to account_security.html without an active authenticated session.
2) On page load, the controller attempts to call getSecuritySettingsSummary.
3) If the call fails due to an unauthorized or similar condition, the controller marks the auth state as unauthenticated and renders the inline login gate instead of the security summary and password change form.
4) User submits the login form with email and password, which calls loginWithEmailPassword.
5) If loginWithEmailPassword returns success=true and isLoggedIn=true, the page resets the auth state to authenticated and re-runs the initialization logic (calling getSecuritySettingsSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary) while preserving any existing URL parameters such as returnTo or from.
6) If loginWithEmailPassword returns success=false, the inline login gate shows the server-provided message and keeps the user on the same page.
interactions: On page load, the controller reads URL query parameters (such as status, from, returnTo, and source) and triggers getSecuritySettingsSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary to populate the security summary and order summary components.
Submitting the password change form triggers client-side validation; if valid, it calls updatePassword and then updates both the UI and the URL parameters based on the result.
Clicking the "Back to Account" breadcrumb navigates the browser to account_dashboard.html, optionally preserving context such as account_dashboard.html?source=security.
Clicking the logout control triggers logoutCurrentAccount and, on success, navigates to the login page with a returnTo URL parameter referencing account_security.html.
Submitting the inline login form (when shown) triggers loginWithEmailPassword; on success, it clears any status=unauthenticated parameter if present and reinitializes the page data.

Changes in URL parameters (e.g., the user copying and revisiting a URL with status=success or status=error) are detected by the controller, which then configures the initial visibility of success/error messages and which sections are expanded.
page_functionality.state_logic:
This page does not use localStorage; it manages cross-navigation and refresh-resilient state exclusively via URL parameters and in-memory runtime state.

Key URL parameters:
- status: Indicates the result of the last main action. Possible values include:
  - success: A previous password change completed successfully; the password change form shows a success state and, if applicable, focuses on confirmation messaging.
  - error: The last call to updatePassword failed; the form displays the server-provided error message.
  - validation_error: Client-side validation failed before calling updatePassword; the form shows validation feedback.
- source: Indicates what triggered navigation to this page, such as source=account_dashboard or source=password_reminder. This can be used to adjust which element gets initial focus (e.g., directly on the password form) but does not change data fetching.
- from: Indicates the logical origin page (e.g., from=account_dashboard). The breadcrumbs component can use this value to decide where the "Back" link navigates. If from is missing, it defaults to account_dashboard.html.
- returnTo: When present, used primarily when the user arrives here unauthenticated. After successful login via loginWithEmailPassword, the controller compares returnTo with the current URL; if returnTo points to account_security.html or another allowed internal page, the controller navigates there or reloads the current page with the same query string.

State handling rules:
- On initial load and on every URL change, the controller parses these parameters and sets up the initial UI state (which messages to show, which workflow is active, and where breadcrumbs point) before rendering core components.
- Results from getSecuritySettingsSummary and updatePassword overwrite any outdated representations of email and passwordLastChangedAt in the in-memory view model, regardless of URL parameters.
- Order summary state from getCurrentOrderSummary is treated as ephemeral and is not encoded into the URL; the widget is refreshed on each page load or refresh rather than relying on query parameters.
- Auth state (authenticated vs unauthenticated) is determined at runtime by the success or failure of getSecuritySettingsSummary or by the result of loginWithEmailPassword, and is not stored in URL parameters except for optional status=unauthenticated for debugging or analytics contexts.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization and high-level state. On load and on URL change, it parses query parameters (status, source, from, returnTo), triggers getSecuritySettingsSummary and getCurrentOrderSummary, derives whether the user is authenticated, and decides whether to show the security views or the inline login gate.
