It also routes responses from updatePassword and logoutCurrentAccount to the appropriate components and updates the URL to reflect action outcomes.

Displays a read-only summary of the user's security-related information, including the account email and the passwordLastChangedAt timestamp returned by getSecuritySettingsSummary or updatePassword. It listens for password update events so it can refresh the displayed timestamp and any relative time rendering. It also reflects status from URL parameters by optionally highlighting recent changes.

Provides input fields for currentPassword, newPassword, and confirmNewPassword. On submit, it performs client-side validation based on required fields, equality of new and confirmation values, and a shared, configurable password policy. If validation passes, it calls updatePassword and displays the resulting success or error message from the API. On success, it emits an onPasswordUpdated event with the new passwordLastChangedAt, and it requests that the controller update the URL to include status=success. On failure, it requests status=error or status=validation_error in the URL.

Renders navigation breadcrumbs for the account area, including a link back to the main account dashboard. It uses the from URL parameter, if present, to determine the origin page but defaults to account_dashboard.html. After a successful password change (status=success), it may emphasize the breadcrumb as the primary next step. Clicking the breadcrumb navigates to account_dashboard.html, optionally adding context parameters (e.g., account_dashboard.html?source=security).

Provides a security-contextual logout action (e.g., a button or link) within the security settings area. When activated, it calls logoutCurrentAccount. If the logout is successful, it emits an onLogoutSuccess event so the controller can redirect the user to the login page with a returnTo parameter referencing account_security.html. If the API returns an error, it displays the message from logoutCurrentAccount.message and keeps the user on the page.

Displays a compact summary of the current pickup order using the data from getCurrentOrderSummary. This includes whether there is an active order (hasActiveOrder), basic order details such as itemCount and pricing, and the pickupStore name and pickupDateTime. The widget is read-only; its role on this page is to give context while users change security settings. It refreshes when the controller loads or reloads order data.

Appears only when the page controller determines the user is unauthenticated (e.g., getSecuritySettingsSummary fails due to lack of session). It presents email and password fields and, on submit, calls loginWithEmailPassword. If login succeeds with isLoggedIn=true, it emits an onAuthStateChange event with authenticated state, and the controller re-runs the security initialization logic. If login fails, it displays the message from loginWithEmailPassword.message and keeps the user on the same URL, optionally updating or setting status to reflect the failure.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same single, centered column pattern as the login mockup. A thin global header bar spans full width at the very top. Inside that bar, the account breadcrumbs sit on the left, respecting the same page side margins as the main content column. Below the header, the main content is a narrow, vertically stacked column centered on the page: page title and short description (from the security-page-controller) at the top of this column, followed by the security summary panel, then the password change form (as the main interactive block), followed by the logout control button. A subtle horizontal divider sits below this primary security area, with the order summary widget placed underneath as low-priority, contextual information. The auth-gate-inline-login uses the same central column and replaces the security content when the user is not authenticated, keeping the very linear top-to-bottom flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same single, centered column pattern as the login mockup. A thin global header bar spans full width at the very top. Inside that bar, the account breadcrumbs sit on the left, respecting the same page side margins as the main content column. Below the header, the main content is a narrow, vertically stacked column centered on the page: page title and short description (from the security-page-controller) at the top of this column, followed by the security summary panel, then the password change form (as the main interactive block), followed by the logout control button. A subtle horizontal divider sits below this primary security area, with the order summary widget placed underneath as low-priority, contextual information. The auth-gate-inline-login uses the same central column and replaces the security content when the user is not authenticated, keeping the very linear top-to-bottom flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar consumes roughly 10–15% of the viewport height, remaining visually slim. The central content column occupies the middle 60–70% of the viewport height, with generous vertical padding above and below to keep it visually floating in whitespace. The column itself is constrained to a max width of about 420–480px (roughly 35–40% of the page width on desktop), centered horizontally. Within this column, the title/description use relatively little vertical space, the security summary and password form together take the bulk of the height, and logout/order widgets share the lower portion as shorter blocks. Footer-like elements (divider, order summary) use compact vertical spacing, emphasizing their secondary nature.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and minimal. Only essential security elements are shown in the main flow: heading, short explanation, concise security summary, password fields, and logout control. Each major block is separated by comfortable vertical gaps (~24–32px), while related elements within a block (e.g., label and input) are grouped more tightly (~8–16px). Inputs and buttons span nearly the full width of the central column with consistent internal padding, mirroring the login form. Supporting text (order summary, breadcrumb labels) uses smaller type and tighter spacing to reduce visual weight and maintain a clean, uncluttered feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is a strong vertical path down the center of the screen. The eye starts at the top left of the header with the breadcrumbs, then naturally drops into the centered main title of the security page. From the title it moves down to the brief descriptive text, then immediately to the security summary panel as a quick status check. Below that, the eye is drawn to the password change form as the main interactive area, focusing on the stacked inputs and the primary 'Save/Update' style button. The logout control below reads as a secondary action in the same flow. A subtle divider then leads the eye to the order summary widget at the bottom, which is visually quieter. When the auth-gate-inline-login is shown, it takes over the central column and becomes the primary visual anchor, with the rest of the security content hidden to keep the flow simple.

layout.overall_description: The Security / Password Settings page feels like a focused, minimalist account screen built on a narrow, centered column. A slim header bar runs across the very top, with the account breadcrumb navigation aligned to the left edge of the main content column. Beneath this bar, the main security content sits in the center of the viewport with generous white space above and below, constrained to a clean, card-like column about 440px wide. At the top of this column, a bold but simple page title and a one- or two-line description establish context. Immediately underneath, a low-profile security summary panel presents key security status details in a flat, full-width block. Below that, the password change form dominates the central area, using stacked labels and full-width inputs, culminating in a large, full-width primary action button. A smaller, full-width logout control follows as a secondary action. A thin horizontal rule then visually closes the security section and introduces a quieter area where a compact order summary widget can live without competing for attention. If the user is not authenticated, this entire security column is replaced by an inline login form that mirrors the original login mockup: same centered column, same vertical rhythm, and a clear primary login button, keeping the overall experience cohesive and visually consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The security-page-controller anchors the main central column. It begins just below the global header bar, centered horizontally on the page, and constrained to a max width of roughly 420–480px. Vertically, it sits in the middle band of the viewport with generous top and bottom margins so that the column feels visually centered. At the top of this column, the controller presents the primary page title ('Security & Password' or similar), occupying a single line centered or left-aligned within the column. Directly beneath the title, a short one- or two-line description runs full width, left-aligned, with about 12–16px of vertical spacing from the title. The security summary panel, password change form, logout control, and order summary widget are conceptually children of this controller and are stacked vertically underneath the description with consistent top spacing between each block (~24–32px), maintaining a clear, linear flow down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page as part of the main document flow. It defines the central content column, maintaining its fixed max-width and centered alignment regardless of viewport size.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The security-summary-panel sits directly beneath the page description within the central column defined by the security-page-controller. It spans the full width of the column, with equal left and right insets that align perfectly with the text and form fields above and below. Vertically, it occupies a compact block—roughly 100–160px tall—presented as a flat, lightly framed panel with subtle rounded corners, visually similar in weight to an input group but slightly taller. Inside, its content (such as last password change date, multi-factor status, etc.) is arranged in 2–3 horizontal rows or short lines of text, left-aligned, with comfortable vertical spacing between lines (~8–12px). There is a generous gap (~24–32px) above and below the panel separating it from the description and the password-change-form, reinforcing it as a distinct but quick-glance summary before the user gets into detailed interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it grows vertically as needed with content, but never exceeds the column width or breaks the single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The password-change-form is placed immediately below the security-summary-panel, separated by a generous vertical gap (~24–32px). It is the dominant interactive block in the column and uses the same stacked, left-aligned layout as the login form from the mockup. The form spans the full width of the central column, with each label aligned to the left edge and each input field extending nearly the full width beneath its label, leaving only a small internal horizontal padding inside the input. The fields appear in a vertical sequence (e.g., 'Current Password', 'New Password', 'Confirm New Password'), with about 8–12px spacing between a label and its input, and 16–20px between each field group. At the bottom of the form, a primary CTA button (e.g., 'Save Password' or 'Update Password') stretches across the full width of the column, mirroring the large blue 'Sign In' button from the mockup. There is a slightly larger vertical gap (~20–24px) between the last input field and this button to visually group the fields as inputs leading to a single clear action. Any helper text or inline validation appears immediately beneath the relevant input, in smaller text, without breaking the overall vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and fully integrated into the central vertical flow. As the form grows (e.g., on error messages), it pushes content below downward while maintaining the same centered column and spacing structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-breadcrumbs-nav lives in the thin header band at the very top of the page, reflecting the mockup’s three-part header layout. It is aligned to the left side of the header, positioned so that its left edge is perfectly aligned with the left edge of the central content column below. Horizontally, it occupies only the left third of the header bar, leaving the center and right thirds visually light or reserved for global elements. Vertically, the breadcrumb text is centered within the header bar height so it feels balanced and easy to scan. The breadcrumb trail itself is concise (e.g., 'Account > Security'), using small, low-contrast text with minimal vertical height to avoid competing with the main page title below. There is a comfortable amount of whitespace above and below the breadcrumb text within the header so the bar still feels slim and unobtrusive, maintaining the airy top-of-page feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Typically static at the top of the document, moving out of view when the user scrolls down. It can optionally be part of a site-wide sticky header, but within this page layout it is treated as a fixed-position top band that the main content starts beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The logout-control appears within the same central column, directly below the password-change-form. It is separated from the form’s primary button by a modest vertical gap (~20–24px), so it clearly reads as a separate, secondary action rather than part of the form. Spatially, it is aligned to the same left and right edges as the form fields and primary button, and it typically spans the full width of the column to be easy to tap and visually consistent. The button’s visual styling is lighter than the main form CTA (e.g., outlined or less saturated) so, while it has similar physical size, it carries slightly less visual weight. This positioning—immediately beneath the main password change action—makes it feel like an advanced or emergency control closely related to security, without overshadowing the primary task.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the column; it scrolls normally with the rest of the content and does not float or stick, keeping the page clean and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-widget sits at the bottom of the central column as a tertiary, contextual element. It is introduced by a thin horizontal rule placed below the logout-control, with a vertical gap of about 24px above and below the rule, visually echoing the divider and low-priority area from the login page’s 'Don’t have an account? Sign Up' region. The widget itself is a compact panel spanning the full width of the column, aligned to the same left and right edges as all other content. Vertically, it is shorter than the main security-summary-panel—roughly 80–140px tall—containing a succinct title (e.g., 'Recent Order') and one or two lines of key info. Text is left-aligned with modest spacing between lines, and there is more whitespace around this widget and smaller text size so it feels clearly subordinate to the security actions above. The overall effect is that this widget sits at the 'footer' of the central column, discoverable but unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and fully within the normal flow; it appears only after the user scrolls past all security-related content on shorter viewports, reinforcing its lower priority.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth-gate-inline-login occupies the same central, narrow column as the security-page-controller content, but is shown only when the user is not authenticated. In this state, it effectively replaces the security-summary-panel, password-change-form, logout-control, and order-summary-widget. It is centered vertically in the viewport where possible, matching the login mockup: the top of the auth form sits noticeably below the header bar, with ample whitespace above, so the form appears as the primary focal element in the middle of the screen. Within the column, the layout mirrors the original login page: a prominent heading at the top (e.g., 'Sign In to Manage Security'), followed by a short instruction line, then stacked email/username and password fields, and finally a full-width primary login button. Below the button, lower-priority links such as 'Forgot Password?' and 'Create Account' are arranged with tighter spacing and smaller type, keeping them clearly secondary. All elements align to the same left and right column edges, preserving the single-column, vertically flowing structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional and static. When active, it replaces or hides the main security content within the central column, ensuring users see only one clear task (login) at a time. It scrolls normally if its content exceeds the viewport height, maintaining the same centered column alignment.

name: About
title: About
description: Informational page showing dynamic brand, history, and sourcing content, with inline account login/signup and a compact current-order summary.

core_features: Load and display the coffee shop’s story, mission, and sourcing content using getAboutPageContent, with in-page navigation between sections via URL parameters.
Allow users to log in with email and password or sign up for an account directly from the About page using loginWithEmailPassword and signupAccount, updating account context in place.
Allow logged-in users to log out from the About page using logoutCurrentAccount, clearing account-specific context from the UI.
Display a mini current-order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary so users can see their active pickup order status while reading About content.
Provide navigation to the Contact page (contact.html) via contextual "Contact Us" links within the About content.
user_workflows:
View About content and navigate between sections:
1. User opens about.html, optionally with a section URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section={sectionKey}).
2. On load, the page calls getAboutPageContent.
3. The About content component renders the returned title and sections in order.
4. If a section parameter is present and matches a section identifier derived from the API response, the page scrolls/focuses that section or highlights it as active.
5. User can click internal section navigation links (e.g., from a section index at the top) which update the section URL parameter and scroll to the corresponding section without reloading the page.
6. If the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation), the state controller re-applies the section selection against the already-loaded content.

Log in from the About page using email and password:
1. User opens about.html, optionally with auth=login in the URL to pre-open the login form.
2. The account auth panel checks the auth URL parameter; if it equals login and no account is loaded, it displays the login form component.
3. User enters email and password and submits the form.
4. The login form calls loginWithEmailPassword with the entered values.
5. While waiting, the component disables the submit button and shows a loading state.
6. On success (response.success and response.isLoggedIn are true), the account auth panel updates to show the logged-in state, including account.fullName, loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, and favoriteStore if returned.
7. The URL state controller may remove or neutralize the auth parameter (e.g., updating to auth=none) to reflect that the user is no longer in an explicit login flow.
8. On failure, the login form shows the response.message as an error and keeps the auth=login parameter in the URL until dismissed or changed by the user.

Sign up for a new account from the About page:
1. User opens about.html, optionally with auth=signup in the URL to show the signup form directly.
2. The account auth panel reads auth=signup and renders the signup form component if no account is currently loaded.
3. User fills in fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms (via a required checkbox) and submits the form.
4. The signup form calls signupAccount with these values.
5. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a loading state is indicated.
6. On success, the accountDashboard object from the response is used to populate the authenticated state (fullName, email, loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, favoriteStore where available).
7. The auth panel switches to a logged-in view; the URL state controller updates the URL to remove or neutralize auth, so that a refresh reflects the default About view.
8. On error, the signup form shows response.message and keeps the auth=signup state until the user cancels or corrects the input.

Log out from the About page:
1. While viewing about.html, the user is in a logged-in state as indicated by the account auth panel.
2. The user clicks the logout control inside the auth panel.
3. The logout button handler calls logoutCurrentAccount with no parameters.
4. On success, the panel clears any in-memory account data and switches the UI back to a non-authenticated state (e.g., offering login/signup options).
5. The URL state controller ensures the URL does not contain an auth parameter that would erroneously reopen an auth form (e.g., updates or removes auth).
6. If logout fails, an error message from response.message is shown and the current authenticated state is kept until the user retries or navigates away.

View mini order summary while on About page and navigate to the order details:
1. User opens about.html.
2. On load, the order summary widget calls getCurrentOrderSummary.
3. If hasActiveOrder is false, the widget renders an appropriate placeholder state (e.g., a compact, non-interactive message or a hidden panel) based only on the API result.
4. If hasActiveOrder is true, the widget displays key fields such as itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore.name, and pickupDateTime as returned by the interface.
5. User can click an action within the widget (e.g., a link or button) to view full order details.
6. The click navigates the user to an order details page URL (e.g., order.html?orderId={orderId}) using the orderId from the API response, without storing any additional state locally.
7. If the user navigates back to about.html, the widget calls getCurrentOrderSummary again to refresh the summary.

Navigate from About to the Contact page:
1. User reads About content on about.html.
2. Within one or more About sections returned by getAboutPageContent, a "Contact Us" link is rendered as part of the structured content or as a contextual action.
3. When the user clicks this link, the navigation component directs the browser to contact.html using plain navigation consistent with the outgoing_connections definition.
4. The About page does not retain additional state beyond what is encoded in its own URL; any return to About will re-run the relevant interface calls.
interactions: On page load (about.html initial load), the URL-state controller parses the current query parameters (e.g., section, auth, orderPanel) and triggers initial data loading: getAboutPageContent for page content and getCurrentOrderSummary for the order widget.

When the user clicks a section navigation link (e.g., a link pointing to about.html?section={sectionKey}), the browser updates the URL, and the section navigation component scrolls to or highlights the matching section in the already-rendered About content without re-fetching data if not needed.

When the user toggles between login and signup modes (e.g., via buttons in the auth panel), the auth panel updates the auth URL parameter (auth=login or auth=signup) and conditionally displays the corresponding form component while hiding the other.

When the user submits the login form, the form component validates required fields, calls loginWithEmailPassword, and then updates the account context in the auth panel based on the response, including switching to the logged-in view or displaying an error message.

When the user submits the signup form, the form component validates required fields (including matching password and confirmPassword and acceptTerms), calls signupAccount, and then updates the account context to the new account using accountDashboard on success or shows an error on failure.
When the user clicks the logout control, the event handler calls logoutCurrentAccount; on success, the auth panel clears all displayed account information and updates the URL to a non-auth state.

When the user expands or collapses the mini order summary (if there is UI to collapse it), the order summary widget may update a URL parameter (e.g., orderPanel=open or orderPanel=closed) to reflect this state and use that parameter to restore the same expanded/collapsed state on reload.

When the user clicks on an action inside the order summary widget to view the active order, the component navigates to an order details page using the orderId and any additional query parameters required, without storing order data locally.

When the user clicks a "Contact Us" link inside the About content, the navigation behavior sends the browser to contact.html as specified, optionally preserving or discarding existing query parameters according to the navigation design (but not relying on local storage).
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page relies solely on URL parameters and live interface responses for state; it does not use localStorage or other client-side persistent storage. The primary URL parameters include: 1) section: A string identifying which About section should be focused or highlighted (e.g., section={sectionKey}). sectionKey is derived from the getAboutPageContent sections (for example, normalized from each section heading or from a separate identifier provided alongside the heading). On initial load and on URL change events, if section is present and matches an available section, the About content loader component scrolls to that section and marks it as active. Changing section via link clicks updates the URL, enabling deep-linking and browser back/forward behavior. 2) auth: A string that controls which authentication mode is active in the account auth panel. Recognized values include login, signup, and optional neutral states (e.g., auth=none or absence of auth) as defined by the implementation. On load, if auth=login and no account is active, the login form is displayed. If auth=signup and no account is active, the signup form is displayed. When an auth action completes successfully (login or signup), the URL-state controller may update the URL to remove or neutralize auth, so that the authenticated state is represented by live interface data instead of URL parameters. 3) orderPanel (optional): A string controlling whether the mini order summary widget is visible or expanded (e.g., orderPanel=open or orderPanel=closed). On load, the widget reads this parameter and adjusts its initial UI (e.g., expanded/visible or collapsed). When the user toggles the widget, the UI updates orderPanel accordingly to keep the state in sync with the URL. All account and order details displayed on the page are always derived from the most recent responses from loginWithEmailPassword, signupAccount, logoutCurrentAccount, getAboutPageContent, and getCurrentOrderSummary rather than from hardcoded or cached values. Navigating away (e.g., clicking Contact Us) changes the page URL to contact.html, and returning to about.html re-executes the appropriate interface calls based solely on the URL and fresh API responses.

components.functionality: Parses and manages URL query parameters for the About page (such as section, auth, and orderPanel), triggers initial data loading via assigned interfaces, and updates the URL in response to user interactions without using localStorage.
Calls getAboutPageContent to retrieve the dynamic About title and sections, renders them in order, and supports focusing a particular section based on the section URL parameter.
Builds a navigable index of About sections from the getAboutPageContent response and provides links that update the section URL parameter and scroll to the corresponding section.
Handles clicks on any "Contact Us" links embedded within the About content and navigates the user to contact.html in accordance with the outgoing_connections definition.

Displays either authentication options (login/signup) or the current authenticated account summary, based on interface results and the auth URL parameter. It orchestrates showing the inline login or signup forms and, when logged in, shows basic account context such as fullName, loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, and favoriteStore from the interface responses.
Collects email and password from the user when the auth mode is login, calls loginWithEmailPassword, and passes the result to the auth panel to update the account state or display error messages.

Collects fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and terms acceptance when the auth mode is signup, calls signupAccount, and on success forwards accountDashboard data to the auth panel to switch to the logged-in view.
Provides a logout control in the authenticated state and calls logoutCurrentAccount to clear the session, updating the auth panel and URL state appropriately on success.

Displays a compact summary of the current order, using getCurrentOrderSummary to show whether there is an active order, its itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime, and optionally provides a link to view full order details.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain a clean, single, centered column for all main About content, account actions, and order summary, aligned under a thin full-width header bar. Inside this column, stack components vertically in a strict top-to-bottom order: section navigation, About content, About contact link, account auth panel (housing login/signup or logout), then the compact order summary. No sidebars or multi-column splits; all interactive elements align to the same central column edges.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain a clean, single, centered column for all main About content, account actions, and order summary, aligned under a thin full-width header bar. Inside this column, stack components vertically in a strict top-to-bottom order: section navigation, About content, About contact link, account auth panel (housing login/signup or logout), then the compact order summary. No sidebars or multi-column splits; all interactive elements align to the same central column edges.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve a shallow band at the top (~10–15% viewport height) for the global header (logo, back, location), then a broad middle band for the central column that holds the About and account content with generous top and bottom padding. The central column stays narrow (about 420–480px wide, roughly 5–6 of 12 grid columns) and is horizontally centered. Within the column, larger vertical gaps separate major blocks (24–40px), while smaller gaps (12–16px) separate related sub-elements to keep hierarchy clear and light.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the page visually low-density: short, readable text blocks, ample white space around each component, and no more than one primary interactive element in view at a time within the central band (e.g., either login or signup). Avoid decorative elements; rely on whitespace, a single brand blue, subtle gray lines, and light typography to structure information. The order summary is compact and minimal, reinforcing that it is supportive rather than dominant.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top header into the centered About column, first to the About heading and section navigation, then down through the story/content, into the account-auth panel (which echoes the strong vertical form pattern of the login mockup), then finally to the order summary at the bottom. The main visual anchors are the About heading at the top of the column and the bold blue primary button inside the auth panel (login, signup, or logout). Text links and secondary controls sit below these anchors with smaller type and tighter spacing, clearly secondary in the scan path.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a slim, full-width header bar pinned to the top, using the same three-part layout as the mockup: logo and back link on the left, a centered location control, and any auxiliary actions on the right, all aligned to wide page margins. Below this, the eye is drawn to a single, narrow central column of content that is horizontally centered and visually floats in a field of white space. At the top of this column, an About heading and a subtle section navigation row introduce the brand story. Directly beneath, the About content flows as left-aligned text blocks spanning the full width of the column, with comfortable breathing room above and below each paragraph. At the end of this story area, a discrete contact link sits with low visual weight. Further down, the account auth panel appears as a simple, lightly framed block that occupies the full width of the same column; within it, either the login form, signup form, or a single logout button is shown, all laid out vertically with the large blue primary button mirroring the login mockup’s treatment. Finally, a compact order summary card is stacked below the auth panel, aligned to the same column width, giving a quick, unobtrusive view of the user’s current order. The entire experience is linear, calm, and minimal, with a clear downward reading and interaction flow and no visual clutter competing for attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and occupies no physical space on the page. It exists logically behind the centered main column, controlling which About section is active, which form (login or signup) is shown inside the account auth panel, and when the logout button and order summary reflect the current state. It does not alter spacing or alignment directly; instead, it toggles visibility and content within the existing fixed layout regions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered controller with no spatial footprint; influences which of the overlapping components (login-form-inline, signup-form-inline, logout-action-button) is visible within the fixed area of the account-auth-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned near the top of the centered main column, directly beneath the section navigation, this component spans the full width of the column (approximately 420–480px, about 5–6 of 12 grid columns) and is horizontally centered in the page. It begins with the About page heading and optional subheading, left-aligned while still confined to the central column’s width, followed by one or more blocks of narrative text about the brand, history, and sourcing. There is a generous top margin (roughly 48–72px) separating it from the global header, and a clear gap (24–32px) between it and the section navigation above. Each paragraph or content block within is spaced with comfortable vertical gaps (16–24px), keeping the area airy and easy to scan. The bottom edge of this content area sits several spacing units above the account-auth-panel, creating a clear visual break between informational content and account actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the central column; scrolls with the page and expands vertically based on content while maintaining fixed horizontal alignment and max width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation row sits at the very top of the main content column, just below the header bar and above the About content loader. It aligns to the left edge of the central column but spans its full width, functioning as a thin horizontal strip of section links (e.g., 'Our Story', 'Sourcing', 'Community') laid out inline across the width. The height of this row is modest, occupying only a small band (roughly 40–56px tall), with ample white space above and below to prevent crowding the About heading and text. Active section indicators are understated—such as a blue underline or a subtle accent in the brand blue—keeping with the minimalist style. The navigation does not touch the viewport edges; instead, it is centered with the column, leaving broad white margins on both sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the top of the main column; may optionally become sticky within the central column region on longer About content, but by default scrolls normally as part of the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact link appears at the lower end of the About content area, aligned to the left edge of the text within the central column. Visually, it can either sit as an inline link at the end of the final paragraph or, more clearly, as a standalone line immediately below the last paragraph with a small vertical gap (8–12px) above it. The link text is in the brand blue while surrounding text remains neutral, making it easy to spot without overpowering the main narrative. It occupies only a single line’s height and the same column width, ensuring it feels integrated with the About content rather than a separate module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline or small-block link within the About content flow; no special behavior beyond standard hover/active states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Located below the About content and its contact link, the account auth panel is a dedicated block that spans the full width of the central column and is horizontally centered, mirroring the proportions of the login form in the original mockup. A generous vertical margin (32–40px) separates it from the content above, clearly signaling a shift from informational reading to account interaction. The panel appears as a simple card or framed area with subtle rounded corners and a light border or soft shadow, keeping it visually distinct yet minimalist. Inside this panel, elements are left-aligned and stacked vertically: a concise title (e.g., 'Your Account'), an optional one-line description, and then either the login form, signup form, or logout button—only one of these states is visible at a time. The inner content respects consistent padding on all sides (16–24px), and the primary action button inside (login, signup, or logout) spans nearly the full width of the panel, echoing the dominant blue button treatment from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block card in the central column; its height varies based on whether it is showing the login form, signup form, or logout button, but its width and centered position remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the account auth panel is in 'login' state, this form occupies the central area within that panel, using a strict top-to-bottom alignment reminiscent of the original login mockup. Inside the panel’s padded area, the login form stretches to nearly 100% of the internal width, with labels left-aligned directly above each input. The email label and input appear first, followed by a comfortable vertical gap (12–16px), then the password label and input. Below the inputs, a full-width primary 'Sign In' button in the brand blue spans almost the entire panel width, serving as the main visual anchor in this region. A small vertical space (8–12px) below the button houses a subtle 'Forgot password?' link aligned to the left. All elements remain within the bounds of the narrow column, and no elements extend to the page edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the central interaction area inside the account-auth-panel; mutually exclusive with the signup-form-inline and logout-action-button, only one being visible at a time under control of the url-state-controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the auth panel is in 'signup' mode, this form replaces the login form within the same internal area of the account auth panel. It follows the same vertical, left-aligned structure: a stack of labels and inputs (e.g., name, email, password, confirm password) each occupying the full internal width, separated by consistent vertical gaps (12–16px). At the bottom of this stack, a full-width primary 'Sign Up' button in the brand blue anchors the form visually, matching the width and relative position of the login button for consistency. A small line of secondary text (e.g., 'Already have an account? Sign In') can appear directly beneath the button with minimal spacing, using a standard font size and an inline blue link. The entire block respects the panel’s internal padding and does not break the central column’s narrow width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same spatial footprint as the login-form-inline inside the account-auth-panel; only one (login or signup) is visible at any given time based on the url-state-controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a user is already authenticated, the login and signup forms are hidden, and this button occupies the same central position inside the account auth panel. The button spans nearly the full internal width of the panel, aligned centrally within the narrow column, and is rendered in the brand blue with the same height, corner radius, and typography as the login/sign-up buttons to maintain visual continuity. It is positioned with a comfortable margin (16–24px) below the panel’s title or confirmation text (e.g., 'You’re signed in'), leaving some white space above and below to preserve the airy, minimal feel. There are no additional elements directly beneath it, so the button is the clear focal point of this panel state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same action area inside the account-auth-panel as the forms’ primary CTA; appears only in the 'logged-in' state under control of the url-state-controller, replacing the forms entirely.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary widget sits below the account auth panel, aligned to the same centered column and spanning its full width. A moderate vertical gap (32px) separates it from the auth panel above, signaling it as a distinct but related section. The widget is visually compact: a short title (e.g., 'Current Order') aligned to the left at the top of the card, followed by a minimal list of items and totals, all within a light card or outlined box similar to, but slightly less visually prominent than, the auth panel. The content is tightly curated to keep the height low—ideally not more than a small fraction of the viewport height—so that it feels like a quick, supportive glance rather than a full cart page. Ample padding (16–20px) inside the widget maintains the spacious style, and the entire card remains well within the narrow central column without touching the page edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element at the bottom of the main column; scrolls with the page and expands vertically only as needed for the small summary, without any sticky or floating behavior.

name: Contact / Support
title: Contact Support
description: Page where customers can view support contact details and submit questions, feedback, or support requests. Supports both guest and logged-in users, and can preselect a topic when navigated from the help page.

core_features: Load and display live support contact details (email, phone, hours) using getSupportContactInfo.
Provide a contact form that submits support requests via submitSupportRequest, including name, email, topic, and message.
Preselect the support topic in the contact form based on the optional topic URL parameter when arriving from help.html.
Show a mini current order summary widget using getCurrentOrderSummary to help users reference the correct order when contacting support.
Allow users to log in with email/password using loginWithEmailPassword so their name and email can be prefilled in the support form.
Allow users to create a new account via signupAccount directly from the page, then use their account details in the support form.
Allow logged-in users to log out via logoutCurrentAccount, after which the support form switches back to manual name/email input.

After a successful support request submission, display a confirmation with the support response message and ticketId, and offer navigation to account_dashboard.html with the source URL parameter when the user is logged in.
Provide navigation to help.html so users can consult help articles instead of or in addition to submitting a support request.
user_workflows:
1) Viewing support contact details as a guest:
   1. User opens contact.html (e.g., via the footer navigation link).
   2. On page load, the page controller calls getSupportContactInfo.
   3. The support-contact-info-panel component displays the returned supportEmail, supportPhone, and supportHours.
   4. The user can choose to call, email, or instead fill out the support form.

2) Submitting a support request as a guest:
   1. User opens contact.html (optionally with a topic URL parameter).
   2. Page controller reads the topic URL parameter and sets the initial selection in the support-topic-selector if it matches a known topic key.
   3. User fills in the name, email, selected topic, and message fields in the support-request-form.
   4. User clicks the form submit action.
   5. The form validates required fields (client-side) and, if valid, calls submitSupportRequest with name, email, topic, and message.
   6. On a successful response (success = true), the support-confirmation-panel shows the returned message and ticketId.
   7. Because the user is not logged in, only generic options (e.g., close or submit another request) are shown; no "Go to My Account" navigation is presented.

3) Submitting a support request as a logged-in user with prefilled details:
   1. User logs in via the auth-header-controls using the loginWithEmailPassword interface.
   2. On successful login (success = true and isLoggedIn = true), the page stores the returned account context in memory (e.g., fullName and email) and updates the UI.
   3. The support-request-form automatically prepopulates the name and email fields from the logged-in account and may render them as non-editable depending on business rules.
   4. User selects or confirms the topic (possibly already set from the topic URL parameter) and types their message.
   5. User submits the form, which calls submitSupportRequest with the prefilled name and email and the entered topic and message.
   6. On a successful response, the support-confirmation-panel displays the message and ticketId.
   7. Because the user is logged in, the confirmation panel also displays a "Go to My Account" action that navigates to account_dashboard.html with the URL parameter source=support.

4) Arriving from help.html with a preselected topic:
   1. User is browsing help.html and clicks a contextual "Contact Support" link.
   2. help.html navigates to contact.html with a topic query parameter (e.g., contact.html?topic=<topicKey>).
   3. On load, the page controller reads the topic URL parameter.
   4. If the provided topic matches one of the selectable topics in support-topic-selector, that topic is set as the currently selected option in the form.
   5. The user reviews or changes the topic, completes the form, and submits it via submitSupportRequest.
   6. The rest of the flow follows the guest or logged-in submission workflow depending on the user’s authentication state.

5) Using the order summary when contacting support:
   1. User opens contact.html.
   2. The page controller calls getCurrentOrderSummary on load.
   3. If hasActiveOrder is true, the order-summary-widget displays the orderId, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore summary, and pickupDateTime.
   4. The user can refer to this summary while writing their support message (e.g., by manually including the orderId in the message body).
   5. If hasActiveOrder is false, the order-summary-widget either remains hidden or displays a minimal state indicating no active order, without using hardcoded example values.

6) Switching between guest and authenticated states on the Contact Support page:
   1. User lands on contact.html and initially sees the support-request-form in guest mode (name/email fields editable).
   2. User chooses to log in via auth-header-controls.
   3. On successful login, auth-header-controls updates to show the logged-in state and triggers an update of the support-request-form to prefill name and email from the account data.
   4. If the user clicks logout via auth-header-controls (calling logoutCurrentAccount), the page clears any in-memory account context, returns the form to guest mode, and continues to allow support submissions as a guest.
interactions: On page load, a controller component reads the topic URL parameter and triggers parallel interface calls to getSupportContactInfo and getCurrentOrderSummary to populate the contact info panel and order summary widget.
When the user changes the topic selection in support-topic-selector, the selected topic value is bound to the support-request-form state so it is sent as the topic parameter in submitSupportRequest.
When the user types into the name, email, or message fields in support-request-form (guest mode), each field updates the local form state used when calling submitSupportRequest.

When the user submits the support-request-form, client-side validation runs (e.g., non-empty name, syntactically valid email, non-empty message, and a selected topic). If validation passes, submitSupportRequest is called; during the call, the submit button can enter a loading state and error messages are cleared.

If submitSupportRequest returns success = true, the support-confirmation-panel is shown or updated to display the response message and ticketId; the support-request-form may be reset or minimized according to business rules.
If submitSupportRequest returns success = false, the support-request-form displays the returned message as an error, and the confirmation panel remains hidden.

In auth-header-controls, when a user submits the login form, loginWithEmailPassword is called with the entered email and password; on success, the component updates the visible header state (e.g., showing the user’s name) and notifies other components that account data is available.

In auth-header-controls, when a user submits the signup form, signupAccount is called with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms. On success, the page treats the user as logged in and exposes the accountDashboard context to other components (primarily to prefill the support-request-form).

When the logout action is clicked in auth-header-controls, logoutCurrentAccount is called. On success, the header reverts to the guest state and dependent components (like support-request-form) revert to non-prefilled, editable fields.

In order-summary-widget, when getCurrentOrderSummary returns hasActiveOrder = true, the widget binds itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime to display or expose them for copying; if hasActiveOrder = false, the widget hides detailed fields.
Clicking the "Go to My Account" action in support-confirmation-panel (visible only when the user is considered logged in) navigates to account_dashboard.html with the URL parameter source=support.

Clicking the help navigation link on this page navigates to help.html (without additional parameters unless added by calling context), allowing users to read help articles instead of or before sending a support request.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL parameter used by this page is topic. On initial load of contact.html, the page reads the topic query parameter from the URL (e.g., contact.html?topic=<topicKey>). If present and if it matches an allowed topic key recognized by the support-topic-selector component, that value becomes the initial selected topic in the contact form. If topic is missing or does not match any allowed topic, the form uses its default topic selection logic without relying on hardcoded labels or values in this specification. The page does not use localStorage; any cross-navigation context is passed only via URL parameters. When the user clicks the 'Go to My Account' action in the support-confirmation-panel, navigation to account_dashboard.html includes the query parameter source=support (e.g., account_dashboard.html?source=support) so that the account dashboard can infer that the user arrived from a support flow. Navigation to help.html originates from a dedicated link on this page (e.g., help.html without additional parameters by default). Transient UI state such as whether the confirmation panel is visible, whether the user is treated as logged in, and the current form field values is maintained in memory for the life of the page and is not persisted across reloads via URL or storage, except for the topic parameter that may be set by help.html or other pages.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact Support page by reading URL parameters and orchestrating interface calls to load support contact information and the current order summary. It also exposes the initial topic selection to the form and determines the initial UI mode (guest vs. authenticated) based on in-memory session state provided by auth interactions.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: read topic from URL, call getSupportContactInfo, call getCurrentOrderSummary, and propagate results to child components.
onAuthStateChange: when auth-header-controls reports login or logout, update global page state and notify support-request-form and support-confirmation-panel.
onUrlChange: if the page supports client-side navigation that changes the topic parameter without full reload, re-evaluate the topic parameter and update support-topic-selector accordingly.

components.functionality: Manages login, signup, and logout functionality accessible from the page header. It authenticates users, creates new accounts, and clears sessions using the provided interfaces, then shares the resulting account context with the rest of the page.
event_handlers: onLoginSubmit: call loginWithEmailPassword with the entered email and password; on success, store account context, mark user as logged in, and emit an authStateChange event.

onSignupSubmit: call signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms; on success, treat the returned accountDashboard as the current account context and emit an authStateChange event.
onLogoutClick: call logoutCurrentAccount; on success, clear account context, mark user as logged out, and emit an authStateChange event.
onAuthStateChangeEmit: notify page-init-controller, support-request-form, support-confirmation-panel, and order-summary-widget of authentication state changes so they can update their behavior.

components.functionality: Displays a compact summary of the user's current pickup order, if any, using data from getCurrentOrderSummary. Provides context for the user to reference their order when writing a support request.
event_handlers: onOrderSummaryLoad: triggered when page-init-controller receives getCurrentOrderSummary response; binds hasActiveOrder, orderId, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime to the widget state.
onAuthStateChange: optionally re-request getCurrentOrderSummary when the user logs in or out, if the underlying system associates carts/orders with accounts.

components.functionality: Shows the current customer support email address, phone number, and support hours returned by getSupportContactInfo so users can reach support directly without submitting the form.
event_handlers: onSupportInfoLoad: triggered when page-init-controller receives getSupportContactInfo response; binds supportEmail, supportPhone, and supportHours to display fields.
onRefreshClick: optional action allowing the user to manually refresh contact information by calling getSupportContactInfo again.

components.functionality: Controls the selected support topic for the contact form. It initializes from the topic URL parameter when possible and lets the user change the selection. The selected value is bound to the support-request-form so it can be sent via submitSupportRequest.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: on page-init-controller load, set the initial selected topic based on the topic URL parameter if it matches an allowed topic key.
onTopicChange: when the user selects a different topic, update SupportRequestFormState.topic and clear any topic-related validation errors in the support-request-form.

components.functionality: Collects user input (name, email, topic, and message) and sends it to the backend via submitSupportRequest. It adapts its fields based on authentication state, prefilling data from the logged-in account when available and validating inputs before submission.
data_binding: AccountProfile (for logged-in prefill of fullName and email)
SupportRequestFormState (local view-model containing name, email, topic, message)
event_handlers: onAuthStateChange: when notified that a user has logged in or out, update the form's name and email fields (prefill or clear) and adjust whether they are editable.
onFieldChange: update SupportRequestFormState.name, email, topic, or message as the user types or changes selections; clear field-specific validation errors as the user corrects them.

onValidate: run synchronous validation before submission (e.g., required fields, email format, selected topic) without using hardcoded example values; if validation fails, show inline errors and prevent submission.
onSubmit: if validation passes, call submitSupportRequest with SupportRequestFormState.name, email, topic, and message; set a submitting state until the response is received.

onSubmitSuccess: when submitSupportRequest returns success = true, store ticketId and response message, notify support-confirmation-panel, and optionally reset SupportRequestFormState.message while keeping name/email/topic for subsequent requests.
onSubmitError: when submitSupportRequest returns success = false, display the returned message as a non-field or form-level error and do not show the confirmation panel.

components.functionality: Displays confirmation details after a successful support request submission, including the response message and ticketId. For logged-in users, it offers a direct navigation link to their account dashboard with the appropriate URL parameter.
data_binding: AccountProfile (to determine if the user is logged in and to show personalized confirmation messaging if desired)
event_handlers: onSubmitSupportRequestResult: listen for results from support-request-form; if success = true, show the panel with the bound message and ticketId; if success = false, ensure the panel is hidden.
onAuthStateChange: enable or disable the 'Go to My Account' navigation action based on whether an account context is present.
onGoToAccountClick: when the user clicks the account navigation action, navigate to account_dashboard.html with the URL parameter source=support (e.g., account_dashboard.html?source=support).
onSubmitAnotherClick: optional action to clear the previous ticket information, hide the panel, and reset relevant support-request-form fields for a new submission.

components.functionality: Provides a direct navigation option from the Contact Support page to help.html so users can view help articles and FAQs instead of or in addition to contacting support.
data_binding: OptionalContext (e.g., current topic, if future enhancements choose to pass it as a URL parameter)
event_handlers: onClick: navigate to help.html without modifying the current page's state; any desired context (such as the current topic) can be appended to the help.html URL as query parameters if required by the broader application.
onKeyboardActivate: support keyboard-based activation (e.g., Enter/Space) to trigger the same navigation behavior as onClick.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All visible content is organized in a single, centered vertical column that mirrors the login mockup. A thin full-width header bar sits at the top with a three-part horizontal layout aligned to the same central column margins. Below the header, the main support content flows linearly from top to bottom: a title/intro block (inside the support-request form), followed by the support contact info panel, then the topic selector, an optional order summary widget, the main support request form fields, a confirmation/notification panel (when active) and finally a low‑priority help navigation link at the very bottom. No sidebars; every component stacks within the same narrow central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All visible content is organized in a single, centered vertical column that mirrors the login mockup. A thin full-width header bar sits at the top with a three-part horizontal layout aligned to the same central column margins. Below the header, the main support content flows linearly from top to bottom: a title/intro block (inside the support-request form), followed by the support contact info panel, then the topic selector, an optional order summary widget, the main support request form fields, a confirmation/notification panel (when active) and finally a low‑priority help navigation link at the very bottom. No sidebars; every component stacks within the same narrow central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a shallow band at the top (~10–15% of the viewport height) with minimal vertical height. The central content column (max-width around 420–480px) sits centered on the page with generous top and bottom padding, visually dominating the page while still leaving comfortable white space above and below. Within this column, the support-request form and topic selector consume most of the column’s vertical real estate. The contact info panel and order summary widget get compact card-like blocks that respect the same column width but use less height. The help navigation link uses minimal vertical space, sitting close to the bottom of the central column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low and airy. Each major block (contact info panel, topic selector, order summary, main form, confirmation panel) is separated by consistent vertical gaps, large enough to clearly differentiate sections but not so large that the flow feels broken. Labels, inputs, and buttons use comfortable spacing with clear breathing room. Only essential support elements are visible: core fields, key contact details, and a small number of auxiliary links, avoiding any promotional or decorative clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top header (brand/auth controls) and quickly drops into the centered column. The first focal point is the form’s title (e.g., ‘Contact Support’), then the brief intro text/instruction. From there, the eye flows down to the contact info panel (where to call/email), then naturally into the topic selector and any associated order summary if relevant. The main visual anchor is the primary submit button within the support-request form, which stands out in brand blue and spans almost the full column width. After the button, attention moves down to any confirmation panel (when shown) and finally to the low-emphasis help navigation link at the bottom. The path is strictly vertical and predictable, minimizing cognitive load.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a clean, centered support experience on a white background. A thin, full-width header bar runs across the top, with its content constrained to the same narrow max-width column as the body. Below this header, a single vertical column sits perfectly centered in the viewport, about 420–480px wide, with substantial white space on both left and right. At the top of this column, the support request form begins with a bold title and a short instruction line, establishing context. Immediately beneath, a compact contact info panel spells out phone, email, and hours in a card-like block. Following this, a full-width topic selector and an optional order summary widget stack neatly, each separated by even vertical gaps. The main form fields (name, email, message, etc.) continue the vertical rhythm, culminating in a wide brand-blue submit button that acts as the page’s main visual anchor. Below the button, any confirmation or error panel appears as a thin, full-width notice, and the layout finishes with a small, subtle help navigation link centered or lightly left-aligned at the base of the column. The structure is minimal, linear, and focused, with no sidebars or extraneous content, echoing the calm, corporate style of the original login mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller is non-visual and has no direct on-screen footprint. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page hierarchy, initializing state such as whether a user is logged in and whether a topic was preselected from the help page. Its influence is reflected spatially by determining which of the visible components (e.g., order summary widget or confirmation panel) actually appear in the central column, but it does not consume any width, height, or padding in the visual layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller with no spatial presence; it only affects which components are rendered and with what data.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth header controls are placed inside a thin horizontal header bar at the very top of the viewport. The bar spans 100% of the page width with a white or very light background and a subtle bottom divider line, echoing the login mockup’s top bar. Within this bar, content is constrained to the same centered max-width as the main column (around 420–480px), aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. The header content is divided into three horizontal zones: on the left, the coffee shop logo and a small ‘Back’ or ‘Home’ style link; in the center, a simple text label like ‘Support’ or a minimal control; on the right, the authentication controls (e.g., ‘Sign In’ / user avatar). Vertically, the bar is shallow—roughly 48–64px tall—so it occupies only a small band (~10–15% of the viewport height), leaving most of the page for the centered support content below. There is modest horizontal padding inside the bar, matching the spacing system used for inputs and buttons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky by default); it pushes the main content column downward and remains at the top when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary widget appears inside the central column, below the support-topic selector and above the main support-request form fields. It spans nearly the full width of the column (about 90–100% of the column width, respecting interior padding), forming a card-like block with a subtle border or faint background and lightly rounded corners, consistent with the minimal flat style. Vertically, it is compact—roughly the height of 2–4 lines of text plus a small product thumbnail or order meta data—so it reads as a contextual detail rather than a dominant section. There is generous vertical spacing (about one standard section gap, e.g., 16–24px) above and below it to distinguish it as its own piece of information. The widget is left-aligned within the column, with key order details aligned along the same text axis as form labels, maintaining a clean vertical rhythm. When not applicable, this block is simply omitted, and the topic selector flows directly into the main form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the vertical flow of the main column; appears conditionally based on state (e.g., if the topic relates to an order) but does not change the surrounding layout behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support contact info panel sits near the top of the central column, directly below the form’s title and introductory text and above the topic selector. It occupies almost the full width of the central column (roughly 90–100% of the column width), styled as a subtle information card with light borders or a faint tinted background, minimal iconography, and small rounded corners. Inside, the content (phone number, email, live chat hours) is left-aligned, with lines of text stacked vertically and small vertical spacing between them, matching the spacing between form labels and inputs. This panel is visually compact but clearly separated from the title above and the topic selector below by consistent vertical gaps (around 16–24px), so it reads as an informational block: ‘Here’s how else you can reach us.’ Its height remains modest, taking up roughly as much space as a couple of short paragraphs, keeping the main form below it clearly visible in the viewport on standard screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the main column; it does not scroll independently and moves with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support topic selector is positioned directly beneath the support contact info panel within the central column, acting as the first interactive element in the form sequence. It is left-aligned, with a clear label on top and a full-width dropdown or segmented control beneath it. The control spans almost the entire width of the column (around 90–100% of its width), with generous horizontal padding inside the field and a height similar to the email/password inputs in the login mockup. There is a comfortable margin (about 8–12px) between the label and the control, and a larger margin (16–24px) separating this block from both the info panel above and the next block (order summary widget or form fields) below, maintaining the spacious vertical rhythm. The selector visually signals its importance in the flow but remains secondary to the main submit button; it blends into the overall minimalist, flat style with a simple outline or understated background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form-control in the vertical flow; it helps determine subsequent content (e.g., showing the order summary widget) but itself does not move or resize dynamically beyond standard responsive behavior within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support request form defines the main content block of the page and sits at the heart of the central column. Its top edge begins just below the support-topic selector (or, if better structured, the form itself encompasses the title, intro text, topic selector, and fields as a single unit, with components visually arranged as described). At the top of this form block, a bold, left-aligned title like ‘Contact Support’ appears, spanning the column width but using only one or two lines. Immediately below it, a short line or two of instructional text describes what users can do (e.g., ‘Tell us how we can help.’), using smaller type and lighter color. Underneath, the form fields stack vertically in a linear order: name, email, optional order reference, message textarea, and any other necessary fields. Each field follows the same pattern as the login mockup: label at the top of the field group, left-aligned, then the input spanning nearly the full width of the form with comfortable padding and a medium fixed height (larger for the message textarea). Vertical spacing between labels and inputs is small (8–12px), while spacing between field groups is moderate (16–24px). At the bottom of the form fields, a single primary submit button in brand blue stretches almost the full width of the column (about 90–100%), with slightly rounded corners and generous vertical padding, making it the main visual anchor. A modest gap (16–20px) sits below the button before any confirmation panel or low-priority link, maintaining the clean, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static central block that forms the vertical core of the page; responsive in width within the fixed max-width column but does not float or stick. Its height grows with content, pushing lower elements downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support confirmation panel appears within the central column, immediately below the primary submit button of the support-request form. When visible, it spans the full width of the column (matching the width of the button and inputs) and has a low-profile card or alert style with subtle background tint (green/neutral for success, perhaps red/neutral for errors) and soft rounded corners. The text inside is concise and left-aligned, with 1–3 short lines. The panel has a modest height, similar to a single form field plus padding, and is separated from the submit button above and the help navigation link below by small, consistent vertical gaps (about 12–16px), maintaining the orderly stacked rhythm. It should not visually overpower the submit button or the main form; instead, it reads as a clear but calm status indicator in the same clean, corporate aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static notification within the main column that appears or disappears based on form submission state; it does not overlay content and simply shifts neighboring elements up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help navigation link is located at the very bottom of the central column, mirroring the placement of the ‘Don’t have an account? Sign Up’ line from the login mockup. It appears as a short line of small, low-emphasis text with an inline, higher-contrast link (e.g., ‘Back to Help Center’). This text block is centered horizontally within the column or lightly left-aligned while still occupying the full column width logically, and it uses noticeably smaller font size than the form labels and button text to emphasize its tertiary role. Vertically, it sits just below the support confirmation panel (or, if the panel is not visible, directly below the primary button) with a small to medium gap (about 16–20px), and it is followed by generous bottom padding between it and the bottom of the viewport to preserve the spacious, airy feel. It does not resemble a button; instead, it feels like a subtle, textual navigation affordance at the end of the task flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline text link at the bottom of the main column; it scrolls with the content and has no special sticky or floating behavior.

name: Help / FAQ
title: Help & FAQ
description: Page for customers to browse help topics, read detailed FAQ articles, and access account/order context, using URL parameters and self-service flows.

core_features: Load and display a list of help topics grouped by category using getHelpTopics.
Open and display a specific help article based on the articleSlug URL parameter using getHelpArticle.
Allow users to switch between help articles by clicking topics, updating the articleSlug URL parameter and reloading article content.
Provide an inline account panel where users can log in with loginWithEmailPassword, sign up with signupAccount, and log out with logoutCurrentAccount while remaining on the Help page.
Show a compact current-order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary so users can reference their active order while reading help content.
Offer a client-side search/filter of loaded help topics (no extra API calls) based on title, slug, and category from getHelpTopics.
Provide a "Contact Support" action from within an article that navigates to contact.html with a topic URL parameter derived from the current article's slug or category.
user_workflows:
Browse topics and read an article:
1) User opens help.html without URL parameters.
2) Page controller calls getHelpTopics and getCurrentOrderSummary.
3) Topics list is rendered from getHelpTopics results.
4) User clicks a topic; the page updates the URL to help.html?articleSlug={selectedSlug}.
5) The controller detects the updated articleSlug, calls getHelpArticle(articleSlug), and displays the full article.
6) User can click other topics, repeating steps 4–5.

Open a specific article via shared link:
1) User opens help.html?articleSlug={someSlug} from an external link or internal navigation.
2) Page controller parses articleSlug and calls getHelpTopics and getHelpArticle(articleSlug) in parallel, and also calls getCurrentOrderSummary.
3) The article viewer displays the returned article; the topics list highlights the topic whose slug matches articleSlug.
4) If the user clicks another topic, the page updates articleSlug in the URL and refreshes article content via getHelpArticle.

Use account actions on the Help page:
1) User expands the account panel on help.html.
2) For existing accounts, user submits email and password in the sign-in form.
3) The page calls loginWithEmailPassword(email, password).
4) On success, page updates local account state with returned account info and reflects logged-in status in the panel (and may show name/email from the response).
5) User can click "Log out" in the panel, which calls logoutCurrentAccount and clears local account state.
6) For new users, instead of signing in, user fills in the sign-up form and submits.
7) The page calls signupAccount(fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, acceptTerms) and, on success, stores returned accountDashboard context and marks the user as signed in.

Reference current order while reading help:
1) On initial load of help.html (with or without articleSlug), the controller calls getCurrentOrderSummary.
2) If getCurrentOrderSummary.hasActiveOrder is true, the order summary widget displays data such as itemCount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime.
3) User reading help content can glance at this widget to understand their active order context.
4) If the user clicks a manual refresh control in the widget, the widget calls getCurrentOrderSummary again and updates its view.

Contact support from an article:
1) User opens an article (either via direct URL articleSlug or by clicking a topic).
2) Article viewer displays the article content from getHelpArticle and determines a support topic key based on either the article's slug or by looking up the matching topic's category from the getHelpTopics results.
3) User clicks a "Contact Support" link or button inside the article viewer.
4) The page navigates to contact.html?topic={derivedTopicKey}, where derivedTopicKey is computed from current article context.
