interactions: Topic click: When a user clicks on a topic in the topic list component, onTopicClick is triggered, updating the browser location to help.html?articleSlug={topic.slug}. The URL change causes the controller to call getHelpArticle with the new articleSlug.

Article deep-link handling: On page load or when the URL changes (onPageLoad/onUrlChange), the controller reads articleSlug from the query string. If present, it calls getHelpArticle(articleSlug) and sets the current article state; if absent, no article is initially selected.

Help topic search: When a user types into the search field of the search/filter form, onSearchChange updates a search query state. The topic list component filters the already-fetched topics from getHelpTopics in-memory based on this query (no additional API calls).

Help topic category filter: When a user changes the category filter control, onCategoryFilterChange updates the selected category in state. The topic list shows only topics whose category matches the selected one or all topics if no category is selected.

Login form submission: When a user submits the login form in the account panel, onLoginSubmit validates required fields client-side and calls loginWithEmailPassword with the entered email and password. Based on the response, it updates account state and displays the message returned by the interface.

Signup form submission: When a user submits the signup form, onSignupSubmit calls signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms taken from the form. On success, accountDashboard data is stored in state and the UI reflects that the user is signed in; any message from the response is shown.
Logout action: When a user clicks the logout control, onLogoutClick calls logoutCurrentAccount. On success, local account-related state is cleared and the help page remains visible.

Contact support link: Inside the article viewer, when a user clicks the Contact Support link/button, onContactSupportClick derives a topic parameter from the current article (either its slug or matched topic category) and navigates to contact.html?topic={derivedTopic}.
Order summary refresh: If the user clicks a refresh control in the order summary widget, onOrderSummaryRefreshClick calls getCurrentOrderSummary again and updates the widget with the returned values.

In-article navigation: If an article body contains links to other help articles represented as slug-based links, clicking these links triggers onArticleLinkClick, which updates the URL to help.html?articleSlug={linkedSlug} and thus reloads the article viewer via getHelpArticle.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary routing state for this page is controlled by the articleSlug URL query parameter. On every page load and on URL change, the controller reads articleSlug from the query string. If articleSlug is defined, the page treats that slug as the selected article and calls getHelpArticle(articleSlug). If articleSlug is not defined, the page loads only the topic list (via getHelpTopics) and no article is initially displayed. User interactions that select a new help topic or an in-article link always update the URL to include articleSlug={selectedSlug}, making the URL the single source of truth for which article is open. No help-related state is persisted in localStorage or cookies; all client-side state (loaded topics, current article, account session information returned by login/signup, and current order summary) exists only in memory and is reloaded as needed. Navigation away from the Help page, such as to contact.html, passes state only through query parameters (e.g., topic={derivedTopicKey}) derived from current in-memory state, not by shared storage.

components.functionality: Initializes and coordinates the Help & FAQ page by reading the articleSlug URL parameter, calling getHelpTopics, conditionally calling getHelpArticle, and always calling getCurrentOrderSummary. It manages in-memory state for topics, the current article, account session info (based on login/signup responses), and current order summary.

Provides controls to filter the list of help topics (search text and optional category filter). It does not call any interfaces; instead, it updates search and filter state used to derive a filtered topics list from the data loaded by getHelpTopics.

Displays the list of help topics returned by getHelpTopics, optionally grouped or filtered by category and search criteria. It highlights the currently selected topic based on the articleSlug URL parameter and allows users to select a topic to open its article.

Shows the full content of the currently selected help article, using data from getHelpArticle based on the articleSlug URL parameter. It also looks up the related topic metadata (such as category) from the topics list for purposes like deriving a support topic key and indicating the article's grouping.

Encapsulates the logic for building the navigation URL to the contact page from within an article. It derives a topic parameter from either the current article's slug or its topic category (looked up from getHelpTopics) and navigates the browser to contact.html?topic={derivedTopic}.

Provides inline authentication on the Help page. It includes a login form that calls loginWithEmailPassword, a signup form that calls signupAccount, and a logout control that calls logoutCurrentAccount. It stores the returned account or accountDashboard context in memory to show that the user is signed in, including basic details such as name, email, and loyalty summary when available.

Displays a compact view of the current pickup order using getCurrentOrderSummary, including whether there is an active order, item count, monetary totals, pickup store, and pickup date/time. It can be refreshed manually by the user without leaving the Help page.

Shows a minimal breadcrumb or contextual navigation based on the current article and its topic category, helping users understand where they are in the help hierarchy and quickly return to the topic list by clearing articleSlug from the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same centered, single-column pattern as the login mockup. A thin global header spans full width at the top. Below that, a narrow central column (roughly 420–480px wide, aligned to the same margins as the header) holds all Help/FAQ content in a strict top‑to‑bottom order: title/intro (handled by the page controller), then the search/filter form, then the topic list, then (when relevant) the article breadcrumbs and article viewer. Under the article, a compact 'Contact Support' link appears, followed by a subtle account/auth panel and finally an order summary widget. There are no sidebars or parallel columns; every component stacks vertically in one linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same centered, single-column pattern as the login mockup. A thin global header spans full width at the top. Below that, a narrow central column (roughly 420–480px wide, aligned to the same margins as the header) holds all Help/FAQ content in a strict top‑to‑bottom order: title/intro (handled by the page controller), then the search/filter form, then the topic list, then (when relevant) the article breadcrumbs and article viewer. Under the article, a compact 'Contact Support' link appears, followed by a subtle account/auth panel and finally an order summary widget. There are no sidebars or parallel columns; every component stacks vertically in one linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a shallow band at the top (~10–15% of viewport height). The central Help column lives in the middle band with generous top and bottom padding so that the search form and first topics are visually central on typical screens. Each major component (search form, topic list, article viewer) uses nearly the full width of the central column with comfortable internal padding. Supporting items (contact support link, account auth panel, order summary) are more compact in height with tighter spacing, visually indicating their lower priority. Ample white space separates the main sections to keep the page feeling airy and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.content_density: The design maintains a low-density, minimalist feel: few elements on screen at once, lots of breathing room, and clear separation between groups. The search form is simple and prominent, the topic list is legible with generous line spacing, and the article viewer reads like a clean document card with ample padding. Supplemental elements (contact link, auth panel, order widget) are visually light and use smaller typography and reduced vertical spacing, avoiding visual noise. No extra promo blocks or decorative graphics are introduced.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows vertically. First, across the top header (logo/back on left, site-level controls centered/right), then directly down to the page title and brief intro text in the central column. From there, attention is drawn to the prominent search/filter form (primary interaction), then down to the topic list. Selecting a topic leads the eye to breadcrumbs and the article viewer just below. After reading the article, the user’s gaze naturally drops to the 'Contact Support' link, then to the account/auth panel and lastly to the order summary widget. Like the large blue 'Sign In' button in the mockup, the search field and its primary action (e.g., a blue search button) serve as the main visual anchor.

layout.overall_description: The Help / FAQ page presents as a clean, modern, single-column layout centered on a white canvas. A slim header bar runs across the top with the brand logo and navigation aligned to the same left/right margins as the content below. Beneath this, in the middle of the screen, a narrow central column (around 420–480px wide) holds all help content, vertically stacked with generous white space above and below. At the top of this column, a concise 'Help & FAQ' title and one-line description introduce the page. Immediately under that, a prominent search/filter form spans most of the column width and becomes the primary focal point. Below the search, a vertically stacked list of topics fills the column, each row left-aligned and easy to scan. When a topic is chosen, slim breadcrumbs appear, followed by a padded article viewer card that occupies the same column width. After the article, a small 'Contact Support' text link appears, then a subtle account/auth panel and an order summary widget, all aligned to the same central axis. The entire page feels airy, linear, and purposeful, echoing the login mockup’s simple, corporate aesthetic while adapting it to a help and self-service context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the structural container for the Help / FAQ page. It sits directly below the global header bar and defines the central column where all other help components live. The controller creates a vertically centered main content band: on a typical laptop screen, the top of the main help content starts roughly 15–20% down from the top of the viewport, leaving a comfortable white space buffer above. Horizontally, it constrains content to a fixed max-width of about 420–480px and centers this column on the page, aligning its left and right edges with the header’s content margins. All subsequent components—search form, topic list, article viewer, links, panels, and widgets—are stacked inside this column in a strict top-to-bottom order, with consistent vertical spacing (roughly 24–32px between major sections and 12–16px between tightly related items).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a centered, fixed‑max‑width column that scales responsively but remains single-column; participates in normal document flow and scrolls with the page. It also enforces consistent padding around the central content band (e.g., 40–64px top and bottom within the viewport).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search/filter form is positioned near the top of the central column, immediately below the page title and introductory text managed by the controller. It spans almost the entire width of the content column (around 90–100% of the column width), with subtle internal padding so that the input field(s) and search button sit comfortably away from the edges. Vertically, there is a generous gap above it (about 24–32px from the title/intro to the top of the form) to emphasize its importance as the primary interaction, and a similar gap below before the topic list begins. The main search input is left-aligned within the column and may be accompanied by a right-aligned or inline blue primary button, echoing the bold button treatment from the login mockup. The overall block appears as a single, clean element that anchors the user’s focus before they scroll further.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the central column; stretches to the column’s width constraints and moves down as content above it grows. On small screens it remains centered and full-width within its column, without becoming sticky; the page scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic list sits directly beneath the search/filter form, separated by a comfortable vertical gap (roughly 24px). It occupies the full width of the central column, with list items stacked vertically, left-aligned, and each taking up nearly the entire row width. Each topic row has ample vertical padding (around 8–12px) and line spacing for readability, but the overall block feels light and not too tall. There is clear separation between the list and components above and below: a slightly larger gap above from the search form, and a similar gap below to the breadcrumbs or article viewer. When the list is long, it extends downward and the page scrolls; there is no separate scrolling region, preserving the simple linear page feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document stream within the central column, expanding vertically as topics are added. It does not have its own fixed height or scroll container; instead, the browser window scrolls over the entire column. On all viewports, it remains a single column of topics with no multi-column wrapping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs appear only when a specific help article is being viewed. They are positioned immediately above the article viewer, aligned to the left edge of the central column. There is a modest vertical gap (about 16–20px) between the bottom of the topic list and the top of the breadcrumbs, visually grouping the breadcrumbs with the upcoming article rather than with the list. The breadcrumb text runs single-line wherever possible, using a slightly smaller font size and lighter weight than body text, so it reads as subtle navigation context. It occupies the full column width but uses minimal height, acting as a thin band of navigation just before the main article content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the column’s normal flow, appearing and disappearing based on article selection. It scrolls with the page and is not sticky; when the user scrolls down through a long article, the breadcrumbs move off-screen like any other header above the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article viewer sits directly below the breadcrumbs when an article is selected, or directly below the topic list when breadcrumbs are not shown. It occupies the full width of the central column, presented as a clean content card with subtle rounding and a thin border or shadow similar to the login form style. Inside the card, there is generous padding on all sides (around 24px) so the text does not touch the edges, creating a relaxed reading experience. The article title is left-aligned at the top of this card, with body text flowing vertically below. The height of the article viewer is fully content-driven, expanding downwards as needed and pushing subsequent elements lower on the page. A clear separation is maintained: 16–24px above to breadcrumbs or topics, and 24px below to the contact support link, echoing how the login button is clearly separated from text below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard block element in the vertical flow, stretching to the column width and growing in height based on article length. It scrolls with the page; for long articles, the user simply scrolls down the single column, without any internal scrollbars within the viewer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Contact Support' link is positioned immediately beneath the article viewer, analogous to the 'Forgot Password?' link placement under the login button in the mockup. It sits left-aligned at the same column edge as the article text, separated from the article viewer by a relatively small vertical gap (about 12–16px) to show that it is related but secondary. The link itself is concise, using the brand blue color, and occupies minimal vertical space—essentially a single line of text. Additional supporting text (if any) remains on the same line or wraps naturally, but the block overall remains compact, reinforcing its secondary importance. There is slightly more spacing (about 20–24px) below this link before the next block (account/auth panel), marking a clear shift from article-specific assistance to account-related context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block in the normal vertical flow. It does not fix or float; it simply follows the article viewer and moves up or down based on the article’s height. On all devices, it remains aligned to the left within the central column and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account auth panel appears below the contact support link as a compact, supportive section prompting users to sign in or confirming their signed-in state for account-specific help. It spans the full width of the central column but is visually lighter and shorter than the article viewer. The panel is separated from the contact support link by a medium vertical gap (around 24px) to indicate a separate functional block. It can be rendered as a low-profile card with a subtle background tint or border, small iconography if needed, and concise copy aligned left. Internal padding (about 16–20px) keeps the content airy while maintaining a slim overall height, so it feels like a secondary utility rather than the primary task. Below the panel, another 20–24px gap separates it from the order summary widget.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky block element within the central column. It appears or changes state depending on authentication, but its spatial position (after contact support, before order summary) remains constant. It grows only as needed for its brief content; no internal scrolling is introduced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary widget is positioned near the bottom of the central column, directly beneath the account auth panel and separated by a consistent vertical gap (around 24px). It shares the same width and alignment as the other components, occupying the full available width of the central column. Visually, it reads as a low-density, contextual card: a short header (e.g., 'Recent Order') and a few key details, all left-aligned, with comfortable padding (16–20px) and minimal decoration. Its height is moderate—enough to show one to three orders or a concise summary—without dominating the page. If there are no orders, the widget either collapses to a minimal placeholder state or hides entirely, leaving extra white space at the bottom. The page ends with a generous buffer of whitespace below this widget to mirror the top padding and keep the composition balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows at the end of the main content stack within the central column. It is non-sticky and scrolls normally into and out of view. Its height is content-driven with no separate scroll region; if more detail is needed, links within the widget lead to other pages rather than expanding indefinitely here.

name: Terms of Use
title: Terms of Use
description: Page for viewing the website and loyalty program terms of use, with optional account login, signup, and current order summary.

core_features: Load and display the latest Terms of Use content using getTermsOfUseContent, including the last updated timestamp and full legal text.
Provide in-page navigation to related legal documents by linking to privacy.html from within the terms content.
Allow a user to log in with email and password from this page using loginWithEmailPassword, updating the page state with basic account and loyalty context.
Allow a logged-in user to log out from this page using logoutCurrentAccount, clearing any account-specific state in the page.
Provide an inline account creation (signup) flow from the Terms of Use page using signupAccount, ensuring that acceptance of terms is passed via the acceptTerms parameter.
Display a compact current-order summary widget using getCurrentOrderSummary so users can reference their active pickup order while reviewing terms.
user_workflows: Reading the Terms of Use and opening Privacy Policy: 1) User navigates to terms.html (e.g., via footer link). 2) On initial load, the page calls getTermsOfUseContent with no parameters. 3) The terms content component renders the returned content and lastUpdated fields. 4) If the user clicks any 'Privacy Policy' link embedded in the terms content, the browser navigates to privacy.html (optionally preserving or appending URL parameters as needed by the wider application).

Deep-linking to a specific terms section via URL parameters: 1) User opens terms.html with an optional section parameter in the URL (e.g., terms.html?section=loyalty). 2) On load, the page reads the section parameter. 3) After getTermsOfUseContent returns, the terms content component attempts to scroll to or highlight the subsection whose identifier matches the section parameter (if present in the content). 4) If the URL section parameter changes (for example via in-page navigation links that update the URL), the component re-applies the scroll/highlight behavior without reloading the terms content.

Logging in while on the Terms of Use page: 1) User arrives on terms.html without being authenticated in the page context. 2) The account/auth component is shown in a logged-out state, exposing email and password inputs and a login action. 3) User fills in email and password and submits the login form. 4) The component calls loginWithEmailPassword with the entered email and password. 5) If the response indicates success and isLoggedIn is true, the component updates its state with the returned account information (fullName, email, loyaltyPointsBalance, loyaltyTier, favoriteStore) and switches into a logged-in view. 6) The header or account strip reflects the logged-in state, and other components (e.g., order summary) can use that context as needed. 7) If the response indicates failure, the component displays the returned message as an error and allows retry.

Logging out from the Terms of Use page: 1) While on terms.html, the user is in a logged-in state as established by an earlier successful login. 2) The account/auth component displays a logout control. 3) User clicks the logout control. 4) The component calls logoutCurrentAccount with no parameters. 5) If the response indicates success, the component clears its local account state, reverts to a logged-out view, and optionally collapses sections that depend on account data (e.g., loyalty info). 6) If the response indicates failure, the component displays the returned message but keeps the current view so the user can retry.

Signing up from the Terms of Use page: 1) User navigates to terms.html with an intention to create an account (optionally using a URL parameter like mode=signup to auto-expand the signup form). 2) The inline signup component exposes fields for fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and a required control for indicating acceptance of the Terms of Use. 3) When the user submits the signup form, the component calls signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms values derived from the form. 4) If the response indicates success, the component receives accountDashboard data and can update the page’s account context (e.g., name, email, loyalty points, loyalty tier, favorite store) and reflect that the user is now signed in. 5) If the response indicates failure, the component displays the message returned and keeps the form values for correction. 6) If a redirectBack URL parameter is present (e.g., pointing to another page), the application can use that after successful signup to redirect the user while preserving that they have accepted the terms.

Viewing the current order summary while reading terms: 1) User navigates to terms.html at any time during a shopping session. 2) On load (and on relevant events like page visibility changes, if desired), the order summary widget calls getCurrentOrderSummary with no parameters. 3) If the response has hasActiveOrder set to true, the widget displays orderId, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime in a compact layout. 4) If hasActiveOrder is false, the widget displays a neutral state indicating that there is no active order. 5) If the user clicks on the widget (if configured to be clickable), the application can navigate to an order details or checkout page using URL parameters such as order.html?id={orderId}, without altering the terms content.
interactions: On page load (DOMContentLoaded or equivalent), trigger a call to getTermsOfUseContent; render the returned content and lastUpdated fields in the terms viewer component.

On URL parameter change (specifically section, mode, or redirectBack), update component states: the terms viewer scrolls or focuses according to section; the auth/signup components expand or collapse according to mode; and redirectBack is stored in memory for use after login or signup.
On click of an internal navigation link within the terms content that points to a section anchor, update the URL section parameter to match the target anchor identifier and scroll to that section.

On click of any 'Privacy Policy' link inside the terms content, navigate to privacy.html, optionally appending any application-level context parameters (e.g., preserving redirectBack if needed by the wider app).

On submit of the login form in the account/auth component, call loginWithEmailPassword with the entered email and password, then: if the call returns success and isLoggedIn is true, store the returned account object in component state and update the UI; if not, show the response message as an inline error.

On click of the logout button in the account/auth component, call logoutCurrentAccount and, on success, clear any account-related state within the page components (account strip and any dependent views).

On submit of the signup form in the inline signup component, call signupAccount with fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms; if the call succeeds, update the page’s account context based on accountDashboard and optionally trigger a redirect using any redirectBack URL parameter; if it fails, show the returned message and keep form inputs intact.

On load of the order summary widget, call getCurrentOrderSummary; bind the returned hasActiveOrder, itemCount, subtotalAmount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime to display fields; update or refresh this data if the page re-enters focus or if a manual refresh control is provided.

On click of the order summary widget (if configured as navigable), navigate to an order-related page using URL parameters derived from the returned orderId, without interfering with the Terms of Use content.
On user-initiated 'refresh terms' action (if provided), call getTermsOfUseContent again and re-render the content, ensuring the current section URL parameter is still honored.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Terms of Use page relies on URL parameters to control behavior and state without using localStorage. The primary URL is terms.html. Optional URL parameters include: section: An identifier for a subsection within the terms content (for example corresponding to anchors or headings in the dynamic terms text). On load, after getTermsOfUseContent completes, the terms viewer component checks the section parameter; if present and a matching subsection exists, it scrolls to or highlights that subsection. If the section parameter changes (e.g., via in-page links that update the URL with history.replaceState or similar), the viewer re-applies scrolling without re-fetching content. mode: Controls which account-related panel is shown by default. For example, if mode is set to a value representing 'login', the account/auth component initializes in login-focused state; if mode represents 'signup', the inline signup component initializes expanded, and the login form may be collapsed or secondary. The components react to changes in mode without reloading the page. redirectBack: Holds a URL (or path) to which the user should be returned after a successful login or signup initiated from this page. After loginWithEmailPassword or signupAccount returns success, the application can use redirectBack to perform navigation if this behavior is configured at a higher level; the Terms page itself simply exposes and passes this value through its auth components. Other parameters: Additional parameters (such as readOnly or source) can be interpreted by the page to toggle UI affordances (e.g., disabling login/signup on an embedded legal view), but they do not alter core data interactions. All account and order data displayed on this page are derived from the assigned interfaces (loginWithEmailPassword, logoutCurrentAccount, signupAccount, getTermsOfUseContent, getCurrentOrderSummary) and are not persisted in URL parameters; URL parameters are used only for navigation and view-state control.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays the Terms of Use text and last updated date using getTermsOfUseContent, supports deep-linking to sections via a URL section parameter, and provides in-text links to privacy.html for the Privacy Policy.

Displays login controls when no account context is present and a compact account summary when logged in; calls loginWithEmailPassword on login attempts and logoutCurrentAccount on logout, updating the page’s account state accordingly.

Provides an inline account creation form tied to the Terms of Use, ensuring acceptTerms is captured and passed to signupAccount; on success it updates the account context with accountDashboard and can trigger navigation based on redirectBack.

Displays a compact view of the current pickup order by calling getCurrentOrderSummary, showing whether an active order exists and, if so, a mini-summary of items, amounts, and pickup details; can optionally link to a dedicated order page using the returned orderId.

Provides structured navigation between legal documents, including a link back to the Terms of Use (terms.html) and a link to the Privacy Policy (privacy.html), while preserving or appending relevant URL parameters such as redirectBack when needed.

Centralizes reading and reacting to URL parameters such as section, mode, and redirectBack, notifying other components when these parameters change so they can update their behavior without using localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered vertical column as the main content rail, constrained to roughly 420–480px max-width and aligned with the header margins. Place elements in a strict top-to-bottom sequence: global header (outside this spec), then a Terms of Use title block, then the terms-content-viewer as the primary block. Directly beneath the terms, stack the account-auth-strip (login), then the inline-signup-panel, then the current-order-summary-widget, and finally the legal-navigation-footer. No sidebars or multi-column layouts are introduced; all components are full-width within the central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered vertical column as the main content rail, constrained to roughly 420–480px max-width and aligned with the header margins. Place elements in a strict top-to-bottom sequence: global header (outside this spec), then a Terms of Use title block, then the terms-content-viewer as the primary block. Directly beneath the terms, stack the account-auth-strip (login), then the inline-signup-panel, then the current-order-summary-widget, and finally the legal-navigation-footer. No sidebars or multi-column layouts are introduced; all components are full-width within the central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top header band remains shallow (~10–15% of viewport height). The central column, containing the Terms of Use and related actions, occupies the visual middle of the page with generous top and bottom padding. The terms-content-viewer consumes the largest vertical space, with the account-auth-strip and inline-signup-panel forming a compact band beneath it. The current-order-summary-widget and legal-navigation-footer occupy smaller vertical slices near the bottom, clearly secondary in space and emphasis. All components share consistent left/right padding and align to the same column edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, spacious feel: generous white space around the central column, comfortable vertical spacing between components (roughly 24–32px gaps between major blocks, 12–20px between internal elements like labels and inputs). The terms-content-viewer uses readable line length without crowding, while login and signup forms preserve the airy label–field–button stacking style from the mockup. Ancillary widgets (order summary, footer links) are compact but not cramped, with smaller type and reduced spacing to signal lower priority.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the top header brand area, then drops to the centered 'Terms of Use' title block at the top of the main column. From there, the flow moves directly down into the terms-content-viewer, which is the primary reading surface. As the user reaches the end of the terms, attention naturally continues downward to the account-auth-strip (login), then to the inline-signup-panel, then to the current-order-summary-widget, and finally to the thin legal-navigation-footer. Strongest visual anchors are the page title and any primary blue buttons in the auth components, but these are placed after the terms so they do not compete with the primary reading task.

layout.overall_description: The page is a clean, full-width canvas with a light, corporate feel and a single, centered content column. A slim global header sits at the very top, spanning edge to edge with the logo and navigation aligned to the same left and right margins as the main content. Below it, the main column appears as a narrow vertical strip centered in the viewport, about 420–480px wide, floating in generous white space on both sides. At the top of this column, a left-aligned 'Terms of Use' heading and a brief explanatory line establish the page purpose. Directly beneath, the terms-content-viewer stretches the full width of the column and extends downward as a scannable, scrollable block of text; this is the main mass of the page and visually dominates the central band of the viewport. Once the user scrolls past the terms, a neatly separated band of account-related blocks appears: first a stacked login strip, then an inline signup panel, both following the same vertical label–input–button pattern from the login mockup and spanning the full column width. Below these, a small order summary card sits as a tertiary widget, aligned with the same column edges but visually lighter. Finally, a thin, understated legal-navigation-footer caps the column at the bottom, with compact text links and subtle spacing that echo the small footer line from the login design. Throughout, the layout maintains a strict top-to-bottom flow, consistent left alignment inside the column, and plenty of breathing room, with a single brand blue used sparingly for primary actions and links.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-content-viewer sits near the top of the central column, directly below the 'Terms of Use' title block and its short description, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full width of the constrained column (roughly 420–480px on desktop, 100% of available width on smaller screens), with consistent left and right padding matching the form fields on the login page. The top edge of this block aligns visually with the column edges, and its content flows downward in a single vertical reading strip, using the browser’s natural page scroll rather than an internal scroll container. Vertically, it occupies the largest portion of the page—typically more than half of the central column’s scrollable height—forming a solid, text-dense but visually uncluttered panel. There is generous white space above its first line of text and below its last paragraph (about 24–32px) before the account-auth-strip begins, reinforcing the separation between reading the terms and taking account-related actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow, expanding vertically as needed with content; it does not stick or float, and the page scrolls normally through the entire terms text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-auth-strip (login-form) is positioned directly below the terms-content-viewer, separated from the end of the terms by a clear vertical gap of roughly 32px and optionally a subtle dividing line or very light top border to signal a new functional section. It occupies the full width of the same central column, with its left edge aligned precisely with the left margin of the terms text and heading. Inside this strip, the login fields are stacked vertically in a compact arrangement that mirrors the login mockup: a small section title (e.g., 'Sign in to your account') aligned left, followed by the email label and input, then the password label and input, and finally a full-width blue primary CTA button anchored beneath them. The inputs and button maintain consistent horizontal padding and rounded corners, matching the mockup’s form styling. Vertical spacing between label, input, and button remains generous but slightly tighter than the main page spacing (around 12–20px), preserving clarity without overpowering the terms section. A small auxiliary link like 'Forgot Password?' sits just below the button, left-aligned, with minimal top margin (8–12px), keeping the overall strip shallow and clearly secondary to the terms.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow, expanding slightly in height on smaller screens but always occupying a single-column band. It does not fix to the viewport; it is revealed only after scrolling past the terms-content-viewer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline-signup-panel (signup-form) is stacked immediately below the account-auth-strip, with a modest vertical separation of about 24px and a light visual divider (such as a thin horizontal rule or a simple section label) to differentiate it from the login form. It aligns perfectly with the same central column edges, using the full available width. The internal layout mirrors the login pattern: labels and inputs stacked vertically, with the primary signup button stretching the full width of the column. The heading or lead-in text for this panel is slightly smaller and lighter in weight than the main page heading, reinforcing its lower priority compared to the terms but still clearly legible. Vertical spacing between labels, inputs, and the button is similar to the login strip (12–20px), maintaining the airy, low-density feel. Any helper text (e.g., “By signing up, you agree to the Terms of Use”) is placed in a smaller font below the button with tight top spacing (8–12px), visually binding it to the action without adding clutter or height. Overall, the panel forms a compact, clearly grouped block that reads as a follow-up option after the login strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the same central flow. It grows vertically with form fields but remains within the single-column pattern, without floating or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current-order-summary-widget appears below the inline-signup-panel, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px so it reads as a distinct, tertiary section. It uses the same central column width and alignment, but the component itself is visually lighter and more compact than the forms: a small card or framed block with a subtle border or faint background tint on a white page. The widget’s header and key order details are stacked vertically, with short lines of text and perhaps a small total amount or status, all left-aligned. The vertical height of this widget is intentionally modest—roughly one-quarter or less of the viewport height on typical screens—so it does not compete with the terms or auth sections. Any buttons or links inside the widget are smaller and less visually dominant (e.g., text links or lighter-outline buttons), staying within the minimalist style and reinforcing its supporting role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, sitting as a lightweight card in the lower portion of the central column. It scrolls normally with the page and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The legal-navigation-footer sits at the very bottom of the central column, following the current-order-summary-widget with a generous top margin of about 32–40px to separate it from the content above and to echo the thin footer treatment from the login mockup. It spans the same column width but has a very shallow vertical footprint: a single line or two of small, muted text and inline links (e.g., Privacy, Cookies, Contact), horizontally arranged. On larger screens, the links are center-aligned within the column to create a balanced, understated footer strip; on narrow screens, they may wrap to a second line but remain centrally positioned. The spacing above the text is slightly larger than the spacing below, helping the footer feel anchored near the bottom of the viewport when content is short. This footer maintains the minimalist aesthetic with no heavy backgrounds—just text and links with subtle hover states in the brand blue.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer within the central content column. On tall viewports with little content, additional top padding can expand so it visually sits closer to the bottom edge of the screen, but it does not use fixed or sticky positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-controller is a non-visual, structural component that does not render any visible UI in the layout. Conceptually, it overlays the entire page or sits just beneath the main column, managing which section of the terms is in view, which auth panel is active, or which anchors are reflected in the URL. It occupies no width or height and does not affect spacing between visible components; instead, it coordinates state changes such as scrolling to specific clauses, toggling between login and signup views, or reflecting order states in the URL without altering the single-column visual structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering, zero-size component that sits logically at the page or app root layer. It does not participate in layout flow; it only responds to and updates state and URL parameters.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page for viewing the coffee shop privacy policy, with basic account and order context, including deep-link navigation to specific policy sections.

core_features: Load and display the latest privacy policy text using getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Allow deep-link navigation to specific sections of the privacy policy via URL parameters (e.g., privacy.html?section=data-rights).
Provide in-page navigation between privacy policy sections (table of contents) that updates the URL state.
Expose a login form so users can authenticate with email and password via loginWithEmailPassword while on the privacy policy page.
Provide a lightweight signup flow using signupAccount for users who decide to create an account after reviewing the policy.
Allow logged-in users to log out via logoutCurrentAccount from the header controls.
Display a compact current order summary using getCurrentOrderSummary so users can see order context while reading the policy.
Enable navigation from the privacy policy content to the Terms of Use page (terms.html) when the relevant link in the policy content is clicked.
user_workflows:
View privacy policy from footer:
1. User clicks the "Privacy Policy" link in the site footer, which navigates to privacy.html.
2. On page load, the page calls getPrivacyPolicyContent to retrieve { lastUpdated, content }.
3. In parallel, the page calls getCurrentOrderSummary to show the current order widget if applicable.
4. Once content is loaded, the policy text is rendered in the main content area.
5. If the URL contains a section parameter, the page scrolls to the corresponding section anchor within the loaded content.

Deep-link to a specific privacy topic:
1. User is redirected or navigates to privacy.html?section=<section-key> (e.g., triggered from an account or settings page).
2. The page shell parses the section URL parameter before or during initial render.
3. getPrivacyPolicyContent is called and the full content is rendered.
4. After render, the page attempts to scroll/focus to the section that matches section using anchors or internal IDs derived from the content.
5. When the user clicks another section in the in-page table of contents, the section parameter in the URL is updated and the viewport scrolls to that section without a full page reload.

Log in from the privacy policy page:
1. User opens privacy.html (with or without existing session).
2. In the header auth area, user selects the login mode (if not already active), which sets auth=login in the URL.
3. User enters email and password and submits the form.
4. The page calls loginWithEmailPassword with the entered credentials.
5. If success is true and isLoggedIn is true, the header updates to show authenticated state using the returned account fields (e.g., fullName, loyaltyPointsBalance) and exposes a logout action.
6. If login fails (success is false), an inline error message is shown in the login form; the URL parameters remain unchanged so user can retry.
7. The privacy content remains visible throughout; only header state changes.

Sign up from the privacy policy page:
1. User on privacy.html decides to create an account and switches the header auth control to signup mode, updating auth=signup in the URL.
2. The signup form is displayed with fields for fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and a required acknowledgment of terms/conditions (acceptTerms).
3. On form submission, signupAccount is called with the entered values.
4. If success is true, the page updates the header to authenticated state using accountDashboard and optionally switches auth back to a non-form mode (e.g., no auth URL parameter or auth=none).
5. If signup fails, an inline error message is displayed and the URL state (auth=signup) is preserved so the user can correct inputs without losing form mode.
6. The user can continue reading the privacy policy, now as a logged-in user.

Log out while reading the privacy policy:
1. While on privacy.html, a logged-in user clicks the logout action in the header.
2. The page calls logoutCurrentAccount.
3. If success is true, the header auth state resets to logged-out mode and may update URL auth parameter to auth=login or remove it depending on design.
4. The privacy policy content and scroll position are not affected, allowing the user to continue reading.

Review order context during privacy review:
1. When privacy.html loads, getCurrentOrderSummary is called.
2. If hasActiveOrder is true, a compact widget shows itemCount, subtotalAmount or totalAmount, and pickupStore information.
3. If the user interacts with the widget’s link or button to view more details, the page navigates to an order details page such as order.html?id=<orderId> using orderId from the summary, preserving any existing privacy URL parameters in the referrer (from=privacy).

Navigate to Terms of Use from the policy content:
1. The privacy policy content returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent contains a hyperlink to terms.html (e.g., for "Terms of Use").
2. When the user clicks the link inside the rendered content, the browser navigates to terms.html, optionally appending from=privacy and/or section-related parameters if they are present in the source URL.
3. The user can use browser back navigation to return to the same scroll position within privacy.html.
interactions:
On initial page load (no user input):
- Trigger getPrivacyPolicyContent to fetch latest policy content.
- Trigger getCurrentOrderSummary to populate the order summary widget.
- Parse URL parameters (e.g., section, auth, from) to configure initial view state (active section, visible auth form).

Section navigation within the policy:
- Click on a section item in the policy table of contents to scroll to the corresponding section and update section=<section-key> in the URL via the History API.
- Manual changes to the section parameter (e.g., via browser navigation or externally constructed URL) cause the page to adjust scroll position after content is rendered.

Login interaction:
- User types into email and password fields in the header login form (onChange events update in-memory state).
- Submitting the form (onSubmit) calls loginWithEmailPassword.
- Based on the response (success/isLoggedIn), the component updates header state, displays messages, and may clear the password field.
- Clicking a "switch to signup" control toggles auth mode and updates the auth parameter in the URL without leaving the page.

Signup interaction:
- User fills fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and accepts terms (onChange updates local form state).
- Submitting the form (onSubmit) calls signupAccount.
- Based on the response (success, error message), the component either transitions to authenticated header state or shows validation/error messages.
- Clicking a "switch to login" link switches auth mode and updates the auth URL parameter.

Logout interaction:
- Clicking the logout control (onClick) calls logoutCurrentAccount.
- If successful, the component clears any in-memory authenticated context and updates the header to logged-out mode.

Order summary widget interaction:
- On load, the widget updates itself based on getCurrentOrderSummary result.
- If hasActiveOrder is true, clicking a "View order" action (onClick) navigates to order.html?id=<orderId>, preserving a from=privacy reference in the URL.

Content link interaction:
- Clicking the Terms of Use link inside the rendered privacy content uses normal anchor navigation to terms.html (optionally including query parameters such as from=privacy).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL parameters to manage and restore state, without using localStorage:
- section: A string identifying the currently focused policy section (e.g., section=data-rights). On load, if present, the page will scroll to the matching section anchor after getPrivacyPolicyContent has rendered the markup. When users navigate between sections via the in-page table of contents, the script updates section using the History API so that browser back/forward restore section focus.
- auth: A string representing which authentication view is currently active in the header (login, signup, or other defined modes such as none). On load, this determines whether the login form, signup form, or a neutral state is visible. When the user switches between login and signup, the page updates auth in the URL and re-renders the corresponding form without reloading the page.
- from: An optional string indicating the referrer context (e.g., from=checkout, from=account). This value does not affect core functionality but can be preserved when navigating away (e.g., to terms.html or order.html) so that other pages can adjust their behavior or provide contextual navigation back to privacy.html.
Other state, such as current input values in forms or transient error messages, is held only in memory for the lifetime of the page session and is not persisted via URL or localStorage. All dynamic data related to account authentication, privacy content, and order summaries is retrieved exclusively via the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading (privacy content and current order summary), parses URL parameters, orchestrates layout for header, policy content area, and order summary widget, and maintains high-level in-memory state for the page.
Provides login and signup mode switching in the header based on the auth URL parameter, and exposes login, signup, and logout actions while displaying minimal account context when available.
Displays email and password fields when auth=login, validates required fields on submit, calls loginWithEmailPassword, and updates header authenticated state and errors accordingly.

Displays the account creation form when auth=signup, collects fullName, email, password, confirmPassword, and acceptTerms, calls signupAccount on submit, and on success updates authenticated header state based on accountDashboard.
Triggers logoutCurrentAccount when clicked and, on success, clears authenticated header state and optionally sets auth=login in the URL.

Calls getCurrentOrderSummary on page load to display a compact summary of the active order, including itemCount, totalAmount, pickupStore, and pickupDateTime when hasActiveOrder is true, and offers navigation to a detailed order page using orderId.
Invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent on page load, stores the returned lastUpdated and content in memory, and passes them to the content viewer and table of contents components once available.

Receives the content from getPrivacyPolicyContent and renders it as scrollable, accessible HTML, preserving any hyperlinks (including the Terms of Use link to terms.html) and section anchors used for deep linking via the section URL parameter.

Builds an in-page table of contents based on structural markers or headings within the privacy policy content and allows users to jump to specific sections; updates the section URL parameter when a section is selected.

Reads and writes URL parameters related to privacy page state (section, auth, from), synchronizes these with in-memory state, and triggers scroll-to-section behavior after the content viewer has rendered.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a centered content column constrained to a fixed max-width (~420–480px). Header uses a three-part horizontal layout (left, center, right) aligned to the same page margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a full-width canvas with a single, narrow, centered content column (about 420–480px wide) for all primary content, mirroring the mockup. At the very top sits a thin header bar spanning the full width with a three-part horizontal layout (brand/back on the left, page context in the center, auth controls on the right). Below the header, the main central column begins with the page title and a brief description, followed by a lightweight order summary/context block, then a vertical list-style table of contents for the privacy sections. Beneath the TOC is the main privacy content viewer, which scrolls vertically. Inline auth forms (login/signup) appear just below the header and above the page title when invoked, still constrained to the central column. Auxiliary actions like logout and order summary remain visually subordinate to the main privacy content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a full-width canvas with a single, narrow, centered content column (about 420–480px wide) for all primary content, mirroring the mockup. At the very top sits a thin header bar spanning the full width with a three-part horizontal layout (brand/back on the left, page context in the center, auth controls on the right). Below the header, the main central column begins with the page title and a brief description, followed by a lightweight order summary/context block, then a vertical list-style table of contents for the privacy sections. Beneath the TOC is the main privacy content viewer, which scrolls vertically. Inline auth forms (login/signup) appear just below the header and above the page title when invoked, still constrained to the central column. Auxiliary actions like logout and order summary remain visually subordinate to the main privacy content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar occupies a slim slice at the top (roughly 10–15% of the viewport height on initial load). The central content column occupies the vertical middle band of the viewport with generous top and bottom white space, so that the title, TOC, and initial part of the policy content feel centered and calm. Inputs and buttons inside inline forms span nearly the full width of the column, leaving only modest horizontal padding. The order summary and TOC use compact vertical spacing, while the privacy content viewer is allowed to extend far downward with consistent spacing between headings and paragraphs. Footer-like inline text (e.g., beneath forms) uses tight vertical spacing to signal lower priority.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is deliberately low density: only essential controls and text are shown at any given vertical slice. The header is thin and uncluttered. The central column has ample white space around the title, order summary, TOC, and content body, with clean separation between each block. Forms are minimal, with one field per row and no extra decoration. The TOC is simple, short text links, avoiding icons or busy styling. No additional marketing or promotional elements are present; everything on the page directly supports reading the privacy policy and managing account context.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye flows from the top header bar (brand/back left, page label center, auth state right) down into the central column where a bold 'Privacy Policy' heading anchors attention. From there the eye moves to the brief descriptive/instructional text and then to the small order context block. Next, the eye is guided to the section TOC links, which act as navigational stepping stones into the main privacy content. Scrolling continues down through a clear hierarchy of section headings and body text within the content viewer. When inline auth forms are visible, they appear directly below the header and above the title, so the user sees auth first, then the policy content, maintaining the strong top-to-bottom flow. The primary blue elements (auth buttons, key links) punctuate this vertical path but do not overpower the content.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The Privacy Policy page sits on a clean white canvas with a subtle, corporate feel and a single brand blue as the accent color. A thin, full-width header bar anchors the top, divided into three horizontal zones: on the left, the coffee shop logo and an understated 'Back' text link; in the center, a simple label like 'Privacy Policy'; on the right, compact auth controls indicating whether the user is signed in or offering a sign-in/ sign-up entry point. Below this bar, the page narrows into a centered column roughly 420–480px wide, floating in generous white space. When needed, a slim, stacked login or signup form appears immediately under the header within this column, echoing the mockup’s linear email/password/CTA flow. The main page content begins with a 'Privacy Policy' heading and a one- or two-line description, followed by a small, lightly framed order/account context snippet. Just beneath, a minimalist table of contents lists key policy sections in a clean vertical list of links. The bulk of the page is the privacy content itself: a single column of left-aligned text with clear section headings, ample line spacing, and no visual clutter. As the user scrolls, this central column remains the sole focus, with the header bar always at the top of the page and no sidebars or multi-column layouts disrupting the strong vertical reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell spans the full browser viewport, providing a white background from edge to edge. It establishes a centered layout by constraining all main content to a single column positioned in the horizontal middle of the page, with equal left and right margins. This content column has a fixed max-width of approximately 420–480px and is vertically offset from the top by generous padding so that the initial title and first lines of content appear in the middle band of the viewport. The shell ensures that the thin top header bar sits flush at the top edge, while the central column (containing inline auth forms, title, order summary, TOC, and privacy content) flows vertically beneath it in a linear stack. There are no sidebars; the rest of the viewport remains clean white space around the centered column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-viewport container that centers and constrains all other components; scrollable page context for the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The auth header controls live inside a thin horizontal bar pinned to the top of the page, spanning 100% of the width. Within this bar, content is aligned to the same central column margins as the main content: a three-part inline layout with the left segment containing the logo and 'Back' text link, the center segment showing a simple page label like 'Privacy Policy', and the right segment containing the auth state controls. In the right segment, small, text-first controls (e.g., 'Sign In', 'Sign Up', or a user name plus a compact logout button) are right-aligned, using minimal vertical height and modest horizontal padding so they do not compete with the main content below. On scroll, this header remains at the top of the document flow (not floating or sticky by default), so as the user scrolls down, it moves off-screen like a standard header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Top-of-page horizontal strip; static in the normal flow (not sticky), always occupying the full width and defining the left/right margins used by the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline login form is positioned directly beneath the top header bar, fully within the centered content column. It occupies nearly the entire column width, leaving a slim inner padding on the left and right. The form follows the mockup’s strict vertical stacking: a small 'Sign In' heading aligned to the left, then an instruction line, followed by the 'Email' label and email input on its own row, and then the 'Password' label and password input on its own row. Below the fields, a full-width primary blue 'Sign In' button spans almost the entire column width, aligning with the inputs. Under the button, a single secondary link like 'Forgot Password?' is left-aligned, using tighter vertical spacing. The overall form block is visually compact but well-spaced, with clear separation above and below so that when shown, it feels like a distinct panel between the header and the rest of the privacy content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in-place beneath the header within the central column; pushes all subsequent content (title, TOC, viewer) downward when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline signup form shares the same horizontal constraints and position behavior as the login form, also confined to the centered column beneath the header. When active, it appears either in place of or directly beneath the login form, maintaining a unified vertical stack. It begins with a left-aligned 'Create an account' heading, followed by a brief one- or two-line description. Below, the form fields are stacked one per row (e.g., Name, Email, Password), each spanning almost the full column width with consistent padding matching the login inputs. A full-width primary blue 'Sign Up' button follows the fields. At the very bottom, a short line like 'Already have an account? Sign In' appears with the 'Sign In' portion as an inline text link. Vertical spacing above and below the entire block is slightly less than the space above the main page title so that it feels like a compact component, not a separate page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive or sequential with the login form in the same central region; expands the vertical height of the top content section when visible, pushing main policy content further down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The logout button is a small, flat, brand-blue button integrated into the right side of the top header bar within the auth area. It sits on the same horizontal line as any user name or account label, aligned to the right edge of the central column. The button height matches the header’s slim vertical dimension, using minimal padding so it is clearly an action but does not dominate the header. There is a modest horizontal gap between the user label and the button. It does not appear in the central column; it is confined to the header strip for a clean, unobtrusive account control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the header-auth-controls region; static, occupying a fixed spot on the right side of the header bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary widget is placed in the central column below the page title and description but above the section TOC. It spans the full width of the content column, with a subtle, low-contrast border or background tint to differentiate it as a contextual card without heavy decoration. Inside, text and any small amounts of data (e.g., 'Current order: Latte, ready at 10:30') are left-aligned and stacked vertically with tight spacing, taking up no more than a few lines. The widget’s vertical height is modest—roughly equivalent to two or three lines of body text plus padding—so it feels like a quick contextual snippet rather than a full card. Adequate white space above and below the widget separates it from the intro text above and the TOC below, preserving the central content’s calm, linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main vertical flow; does not stick or float, simply participates as one block in the single-column stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy content loader occupies the exact footprint of the privacy-content-viewer within the central column. While content is loading, it fills the viewer area with vertically stacked, light gray skeleton lines that mimic headings and paragraphs, all left-aligned and spanning most of the column width. The loader block begins where the content viewer would normally start—directly beneath the section TOC—and extends downward for a few viewport heights, giving a sense of pending text without overwhelming the page. No separate card or container is shown beyond what the viewer would use; visually, the loader simply replaces the content text area in-place.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or replaces the privacy-content-viewer region within the central column while data is loading; does not alter the layout of surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy content viewer is the main body area, starting immediately below the section TOC and filling the central column as the user scrolls. It is a simple, left-aligned text region that spans almost the full width of the column, with comfortable line length and line spacing appropriate for reading. Section headings are larger and bold, separated from preceding content by generous top margins, reinforcing the vertical hierarchy. Paragraphs follow with consistent spacing between them, and any lists are indented slightly while still staying within the column. The viewer extends downward far beyond the initial viewport, forming the bulk of the page’s scrollable surface. There are no side adornments or extra panels—only text and necessary inline links, keeping the layout minimal and focused.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable main content region within the central column; grows vertically with content and defines the majority of the page height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section TOC is a compact block positioned in the central column directly below the order summary widget (or below the page intro if no order summary is present) and immediately above the main content viewer. It spans the full column width and consists of a vertical list of text links, each representing a major privacy policy section. Each link sits on its own line with modest vertical spacing, left-aligned to create a clear reading line. The styling is minimalist—plain text with the brand blue applied to the link text—so it reads as navigational text rather than buttons. The block is visually separated from both the content above and below by generous top and bottom margins, but its overall height remains small (roughly 1/4 of the initial viewport height or less), making it a quick index rather than a heavy panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static anchor list in the main vertical flow; clicking items scrolls the central column to corresponding sections within the privacy-content-viewer but the TOC itself does not move or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and does not render any visible UI. It operates behind the scenes, tying the current scroll position or selected TOC section to the URL (e.g., hash fragments) and responding to incoming URL states by adjusting which section is brought into view within the central column. It shares the same structural boundaries as the main column but does not occupy any physical space, add padding, or alter the spacing of visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, non-layout component that listens and updates URL state without affecting the spatial arrangement of other elements.
